title: BlueQuarry Labs — GPU Channel & Integration Discovery
preview: Introductory partner discovery between Redwood AE/SE and BlueQuarry platform team. Covered channel model, hosted vs dedicated vs private, VPC and KMS concerns, pilot scope (3 models), and next steps for benchmarking and contract materials.
text: Introductory partner discovery between Redwood AE/SE and BlueQuarry platform team. Covered channel model, hosted vs dedicated vs private, VPC and KMS concerns, pilot scope (3 models), and next steps for benchmarking and contract materials.

title: Arkhaven Systems - hosting mode and p95 readiness discovery
preview: Discovery with Arkhaven focused on p95 latency targets for streaming chat, concurrency shaping, and tradeoffs between Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private. Customer wants a pilot to prove 200ms p95 for short-form assistant and 350ms p95 for multi-turn streaming at 1.5k concurrent users. Security and VPC/KMS requirements are gating for Dedicated vs Private decisions.
text: Discovery with Arkhaven focused on p95 latency targets for streaming chat, concurrency shaping, and tradeoffs between Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private. Customer wants a pilot to prove 200ms p95 for short-form assistant and 350ms p95 for multi-turn streaming at 1.5k concurrent users. Security and VPC/KMS requirements are gating for Dedicated vs Private decisions.

title: POC scoping - loadshape, concurrency & token-sketch - Polaris Retail
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2028-10-29 15:00 UTC
Duration: 58 minutes
Location: Zoom

Attendees:
- Ethan Miller (Redwood AE)
- Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE)
- Luis Ortega (Redwood Solutions)
- Maya Lind (Redwood CSM)
- Marco Ruiz (Polaris, Head of Platform)
- Lena Park (Polaris, ML Engineer)
- Derek Shaw (Polaris, SRE)
- Amelia Cho (Polaris, Product)
- Nikhil Rao (Polaris, Security)

Summary: (auto) quick kickoff to align POC success criteria, capture workload shape (qps, concurrency, token length distribution), and agree benchmark plan + timeline for a 2-week pilot. Customer has heavy evening peaks and conversational chat use with mixed short/long responses.

Topics:
- Current infra + constraints (VPC, on-prem requests)
- QPS and concurrency expectations and burst patterns
- Input/output token distribution and caching opportunities
- Benchmark design and success metrics (p95/p99 latency, throughput, cost targets)
- Timeline and next steps

Transcript:
[00:00] Ethan Miller: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros, then Priya will walk through the benchmarking approach.
[00:18] Marco Ruiz: Marco here, I'll kick off from our side — we run the widget search and a conversational assistant inside our app, two main surfaces. High concurrency on the search path between 17:00-22:00 UTC.
[00:27] Lena Park: and hi, lena here — the chat assistant has a mean prompt of ~60 input tokens, responses vary widely, from 20 up to 450 tokens. Some multi-turns.
[00:35] Derek Shaw: Derek, SRE — we care about tail latency, and we need to keep 95th under 250ms for search, chat can be a bit higher but p99 is important.
[00:48] Ethan Miller: got it. Priya, do you want to frame how we normally characterize workload?
[00:52] Priya Natarajan: yeah sure. we usually start with three pieces: steady-state qps, burst profile (short high qps periods), and token-length histogram. then we map those to concurrency and KV/prefix cache effectiveness. for search you often get short input/output tokens so batching helps; for chat long outputs make CPU/GPU time per request higher.
[01:20] Amelia Cho: when you say batching, you mean Redwood's continuous batching in the runtime? any impact on latency SLOs?
[01:28] Priya Natarajan: correct — continuous batching groups in-flight requests to improve throughput, but we have policies so latency targets are prioritized. for sub-250ms targets we typically recommend dedicated pools or pinned instances with smaller batch windows.
[01:45] Marco Ruiz: we were thinking hosted first but worried about data residency and audit logs. we may need VPC/private for the pilot if we can't get SOC2 evidence quickly.
[01:55] Nikhil Rao: soc2 is mandatory for us, and we need SAML SSO + audit logs pushed to our SIEM. also bring-your-own-key for embeddings at rest.
[02:08] Ethan Miller: understood — we can share our SOC2 report and KMS integration doc. for pilot we can run in Redwood Dedicated inside a customer VPC via our private option; it's doable but timeline shifts a bit.
[02:22] Lena Park: quick question on token distribution: we currently see ~55% requests under 128 input tokens, 35% between 128-512, and 10% >512. responses skew longer for the >512 group, often 300-400 tokens.
[02:38] Priya Natarajan: perfect that's the kind of split we need. can you also share a sample of prompt types? we want to build a representative prompt set so the benchmark reflects mixing.
[02:50] Marco Ruiz: yes we'll export a 72-hour sample from our production telemetry.
[02:55] Ethan Miller: ok great. now on QPS: what's your steady qps and peak?
[03:01] Marco Ruiz: steady is around 45 qps across both surfaces, peaks hit 420 qps at evening spike, burst duration usually 8-12 minutes.
[03:10] Derek Shaw: concurrency wise, when bursts hit we see sustained 350 concurrent sessions; search requests are much shorter so concurrency for those is higher but instantaneous.
[03:20] Priya Natarajan: helpful — so baseline 45 qps, peak 420 qps, concurrency ~350. we'll use those numbers to size GPU pool and determine if pre-warmed instances or autoscale is best.
[03:31] Luis Ortega: note — model choice matters a lot for tokens per second. are you targeting a specific open model or would you like Redwood's curated list?
[03:40] Lena Park: we were experimenting with llama-like smaller models and a 13B for chat. cost is a concern so we might quantize.
[03:48] Priya Natarajan: we can run both a 13B float16 baseline and a quantized 8-bit variant in the pilot. Redwood Optimize will suggest quant profiles based on your token mix.
[03:59] Amelia Cho: success criteria — product wants chat p95 < 800ms, search p95 < 250ms, cost below $X per 1M tokens (we'll provide target). If you can't meet those on hosted, we'll look draft for Dedicated.
[04:12] Ethan Miller: good explicit criteria — we'll include p95/p99 latency gating, throughput per dollar, and answer quality regression thresholds.
[04:20] Marco Ruiz: how are you measuring answer quality? we have business rules — hallucination tolerance low for product data.
[04:28] Priya Natarajan: we'll run a small eval set from your prompts and score with BLEU/ROUGE-ish heuristics plus custom checks for factuality; we also support human-in-loop sampling for spot checks during the pilot.
[04:45] Derek Shaw: on networking — we need to understand egress patterns; if using private deployment we want traffic to stay internal and logs to land in our splunk.
[04:54] Ethan Miller: that's supported. for Dedicated in VPC we can set log forwarding and KMS. will note as an action item.
[05:03] [05:03] Speaker 1: (mumbles) also curious about caching — does Redwood do prefix caching?
[05:09] Priya Natarajan: yes we do prefix/KV caching. for search short prefixes are really effective — in one customer we saw token cost drop 20%
[05:17] Lena Park: do we need to change prompts for caching to be effective?
[05:21] Priya Natarajan: small prompt normalization helps; consistent system prompt and minimal per-request metadata increases cache hits. we'll provide best-practice templates.
[05:29] Marco Ruiz: OK, benchmark specifics — what will you run?
[05:33] Priya Natarajan: two phases: 1) replay of the 72h traffic at scaled QPS to validate telemetry and shape; 2) focused throughput runs to determine achievable qps and latency across model variants, including long-output stress tests for the >512 token cohort. we'll capture p50/p95/p99, tokens/sec, GPU utilization, and cost estimate.
[05:54] Ethan Miller: timeline — customer provides traffic by Nov 2, we've got Redwood internal windows week of Nov 6 to run bench. results and recommendations posted by Nov 9. pilot contract to start mid-Nov if ok?
[06:09] Marco Ruiz: mid-Nov is tight because procurement has an internal cycle, but if we have numbers by Nov 9 we can fast-track. procurement typically takes 2 weeks.
[06:18] Amelia Cho: product wants to run a 2-week pilot with an SLA-like gate at the end.
[06:24] Ethan Miller: that's standard. success gate: meet latency/throughput/cost targets and no high-severity security gaps.
[06:31] Nikhil Rao: on security gaps — for pilot we can accept SOC2 + SAML + KMS integration; anything beyond that triggers enterprise legal review.
[06:40] Priya Natarajan: noted. we'll include the compliance artifacts in the packet.
[06:45] Derek Shaw: any suggestions for reducing cost during pilot?
[06:50] Priya Natarajan: quantization + prefix caching + smart batching; also routing lower-priority traffic to smaller models or cached responses. Redwood Optimize can propose a mixed-model routing policy.
[07:03] Marco Ruiz: what about tail latency under burst? we've seen p99 spikes to 2-3s during holidays.
[07:12] Priya Natarajan: we can size for burst capacity (reserved burst nodes) and use autoscale buffers; fallback models are an option too — serve a smaller quantized model when under heavy load to preserve availability.
[07:25] Lena Park: for the response-length distribution, is it possible to limit max tokens server-side per route?
[07:31] Ethan Miller: yes you can set max_tokens per route and also implement streaming so perceived latency for long outputs improves.
[07:38] Amelia Cho: on evals, can we gate on hallucination thresholds? e.g., if factuality score drops by X we fail the gate.
[07:45] Priya Natarajan: yes we can tie regression alerts to your eval set and set a hard threshold for pilot pass/fail.
[07:52] Luis Ortega: quick note — we sometimes see transcription say 'kv cache' as 'k V cache' so if you see odd words in the transcript that's normal.
[07:58] [08:00] Speaker 2: (laughs) fair
[08:05] Marco Ruiz: procurement question — what does your standard pilot contract look like?
[08:10] Ethan Miller: it's a two-week pilot SOW with committed reserved nodes optionally, and a usage cap. we'll send a template.
[08:18] Amelia Cho: any obstacles to running the replay in a hosted environment first to get quick numbers?
[08:25] Priya Natarajan: hosted replay is fastest for initial signals but if you need audit logs and SSO we recommend Dedicated. we can do hosted initial and then repeat in Dedicated if the security team requires it.
[08:38] Nikhil Rao: prefer to avoid sending raw PII to hosted. we'd scrub or tokenise before sending sample.
[08:45] Ethan Miller: totally — scrubbed telemetry is fine; we'll provide templates for redaction.
[08:52] Lena Park: about benchmarks, for long outputs do you measure time-to-first-byte and time-to-last-byte separately?
[08:58] Priya Natarajan: yes we capture TTFB and end-to-end. streaming improves TTFB for long outputs.
[09:07] Derek Shaw: what's the recommended GPU family? we saw Nvidia H100 in your docs, but cost is high.
[09:13] Priya Natarajan: H100 for low-latency large-model production; A100 or equivalent for cost-optimized pilots. we can test both.
[09:22] Marco Ruiz: ok give us rough sizing then — what would you provision for 420 qps peak with 350 concurrency and mixed token mix?
[09:29] Priya Natarajan: rough back-of-envelope: for a 13B model (fp16) we'd estimate needing ~6-8 H100-equivalent GPUs for sustained peak with proper batching and caching; quantized 8-bit variant could reduce GPU count by 30-40% depending on accuracy tradeoffs. exact numbers after replay.
[09:50] Ethan Miller: we'll provide a sizing spreadsheet and run capacity sims once we have traffic.
[09:56] Marco Ruiz: please also include cost per 1M tokens estimates for both hosted and dedicated.
[10:02] Ethan Miller: yep.
[10:04] [10:04] Speaker 3: someone muted?
[10:06] Amelia Cho: quickly — can Redwood do per-route throttles to protect critical flows?
[10:11] Priya Natarajan: yes per-route quotas, rate limits, and automatic fallback policies.
[10:17] Derek Shaw: and tracing integration into our existing APM?
[10:20] Luis Ortega: we support open-telemetry hooks and per-request traces forwarded to your APM.
[10:27] Marco Ruiz: potential blocker: procurement needs a vendor security questionnaire answered. who handles that?
[10:32] Ethan Miller: we'll have our Security lead respond — can you send the questionnaire to vendors@redwood.ai?
[10:38] Nikhil Rao: will do.
[10:40] Priya Natarajan: action recap: we'll run a replay, throughput runs, and long-output stress tests. deliverable is a benchmark report with sizing, latency-cost tradeoffs, and recommended config (batching, caching, quant).
[10:56] Marco Ruiz: sounds good. one last question — do you support model-version pinning for rollouts?
[11:02] Priya Natarajan: yes we support version pinning and can do canary rollouts with automatic rollback if regressions detected.
[11:09] Ethan Miller: ok, let's recap next steps and owners. Marco will provide traffic by Nov 2, Priya shares harness by Nov 1, Ethan sends commercial terms by Nov 3, Nikhil does security check by Nov 5. We'll tentatively run benchmarks week of Nov 6 and deliver results Nov 9.
[11:30] Marco Ruiz: agree. thanks everyone.
[11:33] Ethan Miller: thanks all — we'll follow up in email with links: pilot SOW template, SOC2, KMS integration doc, and the benchmark checklist.

References and shared artifacts:
- Link: Pilot SOW template (sent post-meeting)
- Link: Benchmark harness (Priya will share)
- Note: Customer to upload 72h traffic CSV to shared drive

Auto-detected action items (end of transcript):
- Provide 72h traffic export: Marco Ruiz (Polaris) due 2028-11-02
- Share benchmark harness and replay scripts: Priya Natarajan (Redwood) due 2028-11-01
- Send pilot commercial terms and PO template: Ethan Miller (Redwood) due 2028-11-03
- Confirm compliance gaps / SSO requirements: Nikhil Rao (Polaris) due 2028-11-05

End of transcript.

title: 2028-07-18 Verdant AI POC traffic & benchmark scoping
preview: Discovery/POC scoping to define traffic profile, success metrics, benchmark plan and timelines for a 4-week Verdant AI pilot. Key asks: sample traffic (1 week), golden prompt set, SLA targets (95th/99th latency), budget constraints and integration notes for VPC/KMS. Agreed next steps: share benchmark template, schedule traffic replay step, Redwood to propose bench environment and run initial micro-bench within 5 business days.

title: CalyxBio - Model selection, context length, and tool-calling discovery
preview: Initial scoping with CalyxBio to understand model-fit for genomics annotation pipelines. Customer needs long-context for full-read transcripts (~100k tokens in batches), multilingual support for research collaborators, and robust tool-calling (database lookups + lineage provenance). Discussed tradeoffs between Redwood Hosted for quick iteration, Dedicated for predictable throughput, and Private (VPC/on-prem) for data governance. Agreed to run a two-week bench: sample prompts, latency/cost spreadsheet, and an on-prem pilot scope for legal review.

title: MapleBridge — Concurrency and p95 Tokenstorm Discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2028-01-17
Time: 15:00 UTC
Duration: 48 minutes
Location: Zoom
Attendees: Jordan Lee (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Amir Solano (Redwood SE), Lena Ortiz (CTO, MapleBridge), Samir Patel (Head of Platform), Maya Chen (SRE), Diego Ramos (PM)

Summary:
- Exploration of latency (p95) targets for short chat responses and sustained streaming workloads.
- Constraint: must support 200 steady concurrent streaming sessions with bursts to ~1,000 for marketing spikes.
- Evaluate tradeoffs between Redwood Hosted, Dedicated, and Private (VPC) for latency/cost/data residency.
- Agreed next steps: bench using MapleBridge prompt set; share security docs and Dedicated sizing options.

Topics covered:
- p95 targets, concurrency, stream tokens/sec, cold-starts and caching, autoscaling behavior, KMS/encryption, SSO, and cost modeling.

Transcript body:
00:00 Jordan Lee: hey everyone thanks for joining, uh I see we got, like, most of the platform folks on this call which is great, I'll kick off with a quick agenda then hand to Priya for the performance questions.
00:10 Lena Ortiz: yep thanks jordan, appreciate it, we really want to focus on the p95 numbers and how the platform behaves under those surges we see during release days.
00:18 Jordan Lee: perfect, so very briefly — Redwood supports hosted API, dedicate pools and private VPC packs, today we'll map those to your goals. Priya?
00:27 Priya Natarajan: yeah hi, quick intro — I'm Priya, I work on serving runtime and perf. Couple clarifying questions up front: what's your target p95 for short responses and what's the average token length?
00:37 Samir Patel: for our chat assistant the common case is 20–60 tokens returned, worst-case 200 tokens. For SLAs we want p95 under 350ms for the short paths (20–60 tokens) and for streaming we want steady 40–60 tokens per second per connection with 200 steady concurrent streams. Bursts can go to 1k concurrent for up to 10 minutes.
00:56 Priya Natarajan: ok got it. Just to calibrate, p95 under 350ms for a 20–60 token response is aggressive if you need strict single-digit CPU/GPU allocation with model sizes >13B on shared hosted.
01:10 Maya Chen: can you explain that a bit — why does model size or hosting mode change p95 so much?
01:16 Amir Solano: sure — there are two dimensions: scheduling/queuing latency (how long a request waits for GPU) and generation latency (how long the model takes to produce tokens). In Hosted, we often share pools and batch work which is cost-efficient but introduces queuing during spikes. Dedicated gives reserved GPUs and guaranteed concurrency, Private gives you both isolation and network/peering control but adds ops overhead.
01:36 Diego Ramos: what about KV cache and prefix caching — do you do any prefix reuse to reduce latency?
01:42 Priya Natarajan: yes — we have automatic KV/prefix caching for repeated prompts in Redwood runtime, which can cut re-generation time significantly. The cache hit latency is tiny but depends on your prompt variance. Note that streaming still spends time on first token generation.
02:00 Lena Ortiz: okay — our main worry is the burst. we run marketing promotions where we see 800–1200 concurrent opens for 8–12 minutes. We need the UX to stay sub-500ms p95 for the first message, and then streaming throughput stable.
02:16 Jordan Lee: two quick follow-ups — do you have a traffic shape and the sample prompts? and do you require data to stay in our cloud region for compliance?
02:24 Samir Patel: yes we'll share the traffic histograms and a sample of 3 prompts (short, med, long). For compliance, production data must remain in eu-west-1 and we need SOC2 evidence; private deployment preferred if dedicated is cost prohibitive.
02:40 Priya Natarajan: great. A few numbers from our side to set expectation — for a 13B model quantized and running on our Dedicated pool, we typically see p95 ~180–300ms for 40-token replies when concurrency is moderate. On Hosted, p95 moves to 220–450ms under noisy neighbors and bursty load unless we provision reserve capacity.
03:01 Lena Ortiz: hmm those Hosted numbers worry me. Is reserved capacity an option on Hosted or would we need Dedicated?
03:08 Jordan Lee: there are two options — Hosted with commit-backed reserve (we call it burst-reserve) for transient headroom, or Redwood Dedicated where you get reserved GPUs and predictable throughput/SLO. Reserve reduces latency variance but has minimum commitment.
03:22 Maya Chen: what's the cost delta roughly? ballpark per-1k-concurrent?
03:27 Amir Solano: it's highly workload dependent — but rough thumb: Dedicated with autoscale is often 2–3x the pure hosted pay-as-you-go at steady low usage, but when you factor in burst buffering and p95 guarantees, Dedicated can be cheaper for heavy-concurrency workloads. We'll send a TCO model with your traffic shape.
03:47 Diego Ramos: can you walk through a real example — say 200 steady concurrent streaming sessions at 50 tok/s, what's the GPU footprint?
03:54 Priya Natarajan: ok let me estimate live — 200 streams * 50 tok/s = 10k tok/s. For streaming the runtime multiplexes many connections on a GPU; approximate efficiency depends on model and sequence length, but for a quantized 13B model we might need ~6–8 A100-equivalent GPUs steady, with headroom for burst. For 1k burst you'd want another 6–10 GPUs cold or use reserve burst capacity which can spin up. (note: numbers are rough).
04:27 Samir Patel: that aligns with our internal calc, but we need to see measured tokens/sec on our actual prompts. Can Redwood run those?
04:33 Jordan Lee: yes — action: we'll run token/sec and p95 bench on your short/med/long prompts and share results — Priya will lead.
04:40 (brief crosstalk)
04:43 Lena Ortiz: on the private deployment front — we are regulated for customer PII in logs, so KMS control and audit logs are required. How does Redwood support that?
04:51 Amir Solano: Private gives you control plane in your VPC and you can bring your KMS/HSM for key management, plus audit log export. Dedicated in VPC mode is also available. We'll send the private deployment checklist and SOC2+ISO docs.
05:06 Maya Chen: SSO? we want SAML and SCIM for provisioning.
05:10 Jordan Lee: yes SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning supported; RBAC and token-level auditing available in Console.
05:17 Diego Ramos: any gotchas around cold starts when autoscaling during bursts — we can't degrade the user experience.
05:23 Priya Natarajan: autoscaler can pre-warm by keeping minimal set of workers and we use fast cold-start kernels; however first request heat-up can still add tens to a couple hundred ms depending on model and container cold-start. For strict sub-500ms first-request SLA at 1k burst, we recommend a warm buffer or reserve headroom.
05:44 Lena Ortiz: what about multi-region failover — we need eu-west-1 primary but backup in eu-central — does Redwood route and failover automatically?
05:50 Amir Solano: yes we can configure smart routing with automatic fallback by region; that said cross-region failover increases tail latency due to network hop, but it's possible for availability guarantees.
06:05 Samir Patel: any limitations on streaming APIs — do you support token-level function calling and tooling we use?
06:11 Priya Natarajan: streaming supports function/tool call semantics and structured outputs; there are some edge cases with very large function payloads that affect throughput but usually fine.
06:23 Jordan Lee: quick note — you mentioned privacy, one more thing: logs retention and redaction?
06:28 Amir Solano: retention configurable and we support automatic PII redaction hooks and customer-managed retention policies.
06:35 Lena Ortiz: ok thanks. Let's talk about benchmarking specifics — we want p95 reported as end-to-end (client to final token) not just model time.
06:43 Priya Natarajan: understood. We'll run end-to-end bench from your EU region agents. We sometimes see network variance — we'll run multiple runs and report median and p95.
06:53 Diego Ramos: what's the measurement cadence — do you sample every second or what?
06:56 Priya Natarajan: we'll sample per-request timings with timestamps at request-receive, model-first-token, final-token, and compute p50/p95/p99.
07:06 Samir Patel: perfect. One more: function calling-heavy prompts — we make 3 external DB calls mid-stream — can that block and cause head-of-line issues?
07:14 Amir Solano: external I/O in handlers can block but we offer async hooks and recommend microservices for I/O so the model stream isn't held up; also we can front-load retrieval in prefix caching.
07:28 (pause)
07:31 Jordan Lee: cool — action recap — Jordan to run bench with Priya; Priya to prepare Dedicated sizing; Jordan to send security pack. Anything else?
07:40 Lena Ortiz: if you can include a worst-case cost scenario that'd be helpful.
07:42 Jordan Lee: will do.
07:45 Samir Patel: timeline — we want bench results in 2–3 business days and then a follow-up deep-dive.
07:52 Jordan Lee: that's doable, we'll aim for 48 hours.
07:55 (small cross talk and goodbyes)
07:58 Meeting end.

Notes about transcript quality: auto-transcript had some mis-IDs for product names (Redwood Dedicated sometimes became "Redwood Dedicate"), and some acronyms (KV cache) were hyphenated inconsistently. There are a few overlaps and crosstalk segments.

Referenced artifacts/links:
- MapleBridge prompt_set_v1.csv (customer to share link)
- Redwood SOC2 & ISO pack (will be sent)
- Draft Dedicated sizing spreadsheet (Redwood to share)

Explicit next steps (restated):
- Jordan/Priya: run end-to-end token/sec and p95 benchmarks on customer's prompts from eu-west-1 and share raw results and summary (due ~2028-01-19).
- Jordan: share security & private-deploy checklist and TCO model (due ~2028-01-20).
- Priya: prepare Dedicated pool sizing options and autoscale policy examples (due ~2028-01-24).

End of transcript.

title: Adaptive lookahead + batching cost playbook — Optimize walkthrough with Sequoia Retail
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-10-06
Start: 14:30 UTC
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Asha (Redwood Solutions), Marta, Luis, Sarah, Ben (Sequoia)
Link: https://redwood.app/optimize-demo/seq-2027-10-06

Summary:
Quick Optimize walkthrough focused on adaptive lookahead (prefix / KV cache) + batching recommendations tied to a cost model. Customer provided throughput target (~25 req/s) and 80ms P95 latency target. We discussed sessionization, TTL policy, and tradeoffs between small batches + low latency vs larger batches for unit cost.

Topics covered:
- Current stack and constraints
- Sessionization vs global cache patterns
- Lookahead (prefix) caching heuristics and TTLs
- Batch sizing, concurrency, and autoscale knobs
- Cost-sensitivity spreadsheet walk-through
- Next steps and pilot scope

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros, we'll run through an Optimize playbook that marries lookahead/prefix caching with batching so you can see dollar impact. I'm Jordan, AE for Redwood Inference.
[00:22] Marta: Hi, Marta here. We're excited — our team is trying to reduce per-token cost for chat search features while keeping latency tight.
[00:30] Luis: Luis, platform. We run on GCP, k8s cluster fronting a mix of open models. Throughput target is around 20–30 req/s, peaky.
[00:40] Priya: Great, thanks. Quick perf goals recap? P95 target?
[00:44] Ben: We want P95 <= 80ms and acceptable tail for interactive UI. Latency for embeddings less important.
[00:52] Priya: Good. We'll demo two linked configs: adaptive lookahead (aka prefix / KV caching) plus dynamic batching. We'll show cost modeled at token-level in the workbook.

[02:10] Priya: (shares screen) Here's the Console Optimize tab — you'll see workload heatmap on top, sessionization candidate list in the middle. This is seeded from logs you sent, I smoothed some sessions and grouped by user-id.
[02:35] Marta: Looks like many sessions are repeat prompts with small prefixes, yeah that's our commerce chat pattern.
[03:02] Priya: Exactly — good candidate for prefix caching. Idea: cache first N tokens per session signature so KV hits skip early layers and save compute. Tradeoff is cache TTL and cold-miss amplification.
[03:28] Jordan: Also mention concurrency — caching increases effective batch size because many requests become faster, they can be coalesced into larger batches at the runtime. That lowers per-token cost but can increase headroom requirements.
[03:50] Luis: Headroom as in GPU utilization spikes? We have burst capacity but don't want sustained overcommit.
[03:58] Priya: Right, we model sustained throughput and burst separately. For Sequoia's profile I toggled autoscale burst to 1.5x and reserved capacity to match baseline 25 req/s. See the cost tab.

[05:10] Priya: Walking the cost workbook — columns: scenario name, hit rate, avg tokens, batch size, concurrency, per-token cost, infra cost, net $/1000 requests. I populated three scenarios: baseline (no cache), static prefix cache (TTL 30s), adaptive lookahead (TTL 5m with early evict heuristics).
[05:45] Ben: Can you explain adaptive lookahead heuristics?
[05:49] Priya: Sure — heuristics look at session re-use probability per signature and prefetch probability. If user has a high short-term repeat probability, we keep the prefix in KV with longer TTL; otherwise we evict quickly. There's a small misclassification cost which we model as a 5–12% extra token spend in worst cases.
[06:20] Marta: What about cold-start blowup — when a cached prefix goes stale and then many users request the same cold prefix?
[06:27] Priya: We include amplification factor in the workbook. The safe pattern is staged rollout: start with small fraction of sessions pinned to lookahead, run offline replay for 48h to measure amplification, then widen.

[07:00] Jordan: Let's jump to concrete numbers for Sequoia traces. Baseline: per-1k requests = $4.60. Static TTL 30s with 38% hit rate = $3.20. Adaptive lookahead modeled to hit 62% => $2.10. These are illustrative — your real numbers depend on exact token distribution and your batch-concurrency setup.
[07:40] Luis: Those are big savings, but can you show latency impact?
[07:44] Priya: Yes — look at the latency waterfall. Cached hits are ~12–18ms P95 because they bypass decode-heavy layers (quick KV lookup + small extra kernel). Misses are unchanged. End-to-end P95 for adaptive config is 72ms in sim, within your 80ms goal.
[08:10] Sarah: Are these numbers assuming quantized model variants? We use int8 in prod for cost reasons.
[08:16] Priya: Good point — the workbook has two columns: FP16 baseline and int8 quantized path. Quantization reduces per-token cost by roughly 20–30% in our bench, but can slightly increase latency variance. For your profile we modeled int8 with 18% lower cost; combined with lookahead you hit the $2.10 scenario.

[09:05] Jordan: Real-world gotchas — session signature quality matters. You must ensure consistent session keys (user_id + conversation_id or hashed cookie). If sessionization is noisy you'll see lower effective hit rates.
[09:30] Ben: We're hashing user id and conversation id — should be fine. We do have many anonymous browsers without id.
[09:37] Priya: For anonymous flows, fallback to a short TTL (10–30s) and pair with user-behavior heuristics (IP, recency) — avoids heavy overcache.

[10:20] Priya: Now the demo of Console toggle: we enable adaptive lookahead, set base TTL 300s, early-evict threshold at 0.12 reuse prob, and batch target of 32 tokens per request (we set runtime batch target 64 tokens/batch to encourage packing).
[10:45] Jordan: Small note — runtime continuous batching will pack small requests across 30–40ms windows. If you must hit 50ms strict SLOs, reduce the pack window or set batching disabled for that route.
[11:02] Luis: So can we route high-priority UI calls to lower batching config and low-priority background tasks to high-batch config?
[11:08] Priya: Exactly — Redwood routing supports per-route SLO policies. We'll show an example routing rule in the console after the call.

[12:00] (screen pauses, a short technical exchange)
[13:05] Priya: We also looked at KV sizing. Important: TTL * write rate determines memory size. I put a rough calc — if you have 1M active sessions and TTL 5m average with 1.5KB prefix each, that's ~7.5GB of KV. But write amplification and hot keys need planning.
[13:42] Marta: We don't have 1M active sessions — more like 120k daily active, concurrency peaking at 2k.
[13:50] Priya: Ok then your KV footprint drops substantially. We'll include a sizing tab in the workbook.

[15:00] Ben: Anything we need for security/compliance? We have strict PII rules.
[15:08] Jordan: Good call — Redwood's private and dedicated deployments support KMS/HSM integrations, audit logs, and you can opt to disable KV content persistence (store only hashed prefixes) in Private deploy. We'll attach the security brief.
[15:25] Marta: Can you run the benchmark on our exact model and traces? We want end-to-end numbers including network, egress.
[15:31] Priya: Yes — next step is a 24–48h benchmark run using your anonymized traces. We'll run it in your dedicated sandbox or Redwood hosted sandbox, depending on your comfort with data residency.

[16:10] Jordan: Let's surface a couple of lower-probability risks — misrouted sessions causing cache poisoning (rare if you use proper session keys) and model drift causing different token distributions — both are reasons to keep telemetry and regression alerts.
[16:35] Priya: The Console integrates quality monitors — you can pin prompt sets that should be stable and get alerted when cost-saving changes degrade outputs.

[18:00] (discussion about pricing tiers and reserved capacity)
[18:45] Jordan: If you commit to reserved capacity for peak lanes, we can recommend a commit level that hits your cost targets. The workbook shows reserved vs on-demand infra mix for each scenario.
[19:10] Luis: Any limitations with hybrid private deployment? We need VPC peering and customer-managed KMS.
[19:18] Jordan: Both supported. Private deployment adds 5–10% infra ops overhead in our model due to network egress and encryption CPU — the workbook flags that as an adjustment.

[21:00] Marta: Can you walk slower through the rollout plan? We want a canary and an automated rollback.
[21:12] Priya: Sure — plan: 1) offline replay for 48h to tune TTL/heuristics, 2) 5% canary with live traffic for 24h, monitor amplification and quality, 3) 25% run for 48h, then full rollout. Each step has automatic rollback triggers for hit-rate drop or latency spikes. We can provision a canary route in the Console.

[23:30] (brief bandwidth about cost-sheets — Jordan shares a link)
[23:40] Jordan: I've uploaded the cost-sensitivity workbook here: https://redwood.app/sheets/seq-costs-2027-10-06 (view-only). It includes a tab with your sample traces stats.

[25:15] Ben: A question on metrics — how do you compute hit-rate? Is it by session signature or token-level?
[25:21] Priya: Hit-rate here is session-prefix hit-rate (per-request). We show token-level saved too but it's derived. Sometimes a request has partial prefix hit — we measure effective token-savings.

[27:00] Luis: We want to run a quick experiment: start with TTL 60s and batch target 48 for background jobs. Can Redwood toggle these dynamically?
[27:06] Priya: Yes, Console supports per-route config and dynamic feature flags. You can change TTL and batch target without redeploy.

[29:00] (short off-topic about competitor pricing)
[30:05] Marta: You mentioned amplification risk — can you quantify?
[30:12] Priya: In worst case cold-start storm we modeled ~1.6x token spend for a short window. The workbook's "amplification sensitivity" tab shows how changing canary size and pre-warming reduces that to ~1.1x.

[32:00] Jordan: We'll attach a simple pre-warm script that replays top-N prefixes into KV during deploy to avoid cold storms. It's a standard practice.

[33:00] (customer asks about observability)
[33:05] Asha: Console has per-route token cost, KV hit/miss metrics, batch packing distribution, and trace-level correlation to requests so you can see which prompts cause misses. We'll demo that in follow up.

[35:00] Sarah: Anything else on SLAs? We need 99.9% uptime and audit trails.
[35:10] Jordan: Dedicated and Private offerings come with SLA and extended retention. We'll attach the SLA PDF.

[36:15] Priya: Quick demo of a simulated run — I flipped on adaptive lookahead for 10% of traffic. The console shows slot heatmap: hit rate rose from 0% to 46% in canary, cost down 32% for that cohort, P95 stable at 68ms.

[38:00] Ben: Nice. Can Redwood produce a recommended TTL per route?
[38:10] Priya: Yes, Optimize produces suggested TTLs based on replayed traces and a cost-utility function. It ranks suggestions with expected ROI.

[39:30] Marta: Two follow ups: 1) send workbook, 2) run benchmark with our tracers, 3) show Console playbook for canary.

[40:00] Jordan: We'll do that. Any final questions?
[40:08] Luis: About auditability of KV contents — if we hash prefixes can you still compute hit-rates?
[40:14] Priya: Yes, you can store a salted hash; hit-rate computation works if the same salt is applied consistently. You lose human-readable debug but retain metrics.

[41:00] (wrap up)
[41:10] Jordan: Recap of next steps: I'll send the workbook + security brief, Priya will provision the benchmark and run it once we get traces, Marta will share session logs. We agreed on a 24–48h benchmark then a staged rollout.
[41:35] Marta: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[41:40] Jordan: Thanks — we'll follow up with calendar invites for the benchmark kick-off.

End of transcript.

Attachments/links referenced:
- Cost workbook (view): https://redwood.app/sheets/seq-costs-2027-10-06
- Console playbook (canary example): https://redwood.app/playbooks/canary-adaptive-lookahead
- Security brief: https://redwood.app/security/redwood-private-brief.pdf
- Benchmark job template: https://redwood.app/benchmarks/template-24h

Notes about transcript quality: automated transcription; occasional mislabels and slight word errors (e.g. "look ahead" vs "lookahead", product shorthand).
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-10-06
Start: 14:30 UTC
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Asha (Redwood Solutions), Marta, Luis, Sarah, Ben (Sequoia)
Link: https://redwood.app/optimize-demo/seq-2027-10-06

Summary:
Quick Optimize walkthrough focused on adaptive lookahead (prefix / KV cache) + batching recommendations tied to a cost model. Customer provided throughput target (~25 req/s) and 80ms P95 latency target. We discussed sessionization, TTL policy, and tradeoffs between small batches + low latency vs larger batches for unit cost.

Topics covered:
- Current stack and constraints
- Sessionization vs global cache patterns
- Lookahead (prefix) caching heuristics and TTLs
- Batch sizing, concurrency, and autoscale knobs
- Cost-sensitivity spreadsheet walk-through
- Next steps and pilot scope

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intros, we'll run through an Optimize playbook that marries lookahead/prefix caching with batching so you can see dollar impact. I'm Jordan, AE for Redwood Inference.
[00:22] Marta: Hi, Marta here. We're excited — our team is trying to reduce per-token cost for chat search features while keeping latency tight.
[00:30] Luis: Luis, platform. We run on GCP, k8s cluster fronting a mix of open models. Throughput target is around 20–30 req/s, peaky.
[00:40] Priya: Great, thanks. Quick perf goals recap? P95 target?
[00:44] Ben: We want P95 <= 80ms and acceptable tail for interactive UI. Latency for embeddings less important.
[00:52] Priya: Good. We'll demo two linked configs: adaptive lookahead (aka prefix / KV caching) plus dynamic batching. We'll show cost modeled at token-level in the workbook.

[02:10] Priya: (shares screen) Here's the Console Optimize tab — you'll see workload heatmap on top, sessionization candidate list in the middle. This is seeded from logs you sent, I smoothed some sessions and grouped by user-id.
[02:35] Marta: Looks like many sessions are repeat prompts with small prefixes, yeah that's our commerce chat pattern.
[03:02] Priya: Exactly — good candidate for prefix caching. Idea: cache first N tokens per session signature so KV hits skip early layers and save compute. Tradeoff is cache TTL and cold-miss amplification.
[03:28] Jordan: Also mention concurrency — caching increases effective batch size because many requests become faster, they can be coalesced into larger batches at the runtime. That lowers per-token cost but can increase headroom requirements.
[03:50] Luis: Headroom as in GPU utilization spikes? We have burst capacity but don't want sustained overcommit.
[03:58] Priya: Right, we model sustained throughput and burst separately. For Sequoia's profile I toggled autoscale burst to 1.5x and reserved capacity to match baseline 25 req/s. See the cost tab.

[05:10] Priya: Walking the cost workbook — columns: scenario name, hit rate, avg tokens, batch size, concurrency, per-token cost, infra cost, net $/1000 requests. I populated three scenarios: baseline (no cache), static prefix cache (TTL 30s), adaptive lookahead (TTL 5m with early evict heuristics).
[05:45] Ben: Can you explain adaptive lookahead heuristics?
[05:49] Priya: Sure — heuristics look at session re-use probability per signature and prefetch probability. If user has a high short-term repeat probability, we keep the prefix in KV with longer TTL; otherwise we evict quickly. There's a small misclassification cost which we model as a 5–12% extra token spend in worst cases.
[06:20] Marta: What about cold-start blowup — when a cached prefix goes stale and then many users request the same cold prefix?
[06:27] Priya: We include amplification factor in the workbook. The safe pattern is staged rollout: start with small fraction of sessions pinned to lookahead, run offline replay for 48h to measure amplification, then widen.

[07:00] Jordan: Let's jump to concrete numbers for Sequoia traces. Baseline: per-1k requests = $4.60. Static TTL 30s with 38% hit rate = $3.20. Adaptive lookahead modeled to hit 62% => $2.10. These are illustrative — your real numbers depend on exact token distribution and your batch-concurrency setup.
[07:40] Luis: Those are big savings, but can you show latency impact?
[07:44] Priya: Yes — look at the latency waterfall. Cached hits are ~12–18ms P95 because they bypass decode-heavy layers (quick KV lookup + small extra kernel). Misses are unchanged. End-to-end P95 for adaptive config is 72ms in sim, within your 80ms goal.
[08:10] Sarah: Are these numbers assuming quantized model variants? We use int8 in prod for cost reasons.
[08:16] Priya: Good point — the workbook has two columns: FP16 baseline and int8 quantized path. Quantization reduces per-token cost by roughly 20–30% in our bench, but can slightly increase latency variance. For your profile we modeled int8 with 18% lower cost; combined with lookahead you hit the $2.10 scenario.

[09:05] Jordan: Real-world gotchas — session signature quality matters. You must ensure consistent session keys (user_id + conversation_id or hashed cookie). If sessionization is noisy you'll see lower effective hit rates.
[09:30] Ben: We're hashing user id and conversation id — should be fine. We do have many anonymous browsers without id.
[09:37] Priya: For anonymous flows, fallback to a short TTL (10–30s) and pair with user-behavior heuristics (IP, recency) — avoids heavy overcache.

[10:20] Priya: Now the demo of Console toggle: we enable adaptive lookahead, set base TTL 300s, early-evict threshold at 0.12 reuse prob, and batch target of 32 tokens per request (we set runtime batch target 64 tokens/batch to encourage packing).
[10:45] Jordan: Small note — runtime continuous batching will pack small requests across 30–40ms windows. If you must hit 50ms strict SLOs, reduce the pack window or set batching disabled for that route.
[11:02] Luis: So can we route high-priority UI calls to lower batching config and low-priority background tasks to high-batch config?
[11:08] Priya: Exactly — Redwood routing supports per-route SLO policies. We'll show an example routing rule in the console after the call.

[12:00] (screen pauses, a short technical exchange)
[13:05] Priya: We also looked at KV sizing. Important: TTL * write rate determines memory size. I put a rough calc — if you have 1M active sessions and TTL 5m average with 1.5KB prefix each, that's ~7.5GB of KV. But write amplification and hot keys need planning.
[13:42] Marta: We don't have 1M active sessions — more like 120k daily active, concurrency peaking at 2k.
[13:50] Priya: Ok then your KV footprint drops substantially. We'll include a sizing tab in the workbook.

[15:00] Ben: Anything we need for security/compliance? We have strict PII rules.
[15:08] Jordan: Good call — Redwood's private and dedicated deployments support KMS/HSM integrations, audit logs, and you can opt to disable KV content persistence (store only hashed prefixes) in Private deploy. We'll attach the security brief.
[15:25] Marta: Can you run the benchmark on our exact model and traces? We want end-to-end numbers including network, egress.
[15:31] Priya: Yes — next step is a 24–48h benchmark run using your anonymized traces. We'll run it in your dedicated sandbox or Redwood hosted sandbox, depending on your comfort with data residency.

[16:10] Jordan: Let's surface a couple of lower-probability risks — misrouted sessions causing cache poisoning (rare if you use proper session keys) and model drift causing different token distributions — both are reasons to keep telemetry and regression alerts.
[16:35] Priya: The Console integrates quality monitors — you can pin prompt sets that should be stable and get alerted when cost-saving changes degrade outputs.

[18:00] (discussion about pricing tiers and reserved capacity)
[18:45] Jordan: If you commit to reserved capacity for peak lanes, we can recommend a commit level that hits your cost targets. The workbook shows reserved vs on-demand infra mix for each scenario.
[19:10] Luis: Any limitations with hybrid private deployment? We need VPC peering and customer-managed KMS.
[19:18] Jordan: Both supported. Private deployment adds 5–10% infra ops overhead in our model due to network egress and encryption CPU — the workbook flags that as an adjustment.

[21:00] Marta: Can you walk slower through the rollout plan? We want a canary and an automated rollback.
[21:12] Priya: Sure — plan: 1) offline replay for 48h to tune TTL/heuristics, 2) 5% canary with live traffic for 24h, monitor amplification and quality, 3) 25% run for 48h, then full rollout. Each step has automatic rollback triggers for hit-rate drop or latency spikes. We can provision a canary route in the Console.

[23:30] (brief bandwidth about cost-sheets — Jordan shares a link)
[23:40] Jordan: I've uploaded the cost-sensitivity workbook here: https://redwood.app/sheets/seq-costs-2027-10-06 (view-only). It includes a tab with your sample traces stats.

[25:15] Ben: A question on metrics — how do you compute hit-rate? Is it by session signature or token-level?
[25:21] Priya: Hit-rate here is session-prefix hit-rate (per-request). We show token-level saved too but it's derived. Sometimes a request has partial prefix hit — we measure effective token-savings.

[27:00] Luis: We want to run a quick experiment: start with TTL 60s and batch target 48 for background jobs. Can Redwood toggle these dynamically?
[27:06] Priya: Yes, Console supports per-route config and dynamic feature flags. You can change TTL and batch target without redeploy.

[29:00] (short off-topic about competitor pricing)
[30:05] Marta: You mentioned amplification risk — can you quantify?
[30:12] Priya: In worst case cold-start storm we modeled ~1.6x token spend for a short window. The workbook's "amplification sensitivity" tab shows how changing canary size and pre-warming reduces that to ~1.1x.

[32:00] Jordan: We'll attach a simple pre-warm script that replays top-N prefixes into KV during deploy to avoid cold storms. It's a standard practice.

[33:00] (customer asks about observability)
[33:05] Asha: Console has per-route token cost, KV hit/miss metrics, batch packing distribution, and trace-level correlation to requests so you can see which prompts cause misses. We'll demo that in follow up.

[35:00] Sarah: Anything else on SLAs? We need 99.9% uptime and audit trails.
[35:10] Jordan: Dedicated and Private offerings come with SLA and extended retention. We'll attach the SLA PDF.

[36:15] Priya: Quick demo of a simulated run — I flipped on adaptive lookahead for 10% of traffic. The console shows slot heatmap: hit rate rose from 0% to 46% in canary, cost down 32% for that cohort, P95 stable at 68ms.

[38:00] Ben: Nice. Can Redwood produce a recommended TTL per route?
[38:10] Priya: Yes, Optimize produces suggested TTLs based on replayed traces and a cost-utility function. It ranks suggestions with expected ROI.

[39:30] Marta: Two follow ups: 1) send workbook, 2) run benchmark with our tracers, 3) show Console playbook for canary.

[40:00] Jordan: We'll do that. Any final questions?
[40:08] Luis: About auditability of KV contents — if we hash prefixes can you still compute hit-rates?
[40:14] Priya: Yes, you can store a salted hash; hit-rate computation works if the same salt is applied consistently. You lose human-readable debug but retain metrics.

[41:00] (wrap up)
[41:10] Jordan: Recap of next steps: I'll send the workbook + security brief, Priya will provision the benchmark and run it once we get traces, Marta will share session logs. We agreed on a 24–48h benchmark then a staged rollout.
[41:35] Marta: Sounds good. Thanks everyone.
[41:40] Jordan: Thanks — we'll follow up with calendar invites for the benchmark kick-off.

End of transcript.

Attachments/links referenced:
- Cost workbook (view): https://redwood.app/sheets/seq-costs-2027-10-06
- Console playbook (canary example): https://redwood.app/playbooks/canary-adaptive-lookahead
- Security brief: https://redwood.app/security/redwood-private-brief.pdf
- Benchmark job template: https://redwood.app/benchmarks/template-24h

Notes about transcript quality: automated transcription; occasional mislabels and slight word errors (e.g. "look ahead" vs "lookahead", product shorthand).

title: Migration planning: OpenAI + vLLM/TGI exit pathways to Redwood
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date: 2027-09-29
- Start: 15:00 UTC
- Duration: ~48 minutes
- Attendees: Alex Rivera (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Liam Chen (CTO - HeliosDocs), Nora Park (Lead ML Eng), Evan Morales (Eng Manager)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
HeliosDocs is evaluating an escape path from multi-vendor hosting (OpenAI for prod + vLLM/TGI experiments on prem) to Redwood. Primary concerns: inference parity (responses match current prod), latency/cost tradeoffs, private networking (VPC peering/KMS), and how tokenization/sampling differences affect evals. They want a pilot to run parity checks across 3 endpoints and decide between Hosted vs Dedicated.

Topics: migration path, vLLM/TGI compatibility, function-calling parity, KV/prefix caching, dedicated capacity SLAs, security (SOC2/KMS/SAML), pilot scope/timeline.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Alex Rivera: hey everyone thanks for joining, um we know you have a full calendar so we’ll try to be concise, I'm Alex from Redwood, with Priya our solutions eng.
[00:12] Liam Chen: yea thanks alex, appreciate it. quick context from our side — HeliosDocs runs customer support agent and document summarization in prod on OpenAI, we also spin up vLLM and TGI locally for latency experiments and some fine-tuned models. main ask is, can we move everything to one platform and keep parity.
[00:28] Alex Rivera: got it, and timeline?
[00:31] Nora Park: we’re aiming to have a pilot and decision within 4–8 weeks, budget cycles end-mid october so earlier is better.
[00:39] Priya Natarajan: thanks. a couple quick clarifying q's — when you say parity do you mean deterministic output parity or functional parity for downstream metrics? like exact token-for-token match versus similar scoring on your eval sets?
[00:54] Liam Chen: good point. we need functional parity — same perceived answers on the evaluation prompts, and no regressions in two KPIs: intent-classification F1 and average helpfulness score from our human raters. exact token match is unrealistic.
[01:10] Evan Morales: also latency: our SLO is 300ms p95 for short chat queries; cost target is roughly current spend on OpenAI (we can share numbers).
[01:22] Alex Rivera: that helps. quick note on architecture — Redwood can host in three modes: Hosted API, Dedicated reserved pools, or Private VPC (on-prem/air-gapped). For a migration like yours, common path is pilot on Hosted to validate eval parity and performance, then move to Dedicated or Private if you need VPC/KMS.
[01:41] Nora Park: ah ok. how do you handle the vLLM/TGI models we already run? we have snapshots and a couple of finetuned checkpoints.
[01:50] Priya Natarajan: we do model onboarding for vLLM/TGI formats — runtime picks optimized kernels; there are occasionally tokenizer differences and sampling defaults to normalize. we run a compatibility pass: tokenization map, logits scaling, and temperature mapping. it’s not magic but in our experience we can get functional parity for 80–95% of prompts after a parity tuning pass.
[02:12] Liam Chen: tell me more about tokenization issues — we saw differences between OpenAI’s tokenizer and the on-prem TGI setup that caused subtle prompt drift.
[02:21] Priya Natarajan: right. common things we do: normalize pre-tokenization (strip vs non-strip whitespace), align byte-pair merges where possible, and if needed we run a lightweight re-tokenizer step in the pipeline. sometimes you need to re-run a few shots of prompt tuning to equalize context.
[02:39] Alex Rivera: also worth calling out KV cache handling — for some of your streaming workflows you rely on prefix caching to reduce cost. Redwood supports continuous prefix and KV caching across requests, but there are config choices that affect latency vs cache hit rate.
[02:55] Nora Park: is that compatible with our current redis-based cache? we store some prefix keys in redis right now.
[03:01] Priya Natarajan: yes, you can keep redis; we can integrate at the app layer. Redwood also offers built-in KV caching that’s co-located with the runtime for lower latency. we’ll show numbers in the pilot.
[03:13] Evan Morales: security — our security team will want SOC2, SAML SSO, KMS key control, and audit logs for every token. do you have that?
[03:22] Alex Rivera: yes we have SOC2 Type II, we support SAML/SCIM, and for private deployments you can bring your own KMS/HSM. audit logs are available and we can include a redacted sample in the security pack. I’ll attach the pack after the call.
[03:37] Liam Chen: good. another thing is function calling — we use OpenAI function-calling heavily to orchestrate downstream ops. how similar is Redwood behavior there?
[03:46] Priya Natarajan: we support function and tool-calling primitives; the runtime preserves argument structures and streaming of partial function outputs. there are a few vendor-specific heuristics (stop tokens, argument flattening) — we’ll map those and run the same orchestrated prompts in the parity run.
[04:05] Alex Rivera: quick interjection — has anyone tried seeding or temperature mapping to reduce hallucination differences when moving models?
[04:11] Nora Park: we did some temperature experiments on TGI — lower temp helped but throughput dropped.
[04:17] Priya Natarajan: that’s expected. pragmatic approach: use consistent sampling config in the parity run and measure tradeoffs. our Optimize tooling will recommend batch sizes and quant profiles to hit your latency/cost targets.
[04:29] Liam Chen: talk to me about numeric performance — we run two GPUs on prem for the heavy hits, what would Dedicated look like for predictable throughput?
[04:38] Alex Rivera: Dedicated gives reserved GPU pools with autoscaling and burst options. you can pin a model version and reserve capacity for a guaranteed p95 latency. SLA includes uptime and a latency SLO that we can negotiate for enterprise tiers.
[04:52] Evan Morales: any examples of customers with similar p95 targets?
[04:56] Priya Natarajan: yes — one customer had 250ms p95 at 10k qps with batching and prefix caching on a dedicated pool; we can share anonymized numbers.
[05:06] Liam Chen: ok. about evals — what does the parity suite look like?
[05:10] Priya Natarajan: we take your sample prompt set, traffic distribution, and run it against the three candidate runtimes: your TGI snapshot, your OpenAI baseline (we can replay logged responses), and the Redwood runtime. metrics: helpfulness/human score, F1 for intent, latency distributions, token cost, and diff analysis of tokens. we’ll produce a drift matrix.
[05:33] Nora Park: cool. how invasive is the pilot? we don’t want to expose prod keys or send PII out.
[05:38] Alex Rivera: understood. pilot can be sandboxed: use redacted data or synthetic, and you can bring a VPC/Private deployment if you need the data to remain inside your network. for Hosted pilot we support customer-managed keys and strict retention controls.
[05:54] Liam Chen: retention controls — we need logs for troubleshooting but they must be purged after 30 days and encrypted with our KMS.
[06:01] Priya Natarajan: that’s doable. we'll include the retention and encryption options in the security pack.
[06:07] Evan Morales: one operational detail — how do rollbacks and fallback to OpenAI work if we see regressions mid-migration?
[06:13] Alex Rivera: Redwood supports routing policies: you can route a percentage of traffic to Redwood and fall back to OpenAI on failures or regressions. Canary/A-B rollouts are first-class.
[06:23] Liam Chen: nice. any gotchas with TGI model formats? we used a custom tokenizer and a few nonstandard ops.
[06:29] Priya Natarajan: for nonstandard ops we either replace kernels or run a compatibility shim; sometimes we ask customers for a build artifact. tokenizers are usually the biggest source of drift. we log tokenization diffs per prompt so you can see where drift originates.
[06:44] Nora Park: latency numbers — can you ballpark p50/p95 for a 1k-token generation on Hosted vs Dedicated?
[06:51] Alex Rivera: ballpark — Hosted with shared capacity: p50 ~120ms, p95 ~420ms for short chats; Dedicated optimized pools: p50 ~80ms, p95 ~250–300ms depending on model & batching. big caveat is sequence length and batch strategy.
[07:08] Liam Chen: cost?
[07:10] Alex Rivera: we’ll send pricing ranges; pilot pricing is typically discounted, and Dedicated gives lower per-token unit economics at committed volumes. we’ll include a modeled cost spreadsheet.
[07:19] Evan Morales: ok. one more nit — how do you validate reproducibility for finetuned checkpoints? we have a handful of customer-tuned models.
[07:27] Priya Natarajan: we re-run your training/inference artifacts in our runtime, compare logits distributions and sample outputs. if mismatch, we try tokenization alignment, sampling mapping, and occasionally small re-tuning. we document fixes in a migration playbook.
[07:44] Liam Chen: great. does Redwood support streaming partial responses to our websocket-based frontend?
[07:50] Alex Rivera: yes — streaming via SSE/Websocket is supported and function-calling streaming works too.
[07:56] Nora Park: awesome. any SLA for streaming reliability?
[08:00] Priya Natarajan: streaming reliability is tied to the same SLOs; for Dedicated you can request stricter SLAs.
[08:06] Alex Rivera: quick pause — Priya, do we have a sample parity report to show?
[08:09] Priya Natarajan: yes I’ll attach a redacted parity report from a similar migration in the deck, plus a template benchmark sheet. link I’ll add to notes.
[08:20] Liam Chen: sweet, can you run a small smoke test on one of our TGI snapshots? we can provide a sanitized snapshot and prompt set.
[08:27] Priya Natarajan: yes we can. for the smoke test we typically run: 500 prompts, 3 sampling configs (temp 0.2/0.7/1.0), and measure helpfulness and latency. that gives a quick sense of parity.
[08:42] Evan Morales: how long for that?
[08:44] Priya Natarajan: 3–5 working days after we have artifacts and access to snapshots.
[08:49] Liam Chen: ok that timeline works.
[08:51] Alex Rivera: next steps recap — we’ll send the security pack and pilot plan, Priya will run the smoke parity run once you share sanitized artifacts, and Alex will send pricing. Can we aim to have artifacts by Monday 2027-10-04?
[09:06] Liam Chen: yes we can do that.
[09:08] Nora Park: one last question — can Redwood host a private controller in our AWS account so logs never leave our VPC?
[09:14] Alex Rivera: yes, Private deployment supports a customer-managed control plane and optional on-prem package. we’ll include deployment diagrams in the pack.
[09:24] Evan Morales: any regulatory constraints we should be aware of? our docs contain PHI occasionally.
[09:29] Priya Natarajan: for PHI we recommend Private or Dedicated with customer-managed KMS and strict retention; we also offer an audited deployment checklist for HIPAA-adjacent use cases.
[09:42] Liam Chen: ok thanks everyone, that was helpful.
[09:45] Alex Rivera: thanks Liam, Nora, Evan. we’ll follow up with the doc links and pilot sheet.

(occasionally in the transcript there were short crosstalks and filler words omitted)

Recap / explicit next steps:
- Redwood: send security pack (SOC2, SAML, KMS options), attach parity report example and pilot plan (deck link): https://redwood.app/deck/heliosdocs-pilot
- Customer: share sanitized TGI snapshot and sample prompt set by 2027-10-04
- Redwood SE: run smoke parity run and upload to benchmark sheet: https://docs.redwood.com/benchmarks/heliosdocs-pilot (deliver by 2027-10-06)
- Alex: pricing and pilot agreement by 2027-10-01

End of meeting.
