[03:37] Liam Chen: good. another thing is function calling — we use OpenAI function-calling heavily to orchestrate downstream ops. how similar is Redwood behavior there?
[03:46] Priya Natarajan: we support function and tool-calling primitives; the runtime preserves argument structures and streaming of partial function outputs. there are a few vendor-specific heuristics (stop tokens, argument flattening) — we’ll map those and run the same orchestrated prompts in the parity run.
[04:05] Alex Rivera: quick interjection — has anyone tried seeding or temperature mapping to reduce hallucination differences when moving models?
[04:11] Nora Park: we did some temperature experiments on TGI — lower temp helped but throughput dropped.
[04:17] Priya Natarajan: that’s expected. pragmatic approach: use consistent sampling config in the parity run and measure tradeoffs. our Optimize tooling will recommend batch sizes and quant profiles to hit your latency/cost targets.
[04:29] Liam Chen: talk to me about numeric performance — we run two GPUs on prem for the heavy hits, what would Dedicated look like for predictable throughput?
[04:38] Alex Rivera: Dedicated gives reserved GPU pools with autoscaling and burst options. you can pin a model version and reserve capacity for a guaranteed p95 latency. SLA includes uptime and a latency SLO that we can negotiate for enterprise tiers.
[04:52] Evan Morales: any examples of customers with similar p95 targets?
[04:56] Priya Natarajan: yes — one customer had 250ms p95 at 10k qps with batching and prefix caching on a dedicated pool; we can share anonymized numbers.
[05:06] Liam Chen: ok. about evals — what does the parity suite look like?
[05:10] Priya Natarajan: we take your sample prompt set, traffic distribution, and run it against the three candidate runtimes: your TGI snapshot, your OpenAI baseline (we can replay logged responses), and the Redwood runtime. metrics: helpfulness/human score, F1 for intent, latency distributions, token cost, and diff analysis of tokens. we’ll produce a drift matrix.
[05:33] Nora Park: cool. how invasive is the pilot? we don’t want to expose prod keys or send PII out.
[05:38] Alex Rivera: understood. pilot can be sandboxed: use redacted data or synthetic, and you can bring a VPC/Private deployment if you need the data to remain inside your network. for Hosted pilot we support customer-managed keys and strict retention controls.
[05:54] Liam Chen: retention controls — we need logs for troubleshooting but they must be purged after 30 days and encrypted with our KMS.
[06:01] Priya Natarajan: that’s doable. we'll include the retention and encryption options in the security pack.
[06:07] Evan Morales: one operational detail — how do rollbacks and fallback to OpenAI work if we see regressions mid-migration?
[06:13] Alex Rivera: Redwood supports routing policies: you can route a percentage of traffic to Redwood and fall back to OpenAI on failures or regressions. Canary/A-B rollouts are first-class.
[06:23] Liam Chen: nice. any gotchas with TGI model formats? we used a custom tokenizer and a few nonstandard ops.
[06:29] Priya Natarajan: for nonstandard ops we either replace kernels or run a compatibility shim; sometimes we ask customers for a build artifact. tokenizers are usually the biggest source of drift. we log tokenization diffs per prompt so you can see where drift originates.
[06:44] Nora Park: latency numbers — can you ballpark p50/p95 for a 1k-token generation on Hosted vs Dedicated?
[06:51] Alex Rivera: ballpark — Hosted with shared capacity: p50 ~120ms, p95 ~420ms for short chats; Dedicated optimized pools: p50 ~80ms, p95 ~250–300ms depending on model & batching. big caveat is sequence length and batch strategy.
[07:08] Liam Chen: cost?
[07:10] Alex Rivera: we’ll send pricing ranges; pilot pricing is typically discounted, and Dedicated gives lower per-token unit economics at committed volumes. we’ll include a modeled cost spreadsheet.
[07:19] Evan Morales: ok. one more nit — how do you validate reproducibility for finetuned checkpoints? we have a handful of customer-tuned models.
[07:27] Priya Natarajan: we re-run your training/inference artifacts in our runtime, compare logits distributions and sample outputs. if mismatch, we try tokenization alignment, sampling mapping, and occasionally small re-tuning. we document fixes in a migration playbook.
[07:44] Liam Chen: great. does Redwood support streaming partial responses to our websocket-based frontend?
[07:50] Alex Rivera: yes — streaming via SSE/Websocket is supported and function-calling streaming works too.
[07:56] Nora Park: awesome. any SLA for streaming reliability?
[08:00] Priya Natarajan: streaming reliability is tied to the same SLOs; for Dedicated you can request stricter SLAs.
[08:06] Alex Rivera: quick pause — Priya, do we have a sample parity report to show?
[08:09] Priya Natarajan: yes I’ll attach a redacted parity report from a similar migration in the deck, plus a template benchmark sheet. link I’ll add to notes.
[08:20] Liam Chen: sweet, can you run a small smoke test on one of our TGI snapshots? we can provide a sanitized snapshot and prompt set.
[08:27] Priya Natarajan: yes we can. for the smoke test we typically run: 500 prompts, 3 sampling configs (temp 0.2/0.7/1.0), and measure helpfulness and latency. that gives a quick sense of parity.
[08:42] Evan Morales: how long for that?
[08:44] Priya Natarajan: 3–5 working days after we have artifacts and access to snapshots.
[08:49] Liam Chen: ok that timeline works.
[08:51] Alex Rivera: next steps recap — we’ll send the security pack and pilot plan, Priya will run the smoke parity run once you share sanitized artifacts, and Alex will send pricing. Can we aim to have artifacts by Monday 2027-10-04?
[09:06] Liam Chen: yes we can do that.
[09:08] Nora Park: one last question — can Redwood host a private controller in our AWS account so logs never leave our VPC?
[09:14] Alex Rivera: yes, Private deployment supports a customer-managed control plane and optional on-prem package. we’ll include deployment diagrams in the pack.
[09:24] Evan Morales: any regulatory constraints we should be aware of? our docs contain PHI occasionally.
[09:29] Priya Natarajan: for PHI we recommend Private or Dedicated with customer-managed KMS and strict retention; we also offer an audited deployment checklist for HIPAA-adjacent use cases.
[09:42] Liam Chen: ok thanks everyone, that was helpful.
[09:45] Alex Rivera: thanks Liam, Nora, Evan. we’ll follow up with the doc links and pilot sheet.

(occasionally in the transcript there were short crosstalks and filler words omitted)

Recap / explicit next steps:
- Redwood: send security pack (SOC2, SAML, KMS options), attach parity report example and pilot plan (deck link): https://redwood.app/deck/heliosdocs-pilot
- Customer: share sanitized TGI snapshot and sample prompt set by 2027-10-04
- Redwood SE: run smoke parity run and upload to benchmark sheet: https://docs.redwood.com/benchmarks/heliosdocs-pilot (deliver by 2027-10-06)
- Alex: pricing and pilot agreement by 2027-10-01

End of meeting.

title: 2027-09-08-performance-pulse-poc-bench-contrast-with-incumbent-skylinebio
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date: 2027-09-08
- Start: 16:00 PT
- Duration: 58 minutes (scheduled 16:00-16:58)
- Attendees: Ethan Morales (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Liam Carter (Redwood CSM); Alex Chen (Skyline ML Eng), Marta Ruiz (Skyline Head of Platform), Jordan King (Skyline SRE)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be imperfect):
Redwood and Skyline reviewed the latest POC bench run. Redwood reported consistent p50 improvements vs incumbent but p95 variance remains in tail. Discussed routing changes, dedicated slot run, KV cache warmup and quantization impact. Security and residency questions raised; next steps include raw log share, dedicated run, and executive readout.

Topics: results overview; tail latency; throughput tuning; cache/quantize tradeoffs; security docs; next steps and schedule.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Ethan (Redwood AE): All right good afternoon everybody, thanks for joining. We'll — uh — do a quick recap of the last week, then Priya will walk through the lab numbers and then we'll triage the p95 tail.
[00:18] Alex (Skyline): Hey Ethan, yeah thanks. We had a busy week, ran two overnight sweeps against the incumbent and one against Redwood, so we have some artifacts to share.
[00:26] Priya (Redwood SE): Cool, I'll start with the headline. On average across the 20k sample prompts the p50 is down about 22% versus IncumbentCloudAI under the same sequence length distribution. p95 is still noisy, some runs had spikes around 320ms extra.
[00:44] Marta (Skyline): When you say 22% p50, do you mean absolute latency or relative after quantization? Our baseline runs used FP16, not quantized.
[00:55] Priya: Good call. The 22% is for our quantized profile q4_0 on the Redwood runtime compared to the incumbent's FP16 baseline. We also ran a non-quantized Redwood run — that was closer to 12% improvement.
[01:14] Jordan (Skyline SRE): The thing we worry about is the tail — p95 — for customer facing features we need 99th under 1 second for requests less than 512 tokens. Some of your spikes in the early runs breached that.
[01:30] Priya: Right, we looked into that. The spikes correlated with cold KV cache misses and autoscaler spin-up events. We saw about a 22% miss rate for the prefix cache on those short runs because the traffic mix was more bursty.
[01:45] Ethan: To add, we think two knobs will help: 1) dedicated warm slots during expected peak windows, and 2) a small routing carve — send 20% of traffic to a warmed pool so KV cache hit rate stays high.
[01:58] Alex: Can you explain the warmed pool idea? Is that a Redwood feature or something we need to implement client-side?
[02:06] Priya: It's a routing policy in Redwood — we can allocate a dedicated slot pool or configure a warm-up probe. On the hosted side we can do it in the control-plane routing rules; in Private you'd run the agent that pins slots.
[02:20] Alex: Hmm, okay. For POC acceptance we want head-to-head numbers with our real trace. I'll share that after the call.
[02:29] Ethan: Great. Quick note on cost: unit token cost on the Redwood bench was about 0.14 USD per 1k tokens in this POC profile vs incumbent at 0.19, so roughly 25% lower. But of course that depends on batching and cache.
[02:45] Marta: Those numbers are promising. What did you assume for effective batch sizes? Our requests are low-latency, single-turn chat.
[02:54] Priya: We used dynamic batching tuned for the latency SLO: target batch size of 4 for the short requests. The billing profile included batching benefits and KV cache savings.
[03:05] Jordan: Any chance the billing has hidden costs for dedicated slots or burst capacity? We have procurement folks who will ask.
[03:12] Ethan: We'll include a breakout in the pricing sheet. Dedicated slot monthly fee plus lower per-token on committed capacity — we'll make it explicit.
[03:20] Priya: Back to the p95 — in the runs where the p95 was bad, we saw autoscaler oscillation. We had scale-in events during the middle of the trace and then scale back up which caused some scheduling latencies. We can adjust cooldowns and also increase min-replicas for a stable baseline.
[03:38] Alex: Did you try a run with min-replicas = 3? That might address the oscillation.
[03:44] Priya: Yes, we ran that as an experiment — p95 improved by about 80ms, and the throughput stayed consistent. The tradeoff is a slightly higher infra baseline cost.
[03:56] Marta: For our legal/security team, we also need to see SOC 2 and key management docs — how do you handle KMS and tenant keys?
[04:05] Liam (Redwood CSM): We have integrations with most cloud KMS and can support customer-managed keys in Private deployments. For hosted we'll provide envelope encryption and audit logs; we'll share the SOC two artifacts through the secure portal.
[04:22] Alex: Is SSO supported? We use Okta and need SAML + SCIM for teams.
[04:28] Ethan: Yes, SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning are supported, plus RBAC. We'll put links in the deck.
[04:35] Jordan: One operational question — if we go Private in our VPC, do we still get Redwood Console telemetry?
[04:41] Priya: Yes, the control plane gives telemetry; in VPC mode you can either route telemetry to your monitoring or we can push a filtered view. We'll detail the observability options in the security pack.
[04:52] Alex: Back on the bench: can you walk through the sample prompt types that caused the worst tail?
[05:00] Priya: Sure — two categories: 1) long-context (> 1500 tokens) multi-turn summarization where KV cache grows and the model reads a large history, and 2) highly token-dense single requests that caused larger internal attention kernels and kernel re-selects. The first one is more cache-sensitive.
[05:20] Alex: So those are edge for us but they are real. We need to see deterministic runs for those traces.
[05:27] Priya: We'll schedule a deterministic run with a pinned slot and warmed KV cache. That will isolate scheduling jitter.
[05:34] Ethan: Also we'd like to test the fallback routing: if the dedicated pool is saturated we'll route to a compatible lower-cost variant with slightly higher latency but same semantics — Redwood can do that automatically.
[05:46] Marta: What about output quality? We don't want regressions from quantization or model variant.
[05:51] Priya: Absolutely. We run evals on the perf-flavor and compare to baseline quality metrics. In these runs quantization q4_0 preserved >98% of the eval score on the Skyline prompt set; we'll include the eval CSV.
[06:07] Jordan: Is there any change to tokenization that could impact cost accounting? Our billing ties to token counts.
[06:13] Ethan: Tokenization is model-dependent; for fairness we used the same tokenizer used in your incumbent runs. We can provide the tokenization script so you can reconcile counts.
[06:22] Speaker 5: (inaudible)
[06:27] Priya: Quick note on throughput: in the overnight 10k-user trace Redwood sustained 420 RPS with p50 ~ 120ms and p95 ~ 470ms; incumbent sustained 360 RPS with p50 ~ 150ms and p95 ~ 520ms. That contributed to the 25% better token cost earlier.
[06:48] Alex: Did you measure KV cache hit rate for that run?
[06:52] Priya: Yes, overall ~78% but in burst windows it dropped to mid-50s which is when p95 spiked.
[06:59] Jordan: Anything you can do on the model side to reduce cold cache cost? Like sharded KV preload?
[07:06] Priya: We have a prewarm probe that touches common prefixes and warms the KV pages. Also shard-aware pinning on dedicated pools reduces cross-host misses.
[07:16] Ethan: Operationally for the POC we recommend a weekend run using sample traces with min-replicas bumped and warm probes enabled; that should show the steady-state tail.
[07:26] Marta: Ok let's target that. We'll try to get the trace to you tonight.
[07:31] Alex: On the topic of fallback — how does Redwood guarantee semantics when failing over to a lower-cost model? Determinism concerns.
[07:39] Priya: Fallback uses model equivalence checks; the control plane only routes to verified variants that meet a quality threshold against your eval set. We also stream logs so you can inspect diffs.
[07:50] Alex: Great.
[07:52] Ethan: Anything else on performance before we jump to the action tracker?
[07:56] Jordan: One more: our network team flagged that sometimes cold container pulls caused a 100-200ms spike. Is that something you've seen?
[08:05] Priya: Yep we've seen container image pull time cause cold-starts in on-prem nodes. For hosted we keep images on local cache; for Private you can set up local registries or pre-pulled images. We'll document both approaches.
[08:19] Ethan: Ok moving to the triage list — Priya will run the dedicated-slot test and attach logs; we'll also run a cost-sensitivity sweep toggling min-replicas and warm-probe interval.
[08:31] Priya: I'll add those tests and post results to the shared sheet. Expect CSVs and Grafana snapshots.
[08:37] Marta: Also please include the raw traces for the run so our profiler can ingest — not just aggregated metrics.
[08:44] Ethan: We'll share raw traces; we'll anonymize tokens per your compliance note.
[08:48] Alex: Quick security follow-up — can you share your audit-log retention policies? We need 12 months of immutable audit logs in our SOC review.
[08:57] Liam: In hosted we retain tenant audit logs for 12 months by default for enterprise tier; longer retention available as an add-on. For Private you control retention.
[09:08] Jordan: OK good.
[09:11] Ethan: Any blockers on the timeline? We were hoping to have a pilot decision by mid-October.
[09:17] Marta: The procurement timeline is likely late October unless the engineering team signs off earlier. If the weekend run looks good we can accelerate.
[09:25] Alex: Can you summarise the experiment config you'll run this weekend?
[09:30] Priya: Sure: 1) Dedicated pool with 4 min-replicas, 2) warm-probe every 30s, 3) q4_0 quant profile and an FP16 control run, 4) real Skyline trace with 10k virtual users over 6 hours, 5) capture p50/p95/p99, KV hit rate, scheduler events, and container pull times.
[09:53] Alex: That sounds right. Also include the incumbent re-run with identical start conditions.
[09:58] Priya: We'll coordinate that with your trace.
[10:02] Ethan: I'll make sure pricing and a dedicated vs hosted cost comparison is in the sheet.
[10:06] Marta: Good. One more question — regulatory: any EU data residency concerns if we start with hosted? We have users in EU.
[10:14] Ethan: We have region routing and can pin inference to EU regions; for stricter controls we can do Private in your EU VPC. We'll note options.
[10:23] Jordan: Okay that's it from infra.
[10:27] Priya: I'll recap the next actions — run the dedicated-slot weekend test, upload raw traces and CSVs, and also run the incumbent with identical config.
[10:36] Alex: Perfect.
[10:39] Ethan: Let's open the floor for any last questions — otherwise we'll end with a quick recap and confirm owners.
[10:45] Marta: No more questions. Thanks everyone.
[10:48] Ethan: Cool, recap: Redwood to share logs and pricing (Ethan), Redwood to run dedicated warm-slot weekend test (Priya), Skyline to send the production trace and confirm procurement timeline (Marta). We'll reconvene on Monday for a quick sync if results are ready.
[10:59] Alex: Monday works. Thanks.
[11:02] Ethan: Thanks all. Ending call.

(Auto-transcript notes: some words may be mistranscribed: "KV cache" appeared as "K V cash" in one chunk; "SOC 2" rendered as "SOC two"; speaker labels may be imperfect.)

Questions (auto-detected):
- What caused the p95 spikes? (Answer: cold KV cache misses + autoscaler spin-ups; mitigations: warm probes, min-replicas, dedicated slots)
- How do fallback model variants preserve quality? (Answer: control plane verified variants + eval diffs)
- What security artifacts are available? (Answer: SOC 2, KMS integration docs, audit-log retention)

Ending header: call ended at 16:58 PT

title: Per-unit workflow cost intake - Quantum Harbor
preview: Intro discovery focused on mapping Quantum Harbor's conversational workflow to per-unit economics. Customer cares about predictable monthly spend and sub-200ms token latency for core flow; exploring Hosted for initial pilot with Dedicated for steady throughput if unit cost can't be achieved. Discussed batching, prefix cache reuse, and where quantization might help. Action: send cost model, run sim, and collect prompt samples.

title: Converge Health — Hosted chat hands-on stream primer
preview:
Header: Date: 2027-08-25; Start: 16:00 PT; Duration: ~52 minutes; Recording ID: ff-20270825-7764c
Attendees: Evan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Jordan (Redwood DevAdv), Maya (Converge Lead Eng), Liam (PM), Noah (Platform)

Summary: short walk-through of Redwood hosted chat (streaming behavior, params like temperature/max_tokens/top_p, and retry/backoff patterns). Quick console tour, mention of Optimize suggestions and a proposed pilot. Security questions touched briefly (SOC2, SSO/SAML, KMS).

[00:00] Evan: Hey everyone thanks for jumping on today. quick round of intros?
[00:08] Maya: Maya, lead eng on Conversational care at Converge. we're evaluating hosted vs dedicated.
[00:12] Liam: Liam, product — timeline is Q4 POC ideally.
[00:16] Noah: Noah — platform, mostly infra and security checks.
[00:20] Evan: Awesome. Priya and Jordan from our side will run through streaming basics and then do a short console demo and Optimize recommendations.
[00:28] Priya: Hi all. quick note, i'll demo streaming, show what a retry looks like and how to tune temperature and top-p for your triage flows. Also we'll show the console's trace for a streamed response.
[00:44] Jordan: I'll jump in with a live SDK snippet and a fallback example; i'm gonna intentionally force a retry so you can see the client behavior.
[01:05] Priya: To set expectations — hosted gives you lowest time-to-value, predictable token billing. Dedicated can give slightly lower tail latency at scale but requires capacity commit. you mentioned Q4 POC, right Liam?
[01:18] Liam: yes Q4, we want a 4–6 week POC with 100k monthly active sessions as a target, ballpark.
[01:24] Evan: great. quick qualification: where are you running your frontend — in GCP europe?
[01:28] Maya: mostly GCP us-east now, planning eur nodes later. also, data residency for PHI-ish notes is a concern.
[01:36] Priya: good to know — hosted supports regional routing and we can restrict logs retention; for PHI we usually recommend Private or Dedicated with a customer-managed KMS and longer audit trails.
[01:48] [01:48] Jordan: okay demo time. sending a streaming chat message (shows code) — here's the SDK call: conversation.create with stream:true, model: "rwd-chat-2.1", temperature:0.2, max_tokens:150, top_p:0.9. You'll see chunks arrive every 50–200ms depending on network and tokenization.
[02:10] [02:10] (stream starts) Speaker: Redwood.Runtime: chunk1: "Hi Maya, thanks for trying Redwood..." chunk2: "We can tune temperature down for more deterministic responses..."
[02:35] Maya: ok that streaming looks clean. what happens on a dropped connection?
[02:40] Priya: if the TCP connection drops mid-flight, client should re-establish and request from last known token via idempotency key and resume token index; our SDK exposes resumeToken. If resume is not possible we fall back to a short re-request and we have heuristics to avoid duplicating content. There is also an automatic retry with exponential backoff on the server side for transient 5xx errors.
[03:10] Noah: is the resumeToken stored on client or we persist it server-side?
[03:14] Priya: the token pointer is a small client-side state; we recommend persisting it if you expect long-lived reconnections. in practice many customers do a quick 2–3 second backoff and re-open stream.
[03:28] [03:28] Jordan: i'm going to throttle edges to simulate latency and force a 503 so you can see the retry dance. (typing sounds) okay sending request with header x-retry-id: demo-01
[03:45] (brief silence)
[03:48] Jordan: ok we got a 502 then a 200 on the retry. you'll notice in the console trace the initial attempt shows as failed and the next attempt as succeeded with the same idempotency token.
[04:05] Priya: important pattern: keep your client idempotency key tied to user-turn-id to avoid accidental double-sends. we also surface an SDK helper for exponential backoff with jitter.
[04:20] Liam: curious, how does this interact with caching — you mentioned prefix caching earlier?
[04:25] Priya: right — we do prefix/KV cache on common system prompts and static context. if you re-request the same prefix within the cache TTL, the request can be served faster and sometimes no model forward pass needed. caching benefits depend on prompt stability.
[04:45] Maya: can that cause stale responses?
[04:48] Priya: yes if you cache too aggressively; that's why we give recommendations in Optimize to suggest TTLs per route. you can also pin which parts of the context are cacheable.
[05:02] [05:02] Jordan: small demonstration of changing temperature live. sending a sample prompt (triage message). temperature .8 vs .1 — watch the variance in the streaming chunks.
[05:18] (stream shows more exploratory continuations at .8, and terse deterministic output at .1)
[05:36] Maya: that helps. for our triage flow we want consistent short answers, probably temperature .1–.2 and max_tokens 80.
[05:44] Priya: that's a common pattern. also consider top_p 0.7 if you want to constrain nucleus sampling further.
[05:52] [05:52] Noah: any advice on tokenization surprises? we saw a burst of tokens on a doc paste once.
[05:58] Priya: yes long pasted text can blow up token count; we recommend truncation heuristics or client-side summarization. Optimize can run sample prompts and report expected token counts and cost per-turn.
[06:15] Evan: Jordan, show the console trace please?
[06:18] Jordan: sharing screen. console -> traces -> request demo-01. you can see graph of attempts, latency percentiles, token counts by segment, and a raw event stream. Also there's an exported CSV option for your PM and SRE.
[06:38] [06:38] (screen share noises)
[06:40] Liam: how do we get alerts on regressions?
[06:42] Priya: Optimize can run evals attached to your prompt set; we surface quality regressions vs baseline and can trigger email/slack alerts. there's also model-level regression detection if we detect drift after a model version swap.
[06:59] Maya: regarding security — we need SOC2 attestations and ability to map audit logs to tenant IDs. can you preserve or export raw logs for audits?
[07:07] Evan: hosted can retain full logs for a configurable retention, and we can provide per-tenant audit exports. for PHI we usually recommend Private with customer KMS or Dedicated with a strong contractual SLA.
[07:20] Noah: about encryption — do you support customer-managed keys (HSM/KMS)?
[07:23] Priya: yes, for Dedicated and Private we support KMS integrations; hosted supports envelope encryption and at-rest encryption with provider-managed keys, but we can discuss a plan.
[07:35] [07:35] Jordan: ok back to the demo, i'm now going to show a fallback pattern — primary model rwd-chat-2.1 and fallback to rwd-chat-small when tail latency spikes or capacity is constrained. triggering fallback.
[07:52] (console shows auto-fallback event)
[07:55] Priya: fallback should be a semantic-compatible model; we recommend testing for quality delta before enabling automatic fallback. fallback cutover is controlled by routing policy in the console.
[08:12] Liam: can we A/B test different fallback thresholds?
[08:15] Priya: yes, canary or A/B rollouts are first class — you can route 5% traffic to fallback and measure regret.
[08:24] [08:24] Maya: what's the expected latency improvement for fallback small model?
[08:28] Jordan: usually 20–40% tail improvement but depends on sequence length. we also suggest quantized model variants for Dedicated that reduce memory and improve throughput.
[08:42] Noah: we might need to evaluate cost per 1M tokens — can you send a TCO sketch?
[08:47] Evan: yes i'll include a hosted vs dedicated cost sketch, with a few usage bands.
[08:53] [08:53] (brief pause)
[09:00] Priya: small gotcha — streaming and HTTP/2: some proxies drop long-lived HTTP/2 connections. we recommend websocket or short polling with resume tokens on flaky mobile networks.
[09:16] Maya: our mobile app will be an important client; resumeToken flow sounds important then.
[09:20] Priya: exactly. also SDK includes an autoretry helper tuned for mobile networks.
[09:28] [09:28] Jordan: next, quick look at a failure mode: double responses after an aggressive retry. I'm going to simulate duplicate success — see how the SDK dedups via sequence markers in the chunk metadata. the transcript shows duplicate chunk ids and our client drops duplicates.
[09:46] Liam: that's reassuring.
[09:48] [09:48] Maya: question about rate limits — what happens if we exceed simultaneous streams?
[09:52] Priya: we enforce per-tenant concurrency limits and token rate limits. if you exceed, you'll get 429 with Retry-After and we recommend a client-side circuit-breaker to reduce new streams and queue small requests. Optimize can suggest concurrency targets based on your workload.
[10:14] Noah: on the privacy front — do you do model logging of user content by default?
[10:18] Evan: by default hosted keeps logs for debugging and billing, but you can opt-out of storing prompt/response text for certain routes; we can also redact PII server-side. Private/Dedicated gives stricter controls.
[10:32] [10:32] Jordan: okay switching to sample code for retries: showing an SDK helper retryWithJitter(maxAttempts:3, baseMs:300) — we recommend 3 tries for 5xx transient, with exponential backoff 300->900->2700 and jitter.
[10:52] Priya: also consider idempotency for generation vs chat: if you re-issue a user-turn re-generation, tie to the same idempotency key so you can resolve duplicates on the client.
[11:05] Maya: for evaluation we need deterministic outputs for scoring; how do we lock that down?
[11:09] Priya: set temperature=0, top_p=0, seed if supported, and pin model version; Optimize will run eval sweep to surface variance.
[11:20] [11:20] Jordan: quick failure case: model version swap in the middle of a session. We recommend pinning model at session start or gracefully migrating. The console shows a migration window and rollback hooks.
[11:38] Liam: are there SDKs for Node/Python/Go?
[11:41] Jordan: yes, official SDKs for Node and Python; community Go client exists. snippets in the repo, link in the follow-up.
[11:50] [11:50] Priya: small note on KV cache pruning — if you have long conversations, we prune the KV cache older than X tokens to keep memory in check; users can also configure pruning policy.
[12:05] Maya: can Optimize suggest pruning thresholds per route?
[12:08] Priya: yep Optimize's workload profiler gives TTL and prune suggestions based on sequence length distribution.
[12:16] [12:16] Jordan: time check — about 40 minutes left? actually we've used ~12 mins, sorry math off, we have ~40 left. next i'll run a short simulated pilot using your sample prompts if Maya uploaded them, otherwise i'll use our synthetic set.
[12:34] (typing)
[12:36] Jordan: running batch of 50 sample prompts through hosted chat at temperature .15, measuring p50/p95, token counts, and cost. triggering a couple of 5xx to exercise retry logic.
[12:54] (console shows aggregated metrics)
[12:56] Priya: see p50 ~120ms, p95 ~420ms for these short prompts; cost per-turn ~0.0025 USD on this model for this demo workload.
[13:08] Noah: that p95 looks fine. how will this change with user attachments or longer contexts?
[13:12] Priya: longer contexts drive both token cost and latency; you can use client-side chunking or a summary layer before the model to keep turn cost bounded.
[13:24] Maya: regarding integrations — we use SSO (SAML) and SCIM for provisioning.
[13:28] Evan: we support SAML SSO and SCIM provisioning for Redwood Console; we'll share the SSO setup doc. note sometimes the transcript generator mis-parses 'SAML' as 'sam-el' in older exports.
[13:44] [13:44] Liam: do you expose per-route RBAC?
[13:47] Priya: yes RBAC at the console level and API keys can be scoped to routes/environments.
[13:54] Jordan: another quick note — when using streaming with webhooks or server proxies, ensure the proxy allows long-lived responses; some older NGINX configs need tweaking.
[14:10] Maya: good to know.
[14:12] [14:12] Priya: final demo piece — Optimize suggestions. i uploaded the sample run and Optimize recommends: lower temperature to .12, use prefix cache TTL 300s, concurrency 8 per shard, and enable a small-model fallback at threshold 800ms p95. It also suggests quantization to int4 for dedicated deployment to reduce cost 30%.
[14:35] Liam: can Optimize produce a one-page checklist for engineering?
[14:38] Priya: yes we can export an ops checklist and share it in the pilot folder.
[14:44] [14:44] Evan: time to surface open questions — Noah you had infra blockers?
[14:49] Noah: we need to validate egress rules and whether streaming uses static IPs for allowlists.
[14:54] Priya: hosted does not guarantee static egress IPs; for allowlisting, Dedicated or Private is the recommended route. we'll note that in the follow-up.
[15:05] Maya: we also need an SLA on request latency and an escalation contact.
[15:09] Evan: we'll include SLA language in the pilot agreement and provide an on-call SRE contact for the pilot.
[15:16] [15:16] Jordan: wrapping up demo, quick recap: streaming resume and resumeToken, retry helper with jitter, console trace and Optimize recommendations, fallback model pattern, and security notes.
[15:30] Maya: good recap. next steps?
[15:33] Evan: action items — Priya share retry guide and console export; Evan share TCO and pilot contract; Maya share prompt set. We'll schedule pilot kickoff next Wed.
[15:45] Liam: thumbs up.
[15:48] Noah: fine with that.
[15:50] Priya: sending the links now in chat: https://docs.redwood.inference/streaming-retries, https://console.redwood.inference/playbooks/optimize-suggest, and the sample SDK repo https://github.com/redwood-inference/sdk-samples.
[16:02] Evan: thanks everyone, we'll follow up with the email and calendar invite.
[16:06] (call ends)

Notes about transcript quality: occasional mis-hearings of acronyms (SAML -> sam-el, KV cache -> K-V cash), some filler words and interrupted sentences retained.

title: PrivateNet Connectivity & Runtime Tuning Deep Dive - Nebula Health
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2027-05-14
Start: 15:30 UTC
Duration: 56 minutes
Attendees: Alex Chen (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Marcus Li (Redwood SE), Jordan Reyes (CTO, Nebula Health), Leah Patel (Infra), Omar Ruiz (Security), Sasha Kim (ML)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, imperfect):
- Discussed PrivateNet (customer's private VPC) connectivity patterns to Redwood Private control plane and inference workers.
- Key topics: PrivateLink vs NAT egress, hairpin DNS/resolver behavior, MTU and jumbo frames, KV-cache latency and eviction, batching tuning for 512-1k token workloads, int8 quantization trade-offs.
- Next steps: share diagrams, run KV latency trace, schedule pilot.

Transcript:
[00:00] Alex: Hey everyone, thanks for hopping on, appreciate you taking the time. Quick roll call, uh please say your name and role.
[00:10] Jordan: Jordan Reyes, CTO. We're excited to get this sorted, latency's been a blocker for our triage flows.
[00:16] Leah: Leah Patel, infrastructure, I run our VPC and peering stuff.
[00:20] Omar: Omar Ruiz, security lead, hi.
[00:22] Sasha: Sasha Kim, ML engineer, hey all.
[00:24] Alex: Great. Priya and Marcus from engineering on the call, we'll lead the technical deep dive.
[00:30] Priya: Hi hi, quick agenda — network diagram review, runtime KV-cache and batching, quantization impacts, failure scenarios for private egress. We'll sprinkle in best-practices.
[00:40] Jordan: Sounds good. We sent our rough network topology in advance — there are two private subnets for inference traffic, transit gateway into our security VPC, and a central NAT gateway per AZ.
[00:50] Leah: Correct. We currently hairpin outgoing calls through our NAT, so egress to Redwood's control plane goes via NAT then to PrivateLink endpoints we attempted last week. We saw occasional DNS timeouts.
[01:05] Marcus: Mm-hm. Two quick flags: when you run hairpin NAT plus PrivateLink, DNS resolution sometimes returns private IPs that then require cross-subnet routing; depending on your resolver and timeout settings that causes 50-200ms additional tail latency.
[01:20] Sasha: That's the problem — our request 95p latency spikes when context windows are moderate (512-700 tokens).
[01:28] Priya: Ok let's dig KV-cache first. Our runtime uses an in-memory KV cache per GPU worker; cache hits are local but cache lookups require a small RPC to the worker process. In Private deployments we recommend co-locating the inference clients in the same subnet as workers to avoid cross-AZ latency.
[01:45] Leah: We're trying to keep inference clients in separate subnet for security zoning — is there a recommended subnet layout?
[01:52] Priya: We'd suggest a dedicated inference subnet per AZ with security groups allowing intra-subnet traffic on the Redis-like KV port (it's not Redis but similar pattern). That removes hairpin through NAT for hot-path requests.
[02:05] Jordan: Would that force a lot of peering rules? We have strict NACLs.
[02:10] Marcus: Minimal rules — just allow the Redwood-secured ports between the inference app and the worker nodes. Use RBAC and mutual TLS at the app layer so network rules can be narrow.
[02:20] Omar: On mTLS — we need the HSM/KMS integration notes. Are keys ever in plaintext on the node?
[02:26] Priya: No, keys are managed via your KMS; Redwood's private agent negotiates keys via EKM/HSM over a control channel. We only hold ephemeral session keys in memory. We'll share the detailed KMS flow doc.
[02:40] Leah: Good to know. Back to DNS: our resolver is cross-account inbound resolver in central VPC; sometimes the TTL for PrivateLink endpoints is low and causes flapping. Any mitigations?
[02:52] Marcus: Two options: pin endpoints via interface endpoint IPs in route table (static) or run a local caching resolver in each inference subnet. We prefer local caching resolver — reduces DNS chattiness and helps with TTL jitter.
[03:05] Sasha: If we use local caching, will that break certificate validation for PrivateLink?
[03:10] Priya: Not necessarily; cert validation uses CN/SAN and SNI. As long as the resolver returns the correct private IP for the endpoint and SNI is used in TLS, certs validate. Avoid swapping the host header.
[03:22] Jordan: MTU — we saw fragmentation when we enabled jumbo frames in our internal network; packets between a worker and upstream control plane got dropped. Any recommended MTU?
[03:30] Marcus: Default 1500 works most reliably. If you enable jumbo frames you need end-to-end path MTU awareness including the PrivateLink ENIs and transit gateway. Mismatches lead to ICMP blackholing which looks like sporadic latency.
[03:45] Leah: Ok we'll keep 1500 for now.
[03:50] Priya: Now batching and throughput: for your workload (average prompt 350 tokens, generation 400 tokens) we suggest a dynamic batching window tuned to 30-45ms tail to balance latency vs token-cost. That usually yields higher GPU utilization while keeping p95 under 250ms for generation-only tails.
[04:10] Sasha: We run lots of short chats plus longer summarization. How do we tune batching to not penalize short-chat latency?
[04:18] Priya: Use request-priority tagging — low-latency traffic gets smaller max batch sizes and stricter timeouts, background summarization can be low priority and allowed to wait for larger batches. Redwood's policy routing supports per-route batching.
[04:30] Jordan: Any recommendation on KV-cache eviction policy? We see cold starts for long sessions.
[04:36] Marcus: LRU with frequency-weighted bumping is what we recommend for your workload. Also persist long-session prefixes to a warm-store (local SSD-backed checkpoint) so the runtime can pre-warm KVs on session start.
[04:52] Sasha: That pre-warm idea — is it a separate API call?
[04:56] Priya: Yes, there's a prefetch API. We'll add a sample script for your sessionizer.
[05:02] [05:02] Speaker 1 (recording glitch): ... sorry audio cut for a second ...
[05:08] Leah: What about quantization? We've been measuring a small drop in rouge/bleu on int8 for certain prompts.
[05:15] Priya: Right — quantization trade-offs depend on model and eval. For generative quality, we usually recommend mixed-int8 (group-wise) for decoder-only models; run A/B against your eval prompts. We can help run a quick quality baseline against your holdout.
[05:30] Jordan: If we fallback from int8 to float16 on degradation, is that automatic?
[05:34] Marcus: You can configure an automatic fallback policy — if quality metrics dip below threshold, fallback to higher-precision variant or slower profile. Will increase cost but preserve output.
[05:45] Omar: On audit logs and retention — we need per-request metadata and identities for 2 years for compliance. Does Redwood store that in customer-owned S3?
[05:55] Alex: For Private deployments you control log sinks; Redwood can push structured audit logs to your S3/GCS with encryption and lifecycle policies. We'll include the schema.
[06:05] Sasha: Back to networking failure modes — what happens if NAT gateway in an AZ is unhealthy?
[06:10] Priya: If your egress relies on NAT, you'll see increased cross-AZ hop and possibly hairpin; Redwood's routing can prefer regional fallback workers but that adds latency. Best practice: avoid NAT on hot-path, use interface endpoints or VPC peering to inference subnets.
[06:25] Leah: For our minimal change pilot, can we keep NAT but add interface endpoints gradually?
[06:29] Marcus: Yes, start with a parallel path: configure interface endpoints in one AZ, update routing for select subnets, measure.
[06:36] Jordan: Let's talk numbers — Marcus can you give rough p95 improvement with interface endpoint + local caching vs NAT hairpin?
[06:42] Marcus: For similar customers we've seen p95 drop from ~480ms to ~230-260ms for medium-length requests because you eliminate DNS timeout and cross-AZ hairpin.
[06:52] Jordan: That's compelling.
[06:54] [07:00] Alex: A quick demo moment — Priya, can you show the KV-cache traces you mentioned?
[07:04] Priya: Sure, sharing screen. (screen share) You'll see a waterfall: local cache hits ~1-2ms, warm SSR pre-warm 8-12ms, cold fetch to prefetch store 60-120ms. The problematic spikes correlate with resolver retries.
[07:25] Leah: I see a cluster of 120ms spikes at 14:23:10, is that a DNS retry?
[07:29] Priya: Yep. Resolver TTL was 2 seconds, we saw retries and occasional NX time before fallback.
[07:36] Sasha: Any quick config we can change to mitigate right now?
[07:39] Marcus: Increase retry backoff and run local caching resolver with a positive cache of 30s for PrivateLink endpoints. Also set conservative DNS timeout on the client side (e.g., 300ms) to avoid long TCP fallback.
[07:52] Jordan: Okay. For the pilot, we'll provide subnet CIDRs and NAT config. Leah will also spin up a local caching resolver.
[07:58] Omar: Security note — we'll need the detailed mTLS cert rotation cadence and who has access to ephemeral keys.
[08:05] Alex: We'll attach the cert rotation doc; short answer: 7-day session keys, operator-managed rotation for long-term certs, and full audit trail is included.
[08:15] Priya: Final operational note: for large throughput tests, watch ephemeral connection limits and socket reuse on your app tier. We typically advise using HTTP/2 multiplexing to reduce connection overhead.
[08:30] Leah: We'll test with HTTP/2.
[08:34] Jordan: Great. Anything else?
[08:36] Sasha: One more — can Redwood's runtime push periodic KV-cache warm hints from session history?
[08:41] Marcus: Yes, there's a guidance API — you can push likely prefixes for pre-warm. We'll give you a script.
[08:48] Alex: OK let's recap actions and owners. We already covered them in the next-steps and action-items. We'll also schedule the pilot kickoff for week of May 24th.
[08:58] Jordan: Good meeting. Thanks all.

End notes: small transcription errors present around 05:02 marker (audio glitch). References shared: link to network diagram (uploaded to CRM), KV-cache trace CSV (shared via secure link), SOC2 summary (docs link).

title: CatalystOps platform transition exploratory
preview: 00:00:00 Ethan Moore: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — this is Ethan from Redwood, i'm the AE on the opportunity. Quick roll call?,00:00:12 Maya Chen: Hi, Maya here, CTO at CatalystOps. We pushed the calendar invite, thanks for doing this.,00:00:18 Luis Ramirez: Luis, Head of ML. I'm mainly focused on inference cost and parity vs our current outputs.,00:00:24 Priya Natarajan: Priya, SE. I'll dig into the perf and compatibility questions.,00:00:30 Tom Hines: Tom from procurement, i'll be looping in contracts later but wanted to listen today.,00:01:05 Ethan Moore: Cool. We tried to leave this as a discovery — understand your stack, constraints, success criteria, timeline. Can you give a brief picture of current infra?,00:01:20 Maya Chen: Sure. We have a hybrid setup. Customer-facing product calls OpenAI for most generation, internal tooling runs an on-prem vLLM cluster for cost-sensitive workloads. We're worried about vendor lock, cost spikes, and wanting consistent SLA across workloads.,00:01:50 Priya Natarajan: When you say vLLM cluster, is it the community vLLM or a managed variant? How many replicas, GPUs?,00:02:05 Luis Ramirez: It's community vLLM on 8xA10s right now, a few model variants — we also tried TGI on a staging pool. The main pain is managing multiple runtimes and model drift; outputs sometimes differ between OpenAI and our vLLM runs.,00:02:36 Ethan Moore: Got it. So parity between hosted OpenAI outputs and your vLLM outputs is a big driver. Are you evaluating moving everything to a single provider or keeping mix-and-match?,00:02:50 Maya Chen: Ideally one platform to simplify ops, but must match OpenAI quality for the customer-facing stuff. Also need private hosting option for our regulated clients in healthcare — VPC or on-prem is required for those accounts.,00:03:12 Priya Natarajan: On the private side, Redwood Private supports VPC and on-prem, keys can be customer-managed via KMS/HSM. For Dedicated we give reserved GPU pools and SLA — which sounds relevant if you need predictable throughput.,00:03:36 Anika Bose: Quick question — do you support bringing our existing model artifacts? We also have a small fine-tuned base on LLaMA derivatives and some adapter modules.,00:03:50 Priya Natarajan: Yes, we support custom model variants. For private deployments we can ingest containerized models and help with quantization. We also have guidance on mapping LLM inputs and KV cache management when moving from vLLM/TGI.,00:04:20 Luis Ramirez: The mapping guidance is important — we saw behavior differences around decoding and top-p between providers. We need an evaluation plan to measure regression on our prompt suite.,00:04:36 Ethan Moore: Totally. Our Optimize team typically helps craft an eval parity plan. We'll want your prompt test set and a couple of representative traffic profiles so we can run A/B parity tests across models and runtime configs.,00:05:00 Tom Hines: On the security side, SOC2 is a must. Also SSO/SAML, RBAC, and audit logs for client audits. Do you have those?,00:05:12 Cara Liu: Cara here, CSM. We do have SOC2 Type II, SAML SSO integrations, RBAC and comprehensive audit logs. We'll include the security pack in the follow-up email.,00:05:30 Maya Chen: Good. Also curious about data retention — we currently retain minimal telemetry but no PII, but some regulated clients require zero retention.,00:05:42 Priya Natarajan: We support configurable retention policies and can do no-store for certain routes + customer-managed keys for encryption at rest. For Private, data never leaves your VPC.,00:06:10 Luis Ramirez: Performance: our p95 latency target for interactive customer prompts is ~120-150ms with 1-2 tokens per request on average; cost per token is another axis. Can Redwood hit those latencies on Hosted vs Dedicated?,00:06:26 Priya Natarajan: It depends on model and batching. Hosted with optimized kernels can hit those numbers for smaller models; Dedicated gives predictable throughput and lower queueing variance. We can run a baseline using your sample traffic.,00:06:45 Ethan Moore: Also worth noting our smart routing can fall back to cheaper model variants when capacity is constrained — good for cost control but you can tie that to quality gates.,00:07:02 Anika Bose: How do you handle KV cache and prefix caching? Our vLLM setup does aggressive KV reuse which saves compute for long chat contexts.,00:07:14 Priya Natarajan: We have KV cache primitives and automatic prefix caching — plus suggestions for batching windows per route. We'll recommend cache policies tailored to your sequence length distribution.,00:07:36 Luis Ramirez: About quantization — we already quantize to int4 for some models to reduce footprint. How compatible are your runtimes with int4 quantized variants, and do you have fallback?,00:07:48 Priya Natarajan: We have quantization profiles and runtime paths optimized for int8 and int4. Some model combos may need slight accuracy tuning; we surface expected quality impact per profile.,00:08:10 Maya Chen: Pricing — we run a lot of embeddings and some heavy generation. Are there volume discounts or commit tiers for Dedicated?,00:08:20 Ethan Moore: Yes — Hosted is usage-based, Dedicated has reserved capacity with monthly/annual commits and predictable unit economics. We'll include sample pricing scenarios in the follow-up.,00:08:40 Tom Hines: For procurement, we need a ballpark headcount estimate for support and SLAs. Do you offer an enterprise support add-on?,00:08:50 Cara Liu: We have enterprise support tiers, faster SLAs and a designated tech contact for pilots. We'll outline options in the packet.,00:09:20 Luis Ramirez: On parity testing: we want to run our existing prompt suite against our current OpenAI outputs and a Redwood-hosted/Dedicated run. How reproducible are the outputs?,00:09:34 Priya Natarajan: Determinism varies; we can set seeds and decoding params to increase reproducibility. For production, we recommend evaluation thresholds and monitoring for drift with automatic rollback.,00:09:50 Maya Chen: Another constraint: some clients require data residency in EU. Do you have region pins?,00:09:58 Ethan Moore: Yes — Redwood has region-based routing and private control planes for EU regions; we can map your accounts to EU-hosted instances or do Private in EU.,00:10:15 Anika Bose: About model onboarding — we have a custom adapter that layers on a base model. Will Redwood help validate the adapter during the port?,00:10:26 Priya Natarajan: We can validate adapter compatibility, recommend quantization steps, and run small canary rollouts. We also support per-customer rollout policies and automatic fallback to a baseline model.,00:10:50 Luis Ramirez: What about monitoring and regressions? Our PMs want alerts if generation quality drops beyond a threshold.,00:10:58 Cara Liu: Console includes quality monitoring and regression alerts tied to prompt sets; we can wire your prompt suite into the monitoring so you get alerted on thresholds.,00:11:20 Maya Chen: Ok, good. Timeline: we're planning a phased migration — pilot in Q2, broader production by Q4 if things look solid. Is that realistic?,00:11:30 Ethan Moore: Absolutely feasible. A 2-week pilot to validate parity + a 6-8 week ramp to prod for Dedicated or Private typically lines up with that timeline.,00:11:45 Tom Hines: Contractual notes: we'd need liability language for regulated workloads and SOC2 evidence. Also, can you support customer-owned keys for encryption?,00:11:58 Cara Liu: Yes to both; we'll include the standard enterprise language and KMS integration notes in the package.,00:12:22 Luis Ramirez: Are there customers who have done a similar port from OpenAI + in-house vLLM to Redwood? Any reference architecture?,00:12:31 Ethan Moore: We have a few anonymized case studies and a reference runbook — for example a fintech customer moved to Dedicated and used canary rollouts. We'll share extracts relevant to hybrid migrations.,00:12:55 Anika Bose: Can you run a small benchmark against our vLLM-quantized vs Redwood runtime? We want to see throughput per GPU and p95 latency.,00:13:05 Priya Natarajan: Yes, we typically run benchmarks with your prompts and sample traffic. We'll coordinate env access or you can share logs and we'll run in our test harness.,00:13:28 Luis Ramirez: One small nit: our in-house tests with TGI had some tokenization differences causing misaligned prompts. How are tokenizer differences handled?,00:13:40 Priya Natarajan: Tokenization differences are common. We do tokenizer normalization and will map your tokenizer to the runtime's tokenization pipeline; sometimes slight prompt adjustments are needed.,00:14:05 Maya Chen: From a success criteria POV: parity within a small delta for customer-facing prompts, predictable cost for high-volume embedding workloads, and SOC2-compliant private hosting — that's our checklist.,00:14:20 Ethan Moore: Perfect, we will structure the pilot around those success criteria.,00:14:45 Tom Hines: Procurement question — if we pick Dedicated, what does the commitment model look like? Minimums?,00:14:54 Ethan Moore: Commit levels depend on GPU count and throughput; minimal commitments exist but we'll show scenarios: small Dedicated pool vs burstable capacity. We'll propose a pilot-first approach to avoid heavy upfront commits.,00:15:20 Luis Ramirez: Any lock-in concerns? We want the flexibility to switch models or providers later.,00:15:28 Priya Natarajan: We design for portability: model versioning, exportable configs, and policies for rollbacks. If you're using standard model artifacts, you can redeploy elsewhere with minimal changes.,00:15:55 Maya Chen: Do you have tooling to automate canary rollouts and A/B testing so product can measure impact?,00:16:03 Priya Natarajan: Yes, rollouts, A/B, and automatic fallback policies are in the console. We can demo the console in the next session.,00:16:22 Luis Ramirez: On cost per embedding: we batch-embed large docs at night to save cost. Any batching suggestions?,00:16:30 Priya Natarajan: We provide batching/caching recommendations, and Optimize can suggest quantization and batching windows tuned to your workload.,00:16:55 Maya Chen: Are there any gotchas we should be aware of when moving from OpenAI models to open models on Redwood?,00:17:05 Ethan Moore: The common ones are prompt drift, tokenization, and decoding parameter mismatches. Also, some instruction-following behaviors differ; that's why we do eval parity and threshold-based rollouts.,00:17:30 Anika Bose: For the pilot, what environment access do you need? Can we give logs only or do you need API keys to our staging vLLM?,00:17:40 Priya Natarajan: Logs and sample prompts are a good start; for deeper perf benchmarks we typically ask for a staging API endpoint or a sanitized dataset. We'll be flexible with what you can share.,00:18:05 Luis Ramirez: Any automation to convert our current call patterns and rate limits into Redwood route configs? We have dozens of routes with different SLOs.,00:18:16 Priya Natarajan: We can help map route configs and suggest batching windows and autoscaling policies per route. Our onboarding runbook includes a route mapping template.,00:18:40 Ethan Moore: Quick pause — any red flags from CatalystOps side?,00:18:45 Maya Chen: Nothing immediate, maybe speed of integration for regulated clients, but you covered that with Private and KMS.,00:18:57 Tom Hines: For contracts, how long to finalize enterprise terms?,00:19:02 Ethan Moore: Typical timelines are 2-4 weeks for pilot legal review, longer for full enterprise deals — we'll coordinate with your legal team.,00:19:20 Luis Ramirez: One more technical: how do you handle multi-model routing for ensemble outputs? We sometimes run a reranker pipeline across two models.,00:19:32 Priya Natarajan: We support multi-model pipelines and reranking; you can pin different stages to different model variants and have deterministic orchestration in the runtime.,00:19:55 Maya Chen: That's useful. Okay, let's talk concrete next steps so we leave with momentum.,00:20:03 Ethan Moore: Agreed. Proposed next steps: send security + pricing pack, schedule tech deep-dive for week of 4/12, customer to send prompt set and infra inventory, Redwood to draft 2-week pilot plan. Sound right?,00:20:20 Maya Chen: Sounds right. We'll aim to send prompt set and sanitized logs by 4/9.,00:20:27 Tom Hines: Procurement will review pricing scenarios once received; expecting a follow-up by end of week.,00:20:40 Cara Liu: We'll get the materials out — SOC2, SAML, RBAC docs, pilot proposal, and sample Dedicated pricing scenarios. Ethan will own the email.,00:21:05 Luis Ramirez: Quick note — can the pilot include a p95 SLA target measurement and a small embedding throughput test?,00:21:14 Priya Natarajan: Yes, pilot will measure p95 and embedding throughput per your sample workload. We'll include the benchmark matrix in the perf plan.,00:21:40 Maya Chen: Ok thanks. One final question: who will be our single point of contact for the pilot?,00:21:48 Ethan Moore: I'll be primary AE; Priya will lead technical execution; Cara will coordinate contracts and support.,00:22:05 Maya Chen: Great, thank you all. We'll watch for the follow-up email and security pack.,00:22:12 Ethan Moore: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up with materials by 4/8 and pencil in the deep-dive week of 4/12. We'll wrap now.,00:22:20 [call ends — remaining minutes used for informal side conversation not captured clearly by transcript],00:30:00 [auto-generated note] Transcription shows occasional mis-attributions and some technical terms spelled phonetically (eg 'Dedicaeted' or 'v-L-M' for vLLM), please refer to attached security pack for formal specs.,00:35:00 [follow-up recorder] Ethan Moore: Action items confirmed in chat, leaving off with pilot plan draft and security docs to be sent.
text: 00:00:00 Ethan Moore: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — this is Ethan from Redwood, i'm the AE on the opportunity. Quick roll call?,00:00:12 Maya Chen: Hi, Maya here, CTO at CatalystOps. We pushed the calendar invite, thanks for doing this.,00:00:18 Luis Ramirez: Luis, Head of ML. I'm mainly focused on inference cost and parity vs our current outputs.,00:00:24 Priya Natarajan: Priya, SE. I'll dig into the perf and compatibility questions.,00:00:30 Tom Hines: Tom from procurement, i'll be looping in contracts later but wanted to listen today.,00:01:05 Ethan Moore: Cool. We tried to leave this as a discovery — understand your stack, constraints, success criteria, timeline. Can you give a brief picture of current infra?,00:01:20 Maya Chen: Sure. We have a hybrid setup. Customer-facing product calls OpenAI for most generation, internal tooling runs an on-prem vLLM cluster for cost-sensitive workloads. We're worried about vendor lock, cost spikes, and wanting consistent SLA across workloads.,00:01:50 Priya Natarajan: When you say vLLM cluster, is it the community vLLM or a managed variant? How many replicas, GPUs?,00:02:05 Luis Ramirez: It's community vLLM on 8xA10s right now, a few model variants — we also tried TGI on a staging pool. The main pain is managing multiple runtimes and model drift; outputs sometimes differ between OpenAI and our vLLM runs.,00:02:36 Ethan Moore: Got it. So parity between hosted OpenAI outputs and your vLLM outputs is a big driver. Are you evaluating moving everything to a single provider or keeping mix-and-match?,00:02:50 Maya Chen: Ideally one platform to simplify ops, but must match OpenAI quality for the customer-facing stuff. Also need private hosting option for our regulated clients in healthcare — VPC or on-prem is required for those accounts.,00:03:12 Priya Natarajan: On the private side, Redwood Private supports VPC and on-prem, keys can be customer-managed via KMS/HSM. For Dedicated we give reserved GPU pools and SLA — which sounds relevant if you need predictable throughput.,00:03:36 Anika Bose: Quick question — do you support bringing our existing model artifacts? We also have a small fine-tuned base on LLaMA derivatives and some adapter modules.,00:03:50 Priya Natarajan: Yes, we support custom model variants. For private deployments we can ingest containerized models and help with quantization. We also have guidance on mapping LLM inputs and KV cache management when moving from vLLM/TGI.,00:04:20 Luis Ramirez: The mapping guidance is important — we saw behavior differences around decoding and top-p between providers. We need an evaluation plan to measure regression on our prompt suite.,00:04:36 Ethan Moore: Totally. Our Optimize team typically helps craft an eval parity plan. We'll want your prompt test set and a couple of representative traffic profiles so we can run A/B parity tests across models and runtime configs.,00:05:00 Tom Hines: On the security side, SOC2 is a must. Also SSO/SAML, RBAC, and audit logs for client audits. Do you have those?,00:05:12 Cara Liu: Cara here, CSM. We do have SOC2 Type II, SAML SSO integrations, RBAC and comprehensive audit logs. We'll include the security pack in the follow-up email.,00:05:30 Maya Chen: Good. Also curious about data retention — we currently retain minimal telemetry but no PII, but some regulated clients require zero retention.,00:05:42 Priya Natarajan: We support configurable retention policies and can do no-store for certain routes + customer-managed keys for encryption at rest. For Private, data never leaves your VPC.,00:06:10 Luis Ramirez: Performance: our p95 latency target for interactive customer prompts is ~120-150ms with 1-2 tokens per request on average; cost per token is another axis. Can Redwood hit those latencies on Hosted vs Dedicated?,00:06:26 Priya Natarajan: It depends on model and batching. Hosted with optimized kernels can hit those numbers for smaller models; Dedicated gives predictable throughput and lower queueing variance. We can run a baseline using your sample traffic.,00:06:45 Ethan Moore: Also worth noting our smart routing can fall back to cheaper model variants when capacity is constrained — good for cost control but you can tie that to quality gates.,00:07:02 Anika Bose: How do you handle KV cache and prefix caching? Our vLLM setup does aggressive KV reuse which saves compute for long chat contexts.,00:07:14 Priya Natarajan: We have KV cache primitives and automatic prefix caching — plus suggestions for batching windows per route. We'll recommend cache policies tailored to your sequence length distribution.,00:07:36 Luis Ramirez: About quantization — we already quantize to int4 for some models to reduce footprint. How compatible are your runtimes with int4 quantized variants, and do you have fallback?,00:07:48 Priya Natarajan: We have quantization profiles and runtime paths optimized for int8 and int4. Some model combos may need slight accuracy tuning; we surface expected quality impact per profile.,00:08:10 Maya Chen: Pricing — we run a lot of embeddings and some heavy generation. Are there volume discounts or commit tiers for Dedicated?,00:08:20 Ethan Moore: Yes — Hosted is usage-based, Dedicated has reserved capacity with monthly/annual commits and predictable unit economics. We'll include sample pricing scenarios in the follow-up.,00:08:40 Tom Hines: For procurement, we need a ballpark headcount estimate for support and SLAs. Do you offer an enterprise support add-on?,00:08:50 Cara Liu: We have enterprise support tiers, faster SLAs and a designated tech contact for pilots. We'll outline options in the packet.,00:09:20 Luis Ramirez: On parity testing: we want to run our existing prompt suite against our current OpenAI outputs and a Redwood-hosted/Dedicated run. How reproducible are the outputs?,00:09:34 Priya Natarajan: Determinism varies; we can set seeds and decoding params to increase reproducibility. For production, we recommend evaluation thresholds and monitoring for drift with automatic rollback.,00:09:50 Maya Chen: Another constraint: some clients require data residency in EU. Do you have region pins?,00:09:58 Ethan Moore: Yes — Redwood has region-based routing and private control planes for EU regions; we can map your accounts to EU-hosted instances or do Private in EU.,00:10:15 Anika Bose: About model onboarding — we have a custom adapter that layers on a base model. Will Redwood help validate the adapter during the port?,00:10:26 Priya Natarajan: We can validate adapter compatibility, recommend quantization steps, and run small canary rollouts. We also support per-customer rollout policies and automatic fallback to a baseline model.,00:10:50 Luis Ramirez: What about monitoring and regressions? Our PMs want alerts if generation quality drops beyond a threshold.,00:10:58 Cara Liu: Console includes quality monitoring and regression alerts tied to prompt sets; we can wire your prompt suite into the monitoring so you get alerted on thresholds.,00:11:20 Maya Chen: Ok, good. Timeline: we're planning a phased migration — pilot in Q2, broader production by Q4 if things look solid. Is that realistic?,00:11:30 Ethan Moore: Absolutely feasible. A 2-week pilot to validate parity + a 6-8 week ramp to prod for Dedicated or Private typically lines up with that timeline.,00:11:45 Tom Hines: Contractual notes: we'd need liability language for regulated workloads and SOC2 evidence. Also, can you support customer-owned keys for encryption?,00:11:58 Cara Liu: Yes to both; we'll include the standard enterprise language and KMS integration notes in the package.,00:12:22 Luis Ramirez: Are there customers who have done a similar port from OpenAI + in-house vLLM to Redwood? Any reference architecture?,00:12:31 Ethan Moore: We have a few anonymized case studies and a reference runbook — for example a fintech customer moved to Dedicated and used canary rollouts. We'll share extracts relevant to hybrid migrations.,00:12:55 Anika Bose: Can you run a small benchmark against our vLLM-quantized vs Redwood runtime? We want to see throughput per GPU and p95 latency.,00:13:05 Priya Natarajan: Yes, we typically run benchmarks with your prompts and sample traffic. We'll coordinate env access or you can share logs and we'll run in our test harness.,00:13:28 Luis Ramirez: One small nit: our in-house tests with TGI had some tokenization differences causing misaligned prompts. How are tokenizer differences handled?,00:13:40 Priya Natarajan: Tokenization differences are common. We do tokenizer normalization and will map your tokenizer to the runtime's tokenization pipeline; sometimes slight prompt adjustments are needed.,00:14:05 Maya Chen: From a success criteria POV: parity within a small delta for customer-facing prompts, predictable cost for high-volume embedding workloads, and SOC2-compliant private hosting — that's our checklist.,00:14:20 Ethan Moore: Perfect, we will structure the pilot around those success criteria.,00:14:45 Tom Hines: Procurement question — if we pick Dedicated, what does the commitment model look like? Minimums?,00:14:54 Ethan Moore: Commit levels depend on GPU count and throughput; minimal commitments exist but we'll show scenarios: small Dedicated pool vs burstable capacity. We'll propose a pilot-first approach to avoid heavy upfront commits.,00:15:20 Luis Ramirez: Any lock-in concerns? We want the flexibility to switch models or providers later.,00:15:28 Priya Natarajan: We design for portability: model versioning, exportable configs, and policies for rollbacks. title: Archipelago Biotech — Model decision & deployment options
preview: Introductory discovery focused on model selection (open models), context-length needs, multilingual support, and deployment mode tradeoffs (Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private). Customer emphasized compliance (SOC2, KMS), latency SLOs for interactive UI, and cost sensitivity. Agreed next steps: share benchmark template, security pack, and schedule a dedicated latency/quantization deep-dive with infra team.

title: QuantaCare pilot benchmark contrast readout
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2027-02-09 14:30 UTC
Duration: 46 minutes
Attendees: Jordan (Redwood AE), Priya (Redwood SE), Alex (Redwood SE), Luis (QuantaCare CTO), Hannah (ML Eng), Omar (Infra), Marta (Product)

Summary:
Quick readout of week-3 POC benchmarks comparing Redwood Dedicated path vs their incumbent (IncumbentAI). Focus on tail latency, throughput under burst, and routing/fallback behaviour. Tuning notes on quantization, KV cache, and autoscale thresholds. Action items and next steps captured at end.

Topics:
- Baseline vs Redwood results
- 95/99th pctile latency differences
- Throughput at concurrent sessions and batching behavior
- KV/prefix cache impact and cache hit rates
- VPC/KMS and security checklist
- Next steps and delivery dates

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan (AE): Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call, I think we have full house from QuantaCare and Redwood. Today's goal is the contrast readout vs the incumbent and capture tuning recs.
[00:18] Luis (QuantaCare): yep thanks, excited to see the numbers. We ran your microbench earlier, saw some promising numbers but want to understand the tail.
[00:28] Priya (SE): cool, I'll share my screen after Jordan does the high-level. We have three runs: baseline (your IncumbentAI config), Redwood hosted-ish sim and Redwood Dedicated with routing. Note: some labels in the run names got munged by the test harness so sorry if names look off.
[00:46] Jordan (AE): high-level summary: average token latency is similar but 95/99th tails are where we see "wins" for Redwood Dedicated when KV/prefix caching is enabled and when batch windows are tuned to your request size distributions.
[01:06] Hannah (ML Eng): can you quantify "wins"? our business cares about 99th under load spikes.
[01:12] Priya (SE): yes. Run A (incumbent) 99th token latency ~ 420ms at 1k qps with 4k context. Run B (Redwood Dedicated, default) 99th ~ 360ms. Run C (Redwood Dedicated + KV cache) 99th ~ 230ms. Numbers are approximate; Jordan will send raw CSV.
[01:36] Omar (Infra): those KV cache numbers seem dramatic — what's the eviction policy? our workload has many session prefixes and long tails.
[01:43] Alex (Solutions Eng): Good call — eviction is LRU with sticky prefetch for hot prefixes. In our test we pinned a 30% hot-prefix distribution which produced ~78% cache hit rate. In your production distrib it might be lower, Hannah?
[02:05] Hannah: our hot prefix is about 12% in logs, not 30. We also have many short completions.
[02:12] Priya: with 12% we still saw a 40-50% hit and 99th dropped to ~300ms vs 420ms incumbent. The key is making sure the prefix window and cache allocation are tuned.
[02:26] Marta (Product): any cost delta? we can't blow the budget chasing a few ms.
[02:30] Jordan: great question. Cost per token on Dedicated is a bit lower at scale because of batching and kernel optimizations; the KV cache helps because it reduces token generation. We'll include per-token cost extrapolations in the sheet. Incumbent had higher per-token egress in our runs.
[02:52] Luis: we also saw higher steady-state throughput with the incumbent in one of our runs — wonder if you're optimizing for different batch windows.
[03:02] Priya: yep, there are a few knobs: batch max size, batch delay (ms), and continuous batching policy. In the baseline we matched your incumbent's batch delay of 15ms which favors throughput but hurts tail. When we shorten to 5ms and enable async small-batch fast-path, tail improves.
[03:23] Alex: also model quantization profile matters — we tested q4 and q8 variants; q8 gave slightly lower latency at cost of quality in a couple edge cases. We flagged a few hallucination differences in the eval set (Hannah we've added notes).
[03:41] Hannah: can you name the model variant? the transcripts in the logs say "redwood-7b-v2-quant" but sometimes it's parsed as "redwood-seven-bee-times two" in the exports, so just want clarity.
[03:54] Priya: haha yep, it's redwood-7b-v2-q4 and q8 in the experiments. We'll include exact model hashes.
[04:02] Luis: regarding throughput — at burst 2k concurrent we saw a drop in success rate to 92% on the incumbent but Redwood stayed at 98% with direct warm pools. Is that the Dedicated reserved slots behavior?
[04:15] Jordan: yes, that's the reserved GPU slot + prewarmed KV cache behavior. We also ran a routing fallback in which Redwood sent overflow to a slightly cheaper quantized variant; the fail rate is visible in the routing logs and was minimal. We'll show traces.
[04:34] Omar: about routing — how deterministic is the fallback? we need guaranteed SLA if a critical flow hits degraded model.
[04:41] Alex: you can set deterministic thresholds and policy: e.g., if 95th > 400ms, route to fallback model in same region; if region capacity saturated, route to remote region with latency cap. We ship a routing playbook.
[04:58] Luis: region routing increases P99 — how did you simulate cross-region?
[05:03] Priya: we ran a controlled test with a secondary region in EU-west, measuring added hop ~60ms average. For critical flows we recommend only intra-region fallback unless your SLA tolerates cross-region delta.
[05:18] Marta: can we get the playbook?
[05:21] Jordan: yes Alex will share draft.
[05:25] Hannah: back to numbers — any regression concerns on quality when using q4? we saw a 2% BLEU-ish drop on the eval but it's not consistent.
[05:34] Alex: the drop was mostly on code-gen style prompts; on natural language completions the delta was tiny. We can pin a mixed strategy: q8 for code and q4 for short text to save cost while preserving quality.
[05:48] Omar: security — SOC2 and KMS integration. We need to know if Redwood Dedicated in our VPC supports our HSM provider.
[05:56] Priya: Redwood Private will support customer-managed KMS/HSM and audit logs; for Dedicated we can integrate with external KMS via key-unwrap patterns. I'll loop in security but initial answer is yes, we support external KMS providers (though integration runbook differs).
[06:16] Luis: do you have proof artifacts for SOC2, ISO?
[06:20] Jordan: yes we'll send security pack (SOC2 Type II report summary, pen-test summary, encryption at rest/in transit details).
[06:29] Hannah: also need RBAC and SSO for token-level audit.
[06:33] Alex: RBAC with SAML SSO is standard in our console and we surface token-level cost/latency. We'll include a screenshot of the tracing UI with token breakdown.
[06:48] Jordan: quick note on the test harness differences — your incumbent runs included a bunch of long-context sessions which inflate latency; we rebalanced to match your prod distribution but if you can send the canonical prompt set, we'll re-run to be exact.
[07:02] Hannah: will send it after this meeting.
[07:06] Priya: great. On today's run logs we attached a few oddities — occasional misattributed speaker in the transcripts but I mean in the inference traces, there were a couple of 2.4s spikes — we traced those to cold KV misses during concurrent eviction storms.
[07:24] Omar: eviction storms — can you avoid them with pinning?
[07:27] Alex: yes you can pin prefixes and reserve a small sticky-cache region for them which trades memory for tail stability. We can propose cache sizing based on your median prefix diversity.
[07:38] Luis: what's memory cost?
[07:40] Priya: in our runs each pinned prefix average ~12MB; we pinned ~200 prefixes and it was a couple of GBs per GPU. Numbers will be in sheet.
[07:52] Marta: curious about observability — can we get traces per request including kernel selection and batch windows?
[07:58] Alex: yes, our tracing includes model variant, kernel paths, batch size used, and KV cache hit/miss. We'll give you a link to sample trace artifacts.
[08:10] Jordan: any open risks from QuantaCare?
[08:13] Luis: timeline — we need to decide vendor in 3 weeks; security review in 2. Also want hard numbers vs incumbent running our prompt set.
[08:21] Jordan: understood, we'll prioritize re-run with your exact prompt set and a dedicated routing stress test.
[08:27] Hannah: on the stress test — can you simulate our burst pattern (short spikes, long tails)? we shared the SRE script last week.
[08:33] Priya: yes we'll use your SRE script and overlay 3 different burst scenarios. We'll produce a dashboard with 50/95/99 and success rates.
[08:43] Omar: network — VPC peering latency is critical. We'll share peering endpoints.
[08:47] Jordan: perfect.
[08:50] Marta: any internal blockers on Redwood side (dedicated capacity)?
[08:54] Jordan: we have available slots for a 2-week pilot extension; if you need reserved monthly capacity that's a different procurement thread but doable.
[09:04] Luis: cost modeling question — if we convert hot flows to cached prefixes and move others to cheaper quantized routing what's the expected % cost savings?
[09:10] Alex: on our sample workload, mixing q4 fallback for noncritical prompts and pinning hot prefixes produced ~28% token cost reduction vs incumbent while improving 99th latency 15-30% depending on scenario. We'll include sensitivity analysis.
[09:29] Hannah: nice. Please include worst-case numbers for bursts and tail.
[09:34] Priya: will do.
[09:36] Jordan: ok let's recap next steps — Jordan sends sheet, Priya/Alex run routing stress tests and share logs, QuantaCare sends canonical prompts and peering info, security timestamps set for feb 15. Any other asks?
[09:50] Luis: please also add an explicit row comparing model hashes and quant profiles.
[09:54] Jordan: noted.
[09:58] Marta: can we get a short 20-min follow-up after you publish the runs to walk through the playbook?
[10:03] Jordan: absolutely — propose feb 17 15:00 UTC, will send invite.
[10:09] Hannah: thanks everyone.
[10:11] Jordan: thanks all, we'll send artifacts.

End of transcript.

References and links mentioned:
- Benchmark CSV (to be shared)
- Routing playbook draft (Alex to share)
- Security pack (SOC2/ISO summary, pen-test)
- SRE burst script (from QuantaCare)

Notes on transcription: some model names and acronyms were imperfectly exported (e.g., "redwood-7b-v2-q4" appears as "redwood-seven-bee", SOC2 sometimes parsed as "S O C two"). Speaker attribution in a handful of short snippets may be off.
