[01:43] Alex (Solutions Eng): Good call — eviction is LRU with sticky prefetch for hot prefixes. In our test we pinned a 30% hot-prefix distribution which produced ~78% cache hit rate. In your production distrib it might be lower, Hannah?
[02:05] Hannah: our hot prefix is about 12% in logs, not 30. We also have many short completions.
[02:12] Priya: with 12% we still saw a 40-50% hit and 99th dropped to ~300ms vs 420ms incumbent. The key is making sure the prefix window and cache allocation are tuned.
[02:26] Marta (Product): any cost delta? we can't blow the budget chasing a few ms.
[02:30] Jordan: great question. Cost per token on Dedicated is a bit lower at scale because of batching and kernel optimizations; the KV cache helps because it reduces token generation. We'll include per-token cost extrapolations in the sheet. Incumbent had higher per-token egress in our runs.
[02:52] Luis: we also saw higher steady-state throughput with the incumbent in one of our runs — wonder if you're optimizing for different batch windows.
[03:02] Priya: yep, there are a few knobs: batch max size, batch delay (ms), and continuous batching policy. In the baseline we matched your incumbent's batch delay of 15ms which favors throughput but hurts tail. When we shorten to 5ms and enable async small-batch fast-path, tail improves.
[03:23] Alex: also model quantization profile matters — we tested q4 and q8 variants; q8 gave slightly lower latency at cost of quality in a couple edge cases. We flagged a few hallucination differences in the eval set (Hannah we've added notes).
[03:41] Hannah: can you name the model variant? the transcripts in the logs say "redwood-7b-v2-quant" but sometimes it's parsed as "redwood-seven-bee-times two" in the exports, so just want clarity.
[03:54] Priya: haha yep, it's redwood-7b-v2-q4 and q8 in the experiments. We'll include exact model hashes.
[04:02] Luis: regarding throughput — at burst 2k concurrent we saw a drop in success rate to 92% on the incumbent but Redwood stayed at 98% with direct warm pools. Is that the Dedicated reserved slots behavior?
[04:15] Jordan: yes, that's the reserved GPU slot + prewarmed KV cache behavior. We also ran a routing fallback in which Redwood sent overflow to a slightly cheaper quantized variant; the fail rate is visible in the routing logs and was minimal. We'll show traces.
[04:34] Omar: about routing — how deterministic is the fallback? we need guaranteed SLA if a critical flow hits degraded model.
[04:41] Alex: you can set deterministic thresholds and policy: e.g., if 95th > 400ms, route to fallback model in same region; if region capacity saturated, route to remote region with latency cap. We ship a routing playbook.
[04:58] Luis: region routing increases P99 — how did you simulate cross-region?
[05:03] Priya: we ran a controlled test with a secondary region in EU-west, measuring added hop ~60ms average. For critical flows we recommend only intra-region fallback unless your SLA tolerates cross-region delta.
[05:18] Marta: can we get the playbook?
[05:21] Jordan: yes Alex will share draft.
[05:25] Hannah: back to numbers — any regression concerns on quality when using q4? we saw a 2% BLEU-ish drop on the eval but it's not consistent.
[05:34] Alex: the drop was mostly on code-gen style prompts; on natural language completions the delta was tiny. We can pin a mixed strategy: q8 for code and q4 for short text to save cost while preserving quality.
[05:48] Omar: security — SOC2 and KMS integration. We need to know if Redwood Dedicated in our VPC supports our HSM provider.
[05:56] Priya: Redwood Private will support customer-managed KMS/HSM and audit logs; for Dedicated we can integrate with external KMS via key-unwrap patterns. I'll loop in security but initial answer is yes, we support external KMS providers (though integration runbook differs).
[06:16] Luis: do you have proof artifacts for SOC2, ISO?
[06:20] Jordan: yes we'll send security pack (SOC2 Type II report summary, pen-test summary, encryption at rest/in transit details).
[06:29] Hannah: also need RBAC and SSO for token-level audit.
[06:33] Alex: RBAC with SAML SSO is standard in our console and we surface token-level cost/latency. We'll include a screenshot of the tracing UI with token breakdown.
[06:48] Jordan: quick note on the test harness differences — your incumbent runs included a bunch of long-context sessions which inflate latency; we rebalanced to match your prod distribution but if you can send the canonical prompt set, we'll re-run to be exact.
[07:02] Hannah: will send it after this meeting.
[07:06] Priya: great. On today's run logs we attached a few oddities — occasional misattributed speaker in the transcripts but I mean in the inference traces, there were a couple of 2.4s spikes — we traced those to cold KV misses during concurrent eviction storms.
[07:24] Omar: eviction storms — can you avoid them with pinning?
[07:27] Alex: yes you can pin prefixes and reserve a small sticky-cache region for them which trades memory for tail stability. We can propose cache sizing based on your median prefix diversity.
[07:38] Luis: what's memory cost?
[07:40] Priya: in our runs each pinned prefix average ~12MB; we pinned ~200 prefixes and it was a couple of GBs per GPU. Numbers will be in sheet.
[07:52] Marta: curious about observability — can we get traces per request including kernel selection and batch windows?
[07:58] Alex: yes, our tracing includes model variant, kernel paths, batch size used, and KV cache hit/miss. We'll give you a link to sample trace artifacts.
[08:10] Jordan: any open risks from QuantaCare?
[08:13] Luis: timeline — we need to decide vendor in 3 weeks; security review in 2. Also want hard numbers vs incumbent running our prompt set.
[08:21] Jordan: understood, we'll prioritize re-run with your exact prompt set and a dedicated routing stress test.
[08:27] Hannah: on the stress test — can you simulate our burst pattern (short spikes, long tails)? we shared the SRE script last week.
[08:33] Priya: yes we'll use your SRE script and overlay 3 different burst scenarios. We'll produce a dashboard with 50/95/99 and success rates.
[08:43] Omar: network — VPC peering latency is critical. We'll share peering endpoints.
[08:47] Jordan: perfect.
[08:50] Marta: any internal blockers on Redwood side (dedicated capacity)?
[08:54] Jordan: we have available slots for a 2-week pilot extension; if you need reserved monthly capacity that's a different procurement thread but doable.
[09:04] Luis: cost modeling question — if we convert hot flows to cached prefixes and move others to cheaper quantized routing what's the expected % cost savings?
[09:10] Alex: on our sample workload, mixing q4 fallback for noncritical prompts and pinning hot prefixes produced ~28% token cost reduction vs incumbent while improving 99th latency 15-30% depending on scenario. We'll include sensitivity analysis.
[09:29] Hannah: nice. Please include worst-case numbers for bursts and tail.
[09:34] Priya: will do.
[09:36] Jordan: ok let's recap next steps — Jordan sends sheet, Priya/Alex run routing stress tests and share logs, QuantaCare sends canonical prompts and peering info, security timestamps set for feb 15. Any other asks?
[09:50] Luis: please also add an explicit row comparing model hashes and quant profiles.
[09:54] Jordan: noted.
[09:58] Marta: can we get a short 20-min follow-up after you publish the runs to walk through the playbook?
[10:03] Jordan: absolutely — propose feb 17 15:00 UTC, will send invite.
[10:09] Hannah: thanks everyone.
[10:11] Jordan: thanks all, we'll send artifacts.

End of transcript.

References and links mentioned:
- Benchmark CSV (to be shared)
- Routing playbook draft (Alex to share)
- Security pack (SOC2/ISO summary, pen-test)
- SRE burst script (from QuantaCare)

Notes on transcription: some model names and acronyms were imperfectly exported (e.g., "redwood-7b-v2-q4" appears as "redwood-seven-bee", SOC2 sometimes parsed as "S O C two"). Speaker attribution in a handful of short snippets may be off.

title: Stability & stress test: kernel-swap behavior TRT-LLM vs vLLM on private deploy
preview:
Meeting header:
Date/Time: 2026-12-10 15:00 UTC | Duration: 62m | Zoom call
Attendees: Alex Morgan (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Marcus Lee (Helix), Sana Patel (Helix SRE), Diego Ramos (Helix CTO)
Recording: zoom.us/rec/abcdefg (internal)

Summary: technical deep dive into kernel-swapping and cudagraph replay instability observed on Helix on-prem private deployment. Focus on TRT-LLM vs vLLM behavior, KV cache persistence, batching policy interactions and fallback heuristics.

[00:00] Alex: hey everyone, thanks for carving out time, quick roll call?
[00:07] Marcus: Marcus here, we hit some instability in our private cluster during long chat sessions, curious if it's TRT-LLM cudagraph replay or our NV link setup.
[00:14] Sana: Sana, SRE, here. we've got 8xA100 nodes with NV link, patched to 510 drivers, and we've seen intermittent out-of-memory errors and some latency spikes.
[00:22] Priya: hey hi, Priya from Redwood infra, I went through the trace Marcus sent, there are a few signatures that look like cudagraph replay mismatches and persistent K V cache evictions in the middle of large-batch sequences.
[00:34] Diego: Diego here, CTO. stepping back, we need to understand tradeoffs — TRT-LLM gives us lower p50 but seen more jitter; vLLM is stable but costs more. want to pick a path for our pilot.

[00:48] Alex: quick framing — TRT-LLM uses TensorRT kernels + cudagraph replay for hot paths; that gives great throughput but can be brittle if sequence length distribution changes or if there's eviction of cached KV states. vLLM keeps a more general scheduler, less replay, more deterministic but slightly higher avg latency.
[01:05] Marcus: correct, our long-chat workload has very variable sequence lengths, lots of switching between short queries and long context expansions. We instrumented a 24h window and saw kernel swap events correlate with NV link traffic bursts.
[01:22] Priya: one important detail — cudagraphs are recorded for specific execution paths (seq len, batch shape); when a record is replayed but the KV cache layout changed (evictions or shifted offsets), the GPU can hit protective checks or suboptimal kernels, producing slower kernels or OOMs. The runtime should fallback but sometimes fallback triggers late.
[01:40] Sana: yes, and our current fallback threshold is quite conservative, we only swap after a few failed replays which is why you're seeing spikes.

[02:01] Alex: couple of concrete suggestions: 1) warmup with representative sampled traces to prime cudagraphs, 2) enable prefix-aware recording (so the graph covers typical variations), 3) relax persistent KV eviction or increase K V cache size to avoid mid-dialog evictions.
[02:18] Marcus: Warmup sounds doable, but our on-prem memory is tight. can quantization help? we tried int8 profile and saw quality degradation on generative tasks.
[02:29] Priya: quantization is a tool — INT8 reduces memory but can change kernel selection too. TRT-LLM's int8 kernels may pick different execution paths; important to run quality evals. Also mixed precision FP16 + selective int8 for attention can be a middle ground.
[02:46] Diego: are we seeing deterministic reproducibility? i mean, if you rerun the same trace, do you get the same kernel-swap points?
[02:53] Priya: not always. small nondeterministic timing can change batching boundaries which changes the recorded graph. Also background GC/eviction events cause offsets to shift. We need a more stable batching policy; continuous batching is great for throughput but exacerbates this variability.

[03:18] Marcus: what's Redwood's fallback policy by default?
[03:21] Alex: we try cudagraph replay, then line up a soft fallback to non-replayed kernel with a short warmup period. There's also a more aggressive policy that disables cudagraphs for endpoints with very high sequence variability.
[03:36] Sana: could that be per-route? we'd like chat endpoints to be stable but analytics batch jobs to use cudagraphs.
[03:41] Priya: yes it's per-route — you can pin routing policy per model route. For Helix we'd recommend chat-route: conservative cudagraph policy + larger KV cache quota; analytical-route: aggressive cudagraph + batching.

[04:02] Diego: NVLink config — we saw cross-GPU memory copies spike after a kernel swap. Could that be the kernel change itself, or are we hitting PCIe fallback?
[04:10] Priya: depends. TensorRT kernels sometimes choose different memory layouts requiring extra cross-device synchronization. Also cudagraph replay may skip certain async memcpy optimizations and force synchronous copies if replay path differs. We should capture nvprof / NSIGHT traces around swap events.
[04:28] Marcus: we have some nvprof dumps. I'll upload them. they show sudden sync points that match the timestamp of the spikes.

[04:45] Alex: on the software side, recommended next steps: 1) Priya will run a repro with two scenarios: persistent KV cache enabled vs disabled, and with/without cudagraph replay. 2) collect cuda graf record IDs, kernel names, and time series of NCCL/ memcpy syncs.

[05:02] Priya: also want to test a deterministic warmup — replay sampled 30m of traffic 3x before opening to production to stabilize recorded graphs. That reduces cold-start graph variance.
[05:13] Sana: question — what about quantization + cudagraph? Does quantized cudagraph exacerbate instability?
[05:19] Priya: quantization can change kernel selection and memory layout; TRT-LLM's int8 kernels are often different code paths. If you change precision post-record, replay may mismatch. So record graphs using the target precision.

[05:36] Marcus: any config knobs for delaying fallback so we don't get OOM but also not degrade p50?
[05:41] Alex: yes — two knobs: a) early-detect policy that pre-validates graph compatibility on warmup, b) soft-fallback that serves from a warm non-cudagraph kernel that is already primed. Both reduce oscillation.
[05:56] Diego: cost question — if we disable cudagraphs for chat route, what's the expected cost hit?
[06:01] Priya: rough estimate — 8–15% higher per-token compute cost for smaller batch-size patterns; but much less variability so SLA could be better. We should run targeted econ tests.

[06:18] Sana: on the networking side, we might be able to isolate chat traffic to nodes with strong NVLink topology (full mesh) vs nodes with partial NVLink. That should reduce cross-node memcpy.
[06:27] Priya: good call. Also pinning model shards to NVLink-connected devices reduces NCCL traffic. That's a deployment-level config.

[06:42] Alex: open questions we captured: how often do graph-recorded shapes diverge in your workload? Marcus said about 12% of sessions have >3 lengths changes; we need the raw distribution.
[06:55] Marcus: I'll drop the session histogram into the shared folder.
[07:00] Priya: last operational note — ensure driver/ CUDA runtime matches what TRT-LLM expects; mismatches cause subtle kernel fallbacks. You said you are on 510 drivers, we recommend 515+ for our latest TRT kernels.
[07:14] Sana: noted, we'll test 515 in staging.

[07:22] Alex: recap: shared artifact (bench template + current TRT docker) from Alex, Priya to run 48h controlled replay tests on Helix staging, Helix to provide traces and nvprof logs, followup 2026-12-20.
[07:37] Diego: sounds good, thank you.
[07:40] Marcus: thank you all, appreciate the pragmatic plan.
[07:44] Alex: great, we'll send the sheet and security checklist after the call.
[07:49] Priya: talk soon — bye.

End of transcript.

title: Platform integration kickoff - gateway & SDK feasibility
preview: Initial integration discovery focused on how Everspring will connect model traffic through a gateway/proxy, SDK compatibility with LangChain/LlamaIndex, rate-limits/tenant quotas, and private deployment considerations (VPC/KMS). Redwood AE and SE outlined hosted vs dedicated vs private tradeoffs; customer shared constraints around SOC2/ISO, data residency and bursty financial workloads.

title: Security & deployment intake — Monterey Biosystems
preview: Discovery-style security intake for Monterey Biosystems exploring Redwood Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private. Primary concerns: encryption/KMS, audit trails, SSO integration, RBAC granularity, and data retention/ERASURE requirements. Team requested SOC2 evidence, architecture doc for Private/VPC, and a Dedicated performance benchmark. Legal needs questionnaire and PII handling notes.

Primary concerns: encryption/KMS, audit trails, SSO integration, RBAC granularity, and data retention/ERASURE requirements. Team requested SOC2 evidence, architecture doc for Private/VPC, and a Dedicated performance benchmark. Legal needs questionnaire and PII handling notes.

title: POC traffic thermography + SLA definition - GraniteBank
preview: Discovery and POC scoping focused on workload characterization for a 4-week pilot. Key metrics: baseline QPS ~12, expected peak bursts to 180-230 QPS, concurrency target 40-70, average input tokens ~35-60 and average model output tokens ~220-380. Success criteria: P99 generation latency under 350ms for interactive flows, 99.9% availability during pilot, cost envelope within provided budget. Bench plan: replay production traces in week 2, run controlled burst tests and tail latency characterization, deliver report and sizing recommendations by end of week 3.

title: Aperture Analytics — inference pathways & tool-calling scoping
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-11-18
Start: 16:00 PT
Duration: ~58 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Mills (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Maya Chen (Aperture CTO), Luca Rossi (Head of ML), Diego Alvarez (SRE), Priya Singh (Product)

Auto-summary (auto gen, may be rough):
Aperture is evaluating Redwood for a productionizing path for RAG-heavy features across multilingual documents. Primary concerns: 64k+ context support, cost vs latency tradeoffs, on-prem / VPC option because of regulated customer data, and robust tool-calling for internal search and DB writes. They want a comparative bench of open models (Mistral, Falcon, Mixtral) and clarity on Dedicated vs Private networking + KMS integration.

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: Hey everyone thanks for joining, this is Jordan from Redwood, I'm the AE on the account. Priya from our SE team is here, we said we'd keep it to an hour. How's everyone doing?
[00:08] Maya: Good thanks Jordan. Quick note we have about an hour then need to jump to another meeting.
[00:12] Jordan: Great. Priya, wanna do a quick intro then we can pass it over to Aperture to set context?
[00:18] Priya: Yep. Hi all, I'm Priya Natarajan on Redwood's solutions engineering side. I focus on inference performance especially for large contexts and multimodal toolchains. Today I'd like to understand your workload patterns, requirements for context length, multilingual support and any hard security constraints.
[00:32] Luca: Cool. To give context, Aperture's product ingests enterprise documents across EN/ES/JP and a few other languages. We do extractive QA, summarization, and an internal tool-calling flow where the model can call a search microservice and in some cases trigger a ticket create. We prefer open models where possible for licensing.
[00:50] Priya: Okay great. Can you quantify request rates and typical context sizes?
[00:55] Luca: At peak maybe 18 rps for the assistant use-case, mostly 8k contexts today but we have a subset of legal docs where we'd like to push to 64k for long form summarization. Latency SLO is roughly sub-600ms for short queries; for the long 64k jobs we accept a few seconds but need predictable throughput.
[01:15] Jordan: Got it. When you say 64k, is that tokens or characters?
[01:19] Luca: Tokens, roughly. We're using tokenization from our preproc pipeline so we'll share a sample.
[01:23] Diego: Security - we have customers that require data never leave our VPC, plus KMS-managed keys for encryption at rest. So hosted is ok for some customers but for a couple enterprise accounts we need Private with HSM/KMS.
[01:36] Jordan: Understood. That's a common split. Redwood has Hosted for quick onboarding, Dedicated reserved pools, and Private (VPC/on-prem) with customer KMS support. Priya can explain the Private network options.
[01:47] Priya: Quick note on model selection: from experience with similar RAG setups, a 64k context favors models that have both native long context support or efficient KV cache handling. For open models you mentioned Mistral, Falcon and Mixtral — each has tradeoffs. Mistral variants are cost efficient and fast on short lengths, Falcon-40B family scales better for instruction tuning but can be heavier. Mixtral is strong at multilinguals but sometimes less optimized for extreme context kernels. Also sometimes we use model chunking + KV caching to keep API latency consistent.
[02:20] Luca: Are there Redwood-verified benchmarks for 64k?
[02:23] Priya: We have internal profiles but we'll run a customer-focused bench. For reference we can test Mistral-7B-instruct, Falcon-40B, and Mixtral-8x with both 8k and 64k contexts and provide latency, tokens/sec, and cost per 1k tokens.
[02:36] Maya: Cost is big. We need a predictable cost model for reach customer; reserved capacity sounds interesting. What's the difference between Dedicated reserved and Private? Especially around throughput guarantees.
[02:47] Jordan: Dedicated gives you reserved GPU pools for predictable throughput and can be SLA-backed. Private is your own control plane with VPC or on-prem deployment where you're running the runtime inside your network — that gives you full data residency and KMS control but requires more ops input and typically a different commercial arrangement.
[03:08] Priya: Operationally, Private lets you use your KMS/HSM for model weights and logs, and your VPC isolation. We also support hybrid where sensitive workloads go to a Private cluster and everything else runs Hosted.
[03:19] Priya Singh: Hybrid is attractive. For customers that require strict residency we can gate only the regulated clients but keep general traffic on Hosted. What about tool-calling? We need the model to reliably call our search and ticket APIs with authenticated requests.
[03:32] Priya: We support function/tool calling semantics and can wire your microservices as tool endpoints. Two important things: 1) You decide whether tool payloads transit Redwood control plane (hosted) or are proxied via your network (Private). 2) We can sign tool-calls with ephemeral tokens and support auditable logs for traceability. There is some nuance on latency when the model synchronously waits for a tool response.
[03:54] Diego: Is it possible to keep the tool payloads completely internal for Private? We can't allow payloads to traverse third-party control planes.
[04:00] Priya: Yes, Private can be configured so tool calls remain inside the customer's network and the control plane only coordinates. We'll share the on-prem deployment checklist that details that flow.
[04:10] Luca: On model licensing, we prefer permissive open models. Which exact weights are you testing? Also, we've heard about quantized variants — how does Redwood handle int8 or GPTQ-style quantization for those larger models?
[04:26] Priya: We support quantized execution paths in the runtime. For many models int8 or 4-bit quant gives big cost savings at some quality tradeoffs. Our runtime has different execution kernels and we pick them based on hardware and the model profile. For quantized weights we test quality regressions as part of recommendations. We'll surface accuracy delta vs cost.
[04:46] Jordan: Another question: what's the fallback behavior if a Reserved pool is saturated?
[04:50] Priya: We support routing policies — you can define latency-first, cost-first, or capacity-fallback. If Dedicated is saturated we can route to Hosted or to a compatible model variant automatically. Rollouts and A/B are supported too.
[05:01] Maya: Good. A question on multilingual: our Japanese docs are a pain; Mixtral looked promising but we also want to preserve formatting/structured output for tables in contracts. Any tips?
[05:12] Priya: For multilingual format preservation we recommend a combination: a model with good tokenization for the language (Mixtral/LLama variants often better for Euro-Asian languages) plus structured output templates (function responses/JSON schemas). We can run a test set and check tokenization overhead and hallucination rates per language.
[05:30] Luca: How do you instrument quality? We need to guardrails — e.g., if a model regresses after quantization we need alerts.
[05:36] Priya: Redwood Optimize handles quality monitoring: you can attach prompt sets/evals and get regression alerts tied to deployed model versions. That ties into rollback policies.
[05:47] Priya Singh: Network picture — Diego can you say what infra we need for Private?
[05:51] Diego: We'll likely do a VPC peering model for the first pass. For on-prem we have an air-gapped environment for a subset of accounts; that's more heavy-lift. We use Vault and AWS KMS currently, we also have a HSM in certain regions. The main ask is whether Redwood supports bringing-your-own-KMS and HSM.
[06:09] Priya: Yes we support customer KMS integrations and HSM. We'll send our HSM/KMS integration guide. For the peering flow we have a network diagram we'll share.
[06:19] Jordan: Great. Quick pricing signals — dedicated vs hosted — we can do a sketch but need to follow up with the finance PR deck. For the bench, what performance axes are most important?
[06:28] Luca: Latency P50/P95, throughput at given concurrency (8/20 rps), and cost per 10k tokens. For 64k jobs, also tokens/sec and memory usage. If you can include failure modes like OOM rates that's helpful.
[06:41] Priya: Noted. We'll include OOM and KV evict stats. We'll also profile streaming vs non-streaming for your ticket flows which need partial results fast.
[06:54] Maya: For the tool-calling auth, we need SAML/SSO for the Redwood Console and RBAC. Do you support SCIM provisioning?
[07:03] Jordan: Yes SSO with SAML and SCIM provisioning available for enterprise accounts. RBAC is supported per-token and per-team.
[07:10] Priya: Also audit logs are available for Private and Dedicated with configurable retention.
[07:16] Luca: One more on latency: you mentioned KV cache earlier. Can you explain how Redwood caches long context and what that does to latency?
[07:25] Priya: Sure — we do prefix/KV caching where repeated context (common doc headers, prior turns) can be cached server-side so only new tokens cause generation work. For very long context we split documents into chunks and use a combination of retrieval + prefix caching to reduce token processing. In some cases continual batching and KV reuse gives large cost wins without hurting P50 too much. There's a tradeoff though — if your workload has very few repeated prefixes caching benefits are smaller.
[07:55] Diego: Are cached items encrypted at rest?
[07:58] Priya: Yes, cache and logs are encrypted; in Private you can control keys.
[08:03] Jordan: We're about 8 minutes in — just checking timing.
[08:06] Maya: We're good.
[08:10] Priya: (walkthrough) I'll outline proposed bench plan: 1) pick three model candidates (Mistral-7B, Falcon-40B, Mixtral-8x) 2) run 8k latency/throughput bench at 2/8/18 rps 3) run 64k summarization batch tests with 1-4 concurrency and measure tokens/sec and cost 4) run tool-calling roundtrip with search microservice to measure end-to-end 5) test quantized variants and report quality deltas.
[08:40] Luca: Sounds good. The search microservice overhead is about 120-250ms depending on query complexity; ticket creation is async but we want the model to receive the ticket id synchronously.
[08:51] Priya: We'll include end-to-end latencies with simulated search times.
[08:56] Diego: For the on-prem test, if we want to run the private runtime in our staging DC what are the minimal HW requirements? GPU types etc?
[09:06] Priya: Minimal depends on model choices; for 40B-class you'd want A100 80GB or equivalent with multi-GPU shard support. We also have optimized kernels for T4/RTX for smaller models. We'll send a sizing guide.
[09:18] Jordan: Can you give a quick example of fallback? Say Dedicated pool fails, does user notice?
[09:22] Priya: You can configure fallback to a cheaper model with an explicit notice header, or automatic silent fallback. We recommend explicit headers for audit and debugging.
[09:31] Maya: Anything surprising about real-world customers moving to Private? Time to production?
[09:36] Priya: It varies; Hosted can be minutes to days. Private often takes weeks depending on network and compliance checks. Typical on-prem pilots are 2-8 weeks. We'll provide a migration checklist.
[09:48] Luca: Do you have any guides on best model per language? Especially legal JP and ES?
[09:53] Priya: Yes we'll share a model selection doc with per-language heuristics — tokenization overhead, known hallucination patterns, recommended prompt templates and post-processing for tables.
[10:02] Jordan: Ok let's capture next steps — Priya will run benches, Jordan will follow up pricing, Aperture will share prompt set and network constraints. Any other blockers?
[10:12] Maya: No blockers, just timeline — we'd like pilot in December if feasible.
[10:16] Jordan: Sounds reasonable. We'll aim for bench results and commercial sketch before Thanksgiving week.
[10:22] (background cross talk / audio glitch)
[10:25] Priya: Quick note on streaming and partial results: for ticket creation we can stream an interim summary while waiting for search to finish — that tends to help user-perceived latency. There is a small cost implication for more streaming tokens.
[10:40] Luca: Good point. For the RAG flow we want the model to avoid calling search unless confidence is low; any tooling for that?
[10:48] Priya: You can instrument confidence thresholds or a classification model pre-filter. Redwood Optimize can help with monitoring those heuristics.
[10:55] Diego: A compliance question: audit logs retention — we need 7 years for some customers. Is that supported?
[11:01] Jordan: We have configurable retention on Dedicated and Private; Hosted has tiered retention plans. We'll get specifics from the compliance pack.
[11:09] Priya Singh: Any special network ports or outbound rules for Private peering?
[11:14] Priya: We'll send the network diagram; generally outbound HTTPS plus a control plane port for orchestration, but everything can be tunneled through your network.
[11:24] Maya: Last question — how do you do version pinning and rollouts for model updates?
[11:29] Priya: You can pin model version per-route, do canary deploys, A/B, and automatic fallback. We keep versioned artifacts and you can schedule rollbacks if regressions detected.
[11:40] Jordan: Perfect — time check, about 6 minutes left. Any final questions?
[11:45] Luca: No, just please include the quantized quality deltas in the bench output.
[11:49] Priya: Will do.
[11:52] Jordan: Ok we'll recap and share notes. Thanks everyone.
[11:56] Maya: Thanks.

[END - meeting length ~58 minutes]

Notes on transcription: There are a few stutters and mis-heard product names in the auto transcript (e.g., "Redwood Dedicate" instead of "Dedicated"). Proper nouns may be slightly off.

References shared during meeting (links/notes):
- Redwood on-prem/KMS integration guide (will be shared)
- Proposed bench plan (SE will run; results to be shared)
- Sample prompt set to be uploaded by Aperture

title: Peak readiness & cost-sprint playback
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-11-09
Start time: 15:00 PST
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen, David Ruiz, Priya Natarajan (Redwood); Lena Ortiz, Marcus Lee, Sana Iqbal (AstraCart)

Summary:
Quick playback of the cost-sprint changes we rolled out last month, discussion of capacity for upcoming peak shopping period, and agreed tests + runbook items to de-risk holiday traffic. Main friction is budget vs latency SLA and whether AstraCart should reserve Dedicated pools or rely on hosted with burst capacity.

Topics covered:
- Recent usage trends and token-level cost movements
- Tail latency incidents and a couple of 95th/99th percentile blips during flash promo
- Dedicated vs hosted tradeoffs, burst capacity, and autoscaling policies
- Runbook and fallback model strategy (smaller quantized model fallback)
- Compliance / audit logs (SAML/SSO, KMS questions)
- Next steps and scheduling of load tests

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya Chen: hey everyone thanks for joining, we'll try to keep this tight, agenda sent ahead, main thing is the peak readiness and cost playback, hi lena, hi marcus
[00:10] Lena Ortiz: hey maya, thanks. we saw some spikes last week, want to make sure we're not going to melt the stack in december, right.
[00:15] Marcus Lee: yeah the promo flash on tuesday, our 95th got up to 420ms which is higher than our objective, and cost was up 28% week over week, so trying to pin down root cause
[00:27] David Ruiz: for context, we rolled a batching tweak and a longer kv-cache TTL which should reduce tokens per request for repeated sessions, but there were some long-context chats that bypassed prefix cache, so that can inflate compute.
[00:40] Marcus Lee: right, some of our cart personalization prompts include full order history, maybe we can truncate or move to embeddings?
[00:48] Maya Chen: that's one of the levers. david, can you briefly say the difference in tail latency between hosted and dedicated profiles?
[00:56] David Ruiz: sure — dedicated with pinned GPU pools tends to have lower 99p because we avoid cold starts and node contention, hosted with burst sometimes routes to different model variants which adds routing time; for AstraCart's traffic pattern (lots of short sessions with peaks) dedicated gives consistent tail but costs more upfront.
[01:15] Lena Ortiz: cost is the rub, finance will push back on big commits unless we can show hard savings. sana is on the call and wants to weigh in.
[01:22] Sana Iqbal: yeah, if we commit monthly we're looking at a target of the commit not to exceed 35% of current forecasted cloud infra spend. otherwise we prefer pay-as-you-go with smarter throttles
[01:32] Maya Chen: fair. we can show a modeled ROI: reserved + autoscale buffer vs pure hosted. david, do you have an example math for 6-month commit?
[01:39] David Ruiz: yep — for example, a 6-month 3-GPU reserved pool reduces per-token cost by about 18% for inference at your typical sequence length. with prefix caching it could be another 7% improvement, so combined savings could be ~25% depending on traffic shape.
[01:55] Marcus Lee: sounds good — what's the failover if dedicated is full? we can't have checkout pages slow down.
[02:03] Maya Chen: two options — automatic fallback to a compatible lower-cost quantized variant, or cross-region fallback to hosted shard. both are supported; fallback adds a small latency bump but keeps availability.
[02:15] Lena Ortiz: is the fallback deterministic and auditable? we need to show SREs and security that logs capture that switch.
[02:21] Priya Natarajan: yes, the console yields audit trails for rollouts and fallback events, and we can push enriched logs to your SIEM via the pub/sub integration. there is also an option to have those events emitted to a customer-managed Kinesis or GCS sink.
[02:35] Marcus Lee: any compliance stuff? SOC 2 is fine but we need SOC 2 plus encryption at rest with our KMS. can we BYOK?
[02:42] Maya Chen: yes, Private deployments support customer-managed KMS and HSM integrations; hosted has envelope encryption with Redwood KMS by default but we offer BYOK for enterprise private instances. i'll attach the security brief after.
[02:53] Sana Iqbal: great — i'll need that for procurement. also on billing cadence, can you do quarterly invoicing or does it have to be monthly?
[02:59] Maya Chen: we can do quarterly for enterprise deals, otherwise monthly is standard for reserved. we'll include options in the pricing doc.
[03:07] Marcus Lee: back to the technical side — the 99p blips, are those GC pauses or scheduling?
[03:13] David Ruiz: in the sample traces you shared, there were a few long-payload requests that forced extra kv cache misses plus higher sequence decoding time; not GC. also we saw a small percentage of requests routed to older model variants during the roll; that's something the routing policy can avoid with strict pinning.
[03:30] Lena Ortiz: ok. i'd like a test that simulates our peak with the same prompt mix — not just pure qps.
[03:36] David Ruiz: absolutely — we'll set up a workload replay. need your sample prompt set and the request distribution. marcus you said you'll provide CSV?
[03:43] Marcus Lee: yes i'll pull last year's holiday week and this year's promo traffic in the same format as before and include percentiles.
[03:49] Maya Chen: for the load test we recommend two phases: passive replay for 2 hours to warm caches, then a progressive spike to 2x expected QPS for 30 minutes. measure 95/99/average and cost delta.
[04:05] Lena Ortiz: what's the expected cost delta during the stress run?
[04:08] David Ruiz: during the spike you'll see the per-minute spend go up linearly; if you have cache primed it won't be as bad. we'll instrument token-level breakdown so you can see the benefit of cache.
[04:18] Sana Iqbal: can we set alerting thresholds? finance wants caps and alerts at certain spend rates.
[04:22] Maya Chen: yes — console supports budget alerts at the token level and per-route spend; we can set automated throttles or reject non-critical routes when budget hits a threshold.
[04:32] Marcus Lee: great. what about model rollouts, we don't want a new variant to degrade quality during peak.
[04:36] Priya Natarajan: rollouts can be canary by percent and tied to quality evals; we can also lock model pinning for specific routes during high-traffic windows.
[04:45] Lena Ortiz: that's important. we also have a legal review on December 1st, procurement will ask for SLA language and data retention guarantees.
[04:52] Maya Chen: we'll include standard SLA language and retention options in the package that priya will send.
[04:58] David Ruiz: quick note — for your personalization use case, moving historical order features to embeddings is both cheaper and more cache-friendly. it's a small engineering lift but big ops payoff.
[05:08] Marcus Lee: we'll evaluate that, maybe do a POC on a subset of traffic.
[05:12] Lena Ortiz: ok. recap and next steps?
[05:15] Maya Chen: recap: maya share pricing, david set up benchmark plan and runbook, marcus share traffic + prompt set, priya schedule runbook review, sana to check budget ceiling. we'll aim to run the load test week of 11/16 and a runbook walkthrough the week of 11/23.
[05:32] Sana Iqbal: fine by me.
[05:35] Marcus Lee: thanks all.
[05:37] Maya Chen: thanks — i'll follow up in email with attachments and a calendar invite for the runbook review.
[05:42] Lena Ortiz: thanks, bye.

End of transcript.

References / artifacts mentioned:
- "reserved_capacity_pricing_v2.pdf" (Maya to attach)
- "austracart_traffic_2025-2026.csv" (Marcus to provide)
- Load test config: "peak-replay-plan.yaml" (David will create)

Notes about transcript quality: occasional word swaps, timestamps approximated to minute markers; speaker attribution between Maya and Priya occasionally merged.

Date: 2026-11-09
Start time: 15:00 PST
Duration: ~55 minutes
Attendees: Maya Chen, David Ruiz, Priya Natarajan (Redwood); Lena Ortiz, Marcus Lee, Sana Iqbal (AstraCart)

Summary:
Quick playback of the cost-sprint changes we rolled out last month, discussion of capacity for upcoming peak shopping period, and agreed tests + runbook items to de-risk holiday traffic. Main friction is budget vs latency SLA and whether AstraCart should reserve Dedicated pools or rely on hosted with burst capacity.

Topics covered:
- Recent usage trends and token-level cost movements
- Tail latency incidents and a couple of 95th/99th percentile blips during flash promo
- Dedicated vs hosted tradeoffs, burst capacity, and autoscaling policies
- Runbook and fallback model strategy (smaller quantized model fallback)
- Compliance / audit logs (SAML/SSO, KMS questions)
- Next steps and scheduling of load tests

[00:40] Marcus Lee: right, some of our cart personalization prompts include full order history, maybe we can truncate or move to embeddings?

[01:15] Lena Ortiz: cost is the rub, finance will push back on big commits unless we can show hard savings. sana is on the call and wants to weigh in.

[02:03] Maya Chen: two options — automatic fallback to a compatible lower-cost quantized variant, or cross-region fallback to hosted shard. both are supported; fallback adds a small latency bump but keeps availability.

[02:53] Sana Iqbal: great — i'll need that for procurement. also on billing cadence, can you do quarterly invoicing or does it have to be monthly?
[02:59] Maya Chen: we can do quarterly for enterprise deals, otherwise monthly is standard for reserved. we'll include options in the pricing doc.
[03:07] Marcus Lee: back to the technical side — the 99p blips, are those GC pauses or scheduling?
[03:13] David Ruiz: in the sample traces you shared, there were a few long-payload requests that forced extra kv cache misses plus higher sequence decoding time; not GC. also we saw a small percentage of requests routed to older model variants during the roll; that's something the routing policy can avoid with strict pinning.

[03:36] David Ruiz: absolutely — we'll set up a workload replay. need your sample prompt set and the request distribution. marcus you said you'll provide CSV?

[03:49] Maya Chen: for the load test we recommend two phases: passive replay for 2 hours to warm caches, then a progressive spike to 2x expected QPS for 30 minutes. measure 95/99/average and cost delta.
[04:05] Lena Ortiz: what's the expected cost delta during the stress run?
[04:08] David Ruiz: during the spike you'll see the per-minute spend go up linearly; if you have cache primed it won't be as bad. we'll instrument token-level breakdown so you can see the benefit of cache.
[04:18] Sana Iqbal: can we set alerting thresholds? finance wants caps and alerts at certain spend rates.
[04:22] Maya Chen: yes — console supports budget alerts at the token level and per-route spend; we can set automated throttles or reject non-critical routes when budget hits a threshold.
[04:32] Marcus Lee: great. what about model rollouts, we don't want a new variant to degrade quality during peak.
[04:36] Priya Natarajan: rollouts can be canary by percent and tied to quality evals; we can also lock model pinning for specific routes during high-traffic windows.
[04:45] Lena Ortiz: that's important. we also have a legal review on December 1st, procurement will ask for SLA language and data retention guarantees.
[04:52] Maya Chen: we'll include standard SLA language and retention options in the package that priya will send.
[04:58] David Ruiz: quick note — for your personalization use case, moving historical order features to embeddings is both cheaper and more cache-friendly. it's a small engineering lift but big ops payoff.
[05:08] Marcus Lee: we'll evaluate that, maybe do a POC on a subset of traffic.
[05:12] Lena Ortiz: ok. recap and next steps?
[05:15] Maya Chen: recap: maya share pricing, david set up benchmark plan and runbook, marcus share traffic + prompt set, priya schedule runbook review, sana to check budget ceiling. we'll aim to run the load test week of 11/16 and a runbook walkthrough the week of 11/23.
[05:32] Sana Iqbal: fine by me.
[05:35] Marcus Lee: thanks all.
[05:37] Maya Chen: thanks — i'll follow up in email with attachments and a calendar invite for the runbook review.
[05:42] Lena Ortiz: thanks, bye.

End of transcript.

title: BeaconRetail Edge fallthrough & streaming backpressure demo
preview: Redwood AE+SE walked BeaconRetail through an edge-case focused demo: long 200k+ token contexts, sustained high TPS bursts, streaming backpressure scenarios and graceful fallthroughs to cheaper variants. Security questions about KMS, audit logs and VPC were raised. Agreed next steps: run a short POC with BeaconRetail test corpus, share security docs and benchmark instructions, and schedule a follow-up to review pilot results.

title: Orion Labs - weekly pilot health & econ review
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-09-25
Start: 10:30 AM PT
Duration: 48 minutes
Attendees: Liam Park (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Eva Morales (CSM) / Danielle Ortiz (Orion Head of ML), Marcus Lee (Platform), Priya Singh (Security), Tom Alvarez (SRE)

Summary:
Quick weekly sync to review token economics and performance metrics from this week's pilot runs, discuss batching vs latency tradeoffs, caching savings observed, and next steps for a canary of the quantized model variant. Security questions on KMS/SSO touched on and follow-up assigned.

Topics: tokens/sec/$ trends, prefix/KV caching effects, batching configuration experiments, p50/p95 latency, dedicated vs hosted routing for critical paths, SOC2/KMS questions

--- Transcript ---
[00:00] Liam: hey everyone, thanks for joining. quick roll call?
[00:06] Danielle: yeah thanks liam, danielle here. we got marcus, priya s., tom, all on.
[00:10] Marcus: hi liam.
[00:12] Priya S: hi.
[00:14] Liam: awesome. priya and i will jump into the econ numbers then priya can take the perf bits. eva will capture next steps.
[00:22] Priya N: ok quick context — last 48 hours we ran the three config vectors: baseline hosted medium batch, aggressive batching with 3x concurrency, and prefix/kvcache enabled on the most common prompts.
[00:35] Tom: slight clarification — the kvcache run was only on the customer-visible search flow, not the entire pipeline.
[00:40] Priya N: right good call, thanks tom. so headline: baseline tokens-per-dollar was roughly 8.2k tokens/$ with our standard 16-bit variant. when we enabled prefix caching for the search path, we saw an effective tokens-per-dollar jump to ~13.1k tokens/$ for that flow — so about a 37% token-cost reduction for repeated prefixes.
[01:05] Danielle: ok that 37% is specific to the search flow with heavy prompt reuse?
[01:09] Priya N: correct — for conversational flows with long shared context it's even higher, up to ~45% in a few spot checks, but those were smaller sample sizes.
[01:18] Marcus: and batching? you mentioned aggressive batching — what did that do to throughput and latency?
[01:22] Priya N: so aggressive batching pushed tokens/sec throughput up ~2.8x compared to baseline but the p95 latency on single-request journeys increased ~120ms on average. p50 saw negligible change. that latency bump matters for your customer-facing sync path.
[01:38] Marcus: got it. for our low-latency checkout validation we probably need to keep p95 < 350ms, are we close?
[01:44] Priya N: with medium batch we were hitting p95 around 320–340ms depending on sequence length, the aggressive batch sometimes crossed 450ms. so for your threshold we'd recommend a hybrid: small static batch for the checkout route and larger batch for async background hits.
[02:01] Liam: just to add econ framing — tokens/sec/$ is a bit of a composite: it folds cost per GPU-minute, kernel efficiency, and the savings from caching. we modeled two scenarios in the workbook.
[02:12] Liam: scenario A (prod-like): split 70% background (batch-friendly) and 30% low-latency; with prefix caching applied to background, net tokens/$ jumps to ~11.7k. scenario B (more reuse in convo): tokens/$ to ~14.8k.
[02:27] Danielle: can you share that workbook?
[02:29] Liam: yes — i'll attach a live sheet after the call. it's in the shared folder, "Orion pilot economics v2".
[02:35] Priya N: another datapoint: we ran a quantized 4-bit variant on a replica node for bench. raw token-cost dropped ~10% further versus the 16-bit path, but the generation quality for rare entity hallucinations was slightly worse in a couple of prompts.
[02:54] Danielle: quality tradeoff noted — we can't accept reduced precision on the fraud detection prompts.
[02:58] Priya N: totally — our suggestion is to gate quantized variants behind a canary: route 5–10% of traffic that's low-sensitivity, observe rollback triggers.
[03:08] Marcus: any difference in caching with quantized models?
[03:11] Priya N: negligible — KV cache is architecture-agnostic mostly, the cache keys and hit rates stayed consistent; quantization affects kernel perf but not cache semantics.
[03:20] Tom: quick security question — we saw a doc about KMS integration but have a question about customer-managed keys and key rotation.
[03:27] Eva: priya s, do you want redwood to send the detailed KMS integration diagram?
[03:30] Priya S (customer): yes please, and also SSO flow for the private control plane.
[03:33] Liam: we'll send SOC2 pack and KMS flow diagram. priya n will highlight what is managed vs customer-managed.
[03:40] Priya N: just to be explicit — in Redwood Private you can bring your own KMS (HSM/KMS), and rotation policies are compatible with our audit logs; we'll document the exact steps.
[03:48] Danielle: great. back to perf — can you quantify caching hit rates?
[03:52] Priya N: hit rate on search prefix cache was ~62% for the last 24 hours sample; for the dialog reuse segment it was 71% on average.
[03:59] Danielle: so that 62% hit rate drove the 37% tokens/$ improvement?
[04:03] Priya N: yes roughly; the math in the sheet uses hit-rate * avg prefix length to estimate avoided tokens. remember there's also backend compute amortization so combined effect is multiplicative.
[04:15] Marcus: about routing — is there an easy policy to route high-sensitivity requests to dedicated nodes while letting other requests use hosted to save cost?
[04:21] Liam: yes, we have smart routing policies. you can tag routes with priority/latency requirements; Redwood will bias to dedicated capacity (or pinned model variant) for those routes and use hosted pool for low-priority. we can set up failover to hosted if dedicated is saturated.
[04:35] Marcus: latency between regions? our EU customers are sensitive to cross-region hops.
[04:39] Priya N: we recommend deploying a regional private plane or using Redwood Dedicated in eu-west to keep intra-region latency low — the difference we saw was ~30–60ms extra roundtrip for cross-region routing.
[04:50] Tom: any notable noise in the metrics? were these runs under steady-state traffic?
[04:54] Priya N: the runs were synthetic replay of your observed traffic profile; we'll need the real production traffic to validate variance but the week-over-week samples were fairly stable.
[05:03] Danielle: ok next — can you explain the tokens/sec/$ number you quoted earlier (13.1k etc) in plain terms for finance?
[05:09] Liam: sure — tokens/$ is basically how many output tokens you can expect for one dollar of GPU spend given your request patterns and the runtime optimizations. higher is better. we provide a worked example in the sheet: for 100M tokens/month, the difference between 8.2k and 13.1k tokens/$ changes spend by ~38%.
[05:27] Danielle: that's material. finance will want line-item assumptions.
[05:30] Liam: yes we'll include assumptions: average seq length, prefix reuse rate, GPU hourly cost, and margin.
[05:36] Priya N: one more operational note — batching gives you lower unit cost but requires a stable queue depth; bursty traffic can cause queueing and latency spikes. the hybrid approach we discussed minimizes that.
[05:48] Marcus: what about cold-start kv cache misses? how do we warm caches?
[05:51] Priya N: we seed caches with historical top-N prefixes and also have a background warm-up job — we can prioritize warming for new releases.
[06:00] Tom: any logs or traces we should share to help tuning?
[06:03] Liam: yes please share a sampling of traces for the customer checkout path at peak and off-peak; mark which traces are latency-sensitive.
[06:11] Danielle: will do.
[06:13] Priya N: quick note on A/B testing the batch sizes — we recommend progressive rollout: 10% traffic with current batch, 40% with medium, 50% with aggressive for background; check p95 daily then move.
[06:26] Marcus: ok. how long to set that up?
[06:28] Priya N: we can configure routing rules today and push to staging; canary in prod after you approve schedule, should be a day of config plus monitoring dashboards.
[06:36] Liam: question from product — do you have timeline constraints for this pilot?
[06:40] Danielle: we'd like a pilot decision by mid-october — ideally commit to dedicated nodes end of October if numbers look good.
[06:47] Liam: noted. we'll aim to have the updated workbook and suggested rollout plan in your inbox by Tuesday.
[06:53] Eva: capturing action items now.
[06:55] Priya N: any other blockers?
[06:57] Priya S (customer): just the KMS and SSO clarifications — otherwise good.
[07:01] Liam: ok recap: we'll send workbook, batching configs, KMS/SSO docs, and propose times for quantized canary. orion will send production traffic profile and traces. set up follow-up meeting next week if needed.
[07:15] Danielle: thanks everyone. that was helpful.
[07:18] Liam: thanks all, see you next week.

End of transcript.

Attachments mentioned: "Orion pilot economics v2" (sheet), KMS integration diagram (to be sent), SOC2 pack link (to be sent).

Notes on accuracy / artifacts: speaker labels occasionally abbreviated; transcription contains minor mis-hearings (e.g., "kvcache" vs "KV cache").

[06:13] Priya N: quick note on A/B testing the batch sizes — we recommend progressive rollout: 10% traffic with current batch, 40% with medium, 50% with aggressive for background; check p95 daily then move.
[06:26] Marcus: ok. how long to set that up?
[06:28] Priya N: we can configure routing rules today and push to staging; canary in prod after you approve schedule, should be a day of config plus monitoring dashboards.
[06:36] Liam: question from product — do you have timeline constraints for this pilot?
[06:40] Danielle: we'd like a pilot decision by mid-october — ideally commit to dedicated nodes end of October if numbers look good.
[06:47] Liam: noted. we'll aim to have the updated workbook and suggested rollout plan in your inbox by Tuesday.
[06:53] Eva: capturing action items now.
[06:55] Priya N: any other blockers?
[06:57] Priya S (customer): just the KMS and SSO clarifications — otherwise good.
[07:01] Liam: ok recap: we'll send workbook, batching configs, KMS/SSO docs, and propose times for quantized canary. orion will send production traffic profile and traces. set up follow-up meeting next week if needed.
[07:15] Danielle: thanks everyone. that was helpful.
[07:18] Liam: thanks all, see you next week.

End of transcript.

Attachments mentioned: "Orion pilot economics v2" (sheet), KMS integration diagram (to be sent), SOC2 pack link (to be sent).

Notes on accuracy / artifacts: speaker labels occasionally abbreviated; transcription contains minor mis-hearings (e.g., "kvcache" vs "KV cache").

title: Sandstone Systems — Success Path Kickoff & Technical Intake
preview:
Meeting Header:
Date/Time: 2026-09-23 16:00 UTC
Duration: ~48 minutes
Location: Zoom (recording)
Attendees: Maya Chen (Redwood AE); Omar Ruiz (Redwood SE); Priya Natarajan (Redwood CSM); Liam O'Connor (Sandstone Head of Platform); Neha Patel (SRE); Carlos Mendes (Security Lead); Ava Li (Product)

Summary:
Quick kickoff to align success path and technical intake for Sandstone's Redwood rollout. Main topics: network/topology constraints, compliance questions (KMS/keys, SOC2), decision between Hosted vs Dedicated vs Private deployment for initial POC, benchmarking plan and one-week sprint for onboarding.

Topics:
- Security & compliance (SOC2, SAML/SSO, KMS)
- Deployment mode: Hosted API vs Dedicated vs Private (VPC/on-prem)
- Network & peering requirements (VPC, firewall rules)
- POC scope and benchmark metrics (latency SLO, throughput, cost per token)
- Onboarding timeline and runbook

Action items (quick list in transcript, see metadata): refer to action_items field

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya Chen: All right everyone, thanks for joining. Quick intro, I'm Maya from Redwood, our SE Omar and Priya from CS are here. How's that on your side?
[00:12] Liam O'Connor: Hey Maya, thanks. Liam here, Neha, Carlos, and Ava are with me. Big priorities are latency and data residency for our customers.
[00:25] Maya Chen: Great. Today we want to align on success criteria and get the technical intake so we can spin up a POC. Omar will walk through the config questions.
[00:38] Omar Ruiz: Cool, I'll go slow. First, are you thinking hosted or private? we often suggest starting hosted for speed but Dedicated or Private for regulated tenants.
[00:51] Neha Patel: We need VPC peering and KMS integration, our compliance team will push back on public-hosted if we can't control keys.
[01:05] Carlos Mendes: Right, SOC2 and key custody are musts. Can you clarify what Redwood Private looks like? Is it full air-gapped on prem or a VPC-hosted with customer KMS?
[01:20] Omar Ruiz: Both options are supported. Redwood Private can be a customer-managed VPC with our control plane or an on-prem package for air-gapped installs. For keys you can plug in your KMS/HSM. There's sometimes confusion in names — our docs call it "Private" and "Dedicated" for reserved capacity, I'll share the one-pager.
[01:40] Liam O'Connor: OK, for the POC we could demo in Dedicated and then if compliance requires, move to Private. What's the typical time to provision a Dedicated cluster?
[01:53] Maya Chen: Typically 3–5 business days once network and billing are squared away; can push faster for pilots.
[02:05] Ava Li: Quick question — how are you handling KV cache and prefix caching? we worry about cost spikes on long conversations.
[02:18] Omar Ruiz: We do automatic KV caching and prefix caching, but it's workload dependent. For conversational agents we recommend a cache policy + batching config — Redwood Optimize will give suggestions based on traces. The transcriber sometimes picks up KV as K V or K-V, so apologies if earlier docs had weird spacing.
[02:40] Neha Patel: Important: our egress policy requires TLS to a specific proxy and we need to list IP ranges for firewall rules.
[02:51] Omar Ruiz: We'll need your VPC CIDR and the egress proxy details. For Dedicated and Private we can peer VPCs directly or set up NAT tunnels. I'll include a template.
[03:06] Priya Natarajan: On the project plan side, we propose a two-week onboarding sprint: week 0 runbook & provisioning, week 1 POC benchmarks and integration tests, then a go/no-go. Thoughts?
[03:20] Liam O'Connor: Sounds good. Success criteria: < 120ms median token latency for short prompts, 99.9% availability for API calls during test window, and cost per 1k tokens under X (we'll fill in exact budget).
[03:39] Carlos Mendes: Also need audit logs retention and SSO. We use Okta.
[03:45] Maya Chen: We support SAML/SSO with Okta, RBAC, and audit logs — retention is configurable. I'll send the security docs.
[03:56] Ava Li: For POC models, do you have recommended quantized variants? we prefer smaller models for cost but need quality.
[04:08] Omar Ruiz: Yes — we have curated profiles. For many customers we recommend a mixed strategy: run a higher-quality model for critical paths and a quantized variant for bulk inference. Redwood Console shows per-route cost and latency so you can split traffic.
[04:27] Liam O'Connor: Competitors we've looked at are Anthropic and OpenAI endpoints, also exploring Vertex AI for infra. Why Redwood vs others?
[04:40] Maya Chen: Two reasons: production observability and predictable economics. We give token-level cost, per-route dashboards, and tooling to recommend batching/quantization. Also the unified runtime across hosted/dedicated/private is a big differentiator.
[04:58] Neha Patel: Can we run benchmarks that mirror our bursty traffic pattern? 200 concurrent short requests with p99 under 800ms?
[05:10] Omar Ruiz: Yes, we'll provide a benchmark harness — we'll need your sample prompt distribution and expected concurrency. I'll share a script.
[05:21] Liam O'Connor: On the legal side any typical procurement hurdles?
[05:26] Maya Chen: Standard enterprise SLAs and contracts — Priya will loop in our legal. We can share a starter contract.
[05:36] Carlos Mendes: Encryption at rest — does Redwood manage keys if we go hosted?
[05:41] Maya Chen: If hosted, we manage application keys by default but can enable customer-managed KMS for enterprise customers. For Dedicated/Private you can plug your KMS.
[05:53] [08:00] -- transcript jumps, brief audio blip --
[08:12] Ava Li: For integration, is there a reference SDK we should use?
[08:17] Omar Ruiz: Yes, SDKs in Python/Go/JS. We'll share sample code and a Postman collection. There's sometimes mismatch in naming conventions between SDKs and docs, we'll include examples.
[08:36] Priya Natarajan: I'll coordinate onboarding sessions and send an initial runbook. We'll also set a weekly checkpoints during POC.
[08:44] Neha Patel: On networking, we need a diagram. I'll attach our current infra.
[08:51] Omar Ruiz: Great — please attach it to the ticket. Also quick note: when we talk about "batching" sometimes customers equate that to slower latencies — we can tune continuous batching to respect latency SLOs.
[09:08] Liam O'Connor: I'd like to see token-level cost breakdowns in the console during the POC so we can set alerts.
[09:16] Maya Chen: Console supports token-level cost and alerting per route. We'll add you with viewer access.
[09:24] Carlos Mendes: Audit trail compliance — how immutable are logs?
[09:29] Omar Ruiz: Audit logs can be forwarded to your SIEM or self-hosted storage. Retention and immutability depend on configuration; we'll include an architecture note.
[09:42] Ava Li: Any known performance regressions with long-context models?
[09:46] Omar Ruiz: We recommend kernel selection and quantization profiles per model; sometimes older kernels show perf regressions at 32k contexts. We'll test with your workload.
[09:59] Maya Chen: Let's recap next steps.
[10:02] Maya Chen: Action 1: I'll send SOC2/ISO and the security whitepaper plus starter contract (Maya, due 2026-09-27).
[10:12] Omar Ruiz: Action 2: I'll provision benchmark templates and a Dedicated trial config and share instructions for VPC peering (Omar, due 2026-10-01).
[10:21] Priya Natarajan: Action 3: I'll send the onboarding runbook and propose sprint dates (Priya, due 2026-09-25).
[10:29] Neha Patel: Action 4: we'll send our VPC CIDR and proxy details (Neha, due 2026-09-28).
[10:36] Carlos Mendes: Action 5: I will compile compliance questions related to KMS rotation and key escrow (Carlos, due 2026-09-30).
[10:44] Liam O'Connor: OK, sounds good. If we can get a Dedicated cluster for testing that'd be ideal.
[10:51] Maya Chen: We'll start provisioning once we have the topology and PO. Priya will include legal/contract steps.
[11:02] Ava Li: One last thing: do you have a guide for model version pinning and rollbacks? we need a safe way to revert.
[11:10] Omar Ruiz: Yes — rollouts and canaries are supported in the Console; you can pin model versions per route and use automatic fallback to compatible variants. We'll send the rollout playbook.
[11:26] Maya Chen: Thanks everyone. We'll follow up in email with the docs and a meeting to run the benchmark next week.
[11:33] Liam O'Connor: Thanks all.
[11:36] [END]

title: Q3 Usage & Resilience Strategy Review - Redwood x Ironbridge
preview: Q3 check-in focused on rising token usage from a new summarization feature, resulting cost increases; two recent production incidents were root-caused to cache invalidation and aggressive autoscaler policies. Agreed to run a 2-week perf POC to validate quantized model profile and prefix-caching; Redwood to provide security pack, runbooks, and 12-month cost forecast. SLO templates and dashboard will be shared; next touchpoints include POC kickoff and forecast delivery.
text: Q3 check-in focused on rising token usage from a new summarization feature, resulting cost increases; two recent production incidents were root-caused to cache invalidation and aggressive autoscaler policies. Agreed to run a 2-week perf POC to validate quantized model profile and prefix-caching; Redwood to provide security pack, runbooks, and 12-month cost forecast. SLO templates and dashboard will be shared; next touchpoints include POC kickoff and forecast delivery.

title: Mnemosyne Labs POC: Eval Load & Quality Rubric Kickoff
preview: Kickoff for a 6-week POC scoping focused on quality evaluation and load benchmarks. Discussed success criteria (latency SLO, hallucination rate, precision on extraction tasks), workload sizing, golden prompt construction, regression test design, and timeline for pilot. Agreed next steps and owners.
text: Kickoff for a 6-week POC scoping focused on quality evaluation and load benchmarks. Discussed success criteria (latency SLO, hallucination rate, precision on extraction tasks), workload sizing, golden prompt construction, regression test design, and timeline for pilot. Agreed next steps and owners.

title: Scaling to 1M context: sharding & runtime tuning (Valen Med)
preview: Deep technical session focused on strategies to serve extremely long contexts (800k-1M tokens) for Valen Med's longitudinal patient records use case. Discussed KV-sharding across GPUs, block-sparse and sliding-window attention, quantization tradeoffs, runtime memory offload, RDMA/InfiniBand networking considerations for on-prem, and realistic latency vs cost targets. Agreed to run a Redwood Dedicated benchmark with customer's sample corpus and follow up on networking and security requirements.

Discussed KV-sharding across GPUs, block-sparse and sliding-window attention, quantization tradeoffs, runtime memory offload, RDMA/InfiniBand networking considerations for on-prem, and realistic latency vs cost targets. Agreed to run a Redwood Dedicated benchmark with customer's sample corpus and follow up on networking and security requirements.

title: 2026-07-16-bench-standup-latency-throughput-vs-legacy-lyonexus
preview: Weekly POC bench standup. Reviewed latency and throughput deltas vs Lyonexus' legacy provider. Identified routing and KV cache warmup as main drivers for p99. Discussed dedicated slot request and cost tradeoffs. Agreed on follow-up bench with hot KV cache and updated routing thresholds. Next steps: share configs, run targeted 100k token head-to-head, customer to share traffic trace and decision timeline.

title: POC Multilang Knowledge Extractor Workshop - Sandbridge Labs
preview: Kickoff scoping for a 4-week POC to validate a multilingual knowledge-extraction pipeline for Sandbridge Labs. Discussed success criteria (precision/recall on entity pairs, latency SLO, cost envelope), workload sizing (peak qps and daily tokens), benchmark plan (human eval + automated metrics), and shortlisted open models with tradeoffs. Agreed on deliverables: SOW, 200-sample test corpus, and initial micro-benchmarks run by Redwood.

title: Cedarbrook Health - Contract extension & Private Edge pilot review
preview:
Header:
Date: 2026-07-14
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Samir Shah (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Jordan Lee (Redwood CSM), Maya Patel (Cedarbrook), David Rios (Cedarbrook CTO), Ellen Fox (Security), Noah Grant (SRE), Lina Torres (Procurement), Speaker 1 (intern)

Summary: Quick check-in to align on contract extension length, committed capacity, and plan for Private (VPC/on-prem) pilot in the EU cluster. Security artifacts and pilot logistics to be shared. Customer raised latency and data residency constraints for EU patients.

Topics: contract term / committed capacity, private edge on-prem pilot, SOC2/KMS, latency vs cost tradeoffs, timeline for PO and pilot kickoff.

Transcript:
[00:00] Samir: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — we'll keep this tight, fifteen to fifty two wait, sorry, fifty two minutes ish. Quick intros — Jordan, Priya and I'm Samir from Redwood.
[00:12] Maya: Hi all — Maya here, I'll run this on our side. We've got David, Ellen and Noah on the line too.
[00:20] Samir: Awesome. The goal today is to confirm the extension term and go over the private/on-prem pilot plan in the EU region, since that's the blocker for your security and residency folks.
[00:29] David: Yep, main ask from execs is predictable unit costs and having EU-resident inference for PHI-adjacent data. We don't need full air gap but VPC with customer-managed keys and audit logs.
[00:41] Ellen: Also need to validate KMS/HSM options and retention policies. We do regular audits and SOC two is non-negotiable.
[00:48] Jordan: Understood. We have the SOC2 bundle ready; I'll attach it after the call. We also have our private control-plane option — that's what most customers use for EU residency.
[00:58] Priya: Quick technical note: for the on-prem pilot we typically bring a scaled-down cluster (2-4 GPU nodes) to validate latency and KV cache behavior — and we'll instrument tracing for token-level cost.
[01:10] Noah: Do you support the same model catalog on private? We run mixed workloads: short chatQL and longer document summarization, so sequence length variance matters.
[01:18] Priya: Yes, same catalog; we recommend a quantized variant for cost-sensitive routes. The runtime supports prefix caching and continuous batching, so we can tune for your traffic profile.
[01:28] Lina: From procurement: we need pricing bands for committed vs burst usage and an example amendment for a 12-month extension. Also PO lead times.
[01:38] Samir: We'll share a term-sheet with committed capacity tiers and burst buffers. For renewals we usually do 10-15% headroom; for private/on-prem there's an initial pilot uplift that can be credited back if you convert to a reserved pool.
[01:52] Ellen: On encryption: can we use our KMS? HSM? We can't have keys managed in Redwood's shared account.
[02:00] Priya: Yes — we integrate with customer KMS in the private deployment model. HSM support is part of the enterprise package; we'll include the integration notes in the security bundle.
[02:10] David: Great. Also latency targets — our product needs 95th percentile < 120ms for short chat flows, and batch summarization is allowed to be slower. Is that realistic with the EU private pilot?
[02:22] Priya: Short answers: yes, if we place the inference nodes in the same region as your frontend and we enable KV prefix caching for repeated prompts, we can hit sub 120ms 95p for 1-2k tokens. We'll validate during the pilot.
[02:34] Maya: There's also concern about fallback behavior: if private nodes are saturated, will requests route to hosted and does that break residency?
[02:42] Jordan: Good callout. Our smart routing can be policy-based — you can force strict-local or allow cross-region fallback. For PHI, we'd recommend strict-local failover to a compatible private in another EU AZ, not to hosted.
[02:58] Noah: How do you handle KV cache warming when we roll a new model version? We saw cold-start hits previously with another vendor.
[03:06] Priya: We have warmup hooks to prefill caches from a snapshot and can seed frequently-used prefixes. We'll show the runbook during the pilot.
[03:14] Speaker 1: (muffled) sorry, can you repeat the cache part?
[03:18] Priya: Sure — we snapshot active keys and replay them on deploy so the productivity drop is minimal.
[03:24] Samir: Back to commercial — Maya, do you have a preferred extension length? 12 vs 24 months changes pricing.
[03:31] Maya: Leadership prefers 12 with an option to extend at renewal if performance and cost meet targets — given budget cycles.
[03:38] Samir: Noted. We'll draft a 12-month amendment with an optional conversion credit clause for pilot to reserved capacity.
[03:46] Lina: Payment terms: Net30 is standard. If we commit higher capacity, can we discuss Net45 or installments?
[03:53] Samir: We can explore installments for larger deals; I'll flag finance. Net30 default, but open to terming in the amendment.
[04:02] Ellen: A security question — audit logs: do you provide immutable audit trails and retention controls per customer? We need 7 years for some artifacts.
[04:11] Jordan: Yes, for private we can configure retention and exports to your logging stack. We'll include sample retention configs.
[04:20] David: Any chance to run a small performance comparison vs MegaCloudAI — they quoted lower per-token but didn't include caching advantage.
[04:28] Priya: We can run a benchmark focusing on cost-per-effective-token considering caching and batching — sometimes headline per-token is misleading. We'll include the assumptions in the spreadsheet.
[04:38] Maya: Timeline check: if we sign the amendment by 7/23, when can pilot start?
[04:44] Priya: Pilot kickoff week of 7/27 if lab and networking pre-reqs are ready. We need a signed access form and a small lab network range by 7/21.
[04:52] Noah: Lab network — we prefer to keep it on our VPN with a jump-host. Is that supported?
[04:58] Priya: Yes, we support connectivity via VPN peering or private interconnect — we have a checklist. We'll share details.
[05:06] Samir: Ok let's surface risks — anything else from Cedarbrook side?
[05:11] Ellen: Compliance sign-off is the big one. We'll need the SOC2 evidence and the KMS integration plan to satisfy internal auditors.
[05:19] Maya: Also want to confirm model pinning/rollout controls so we don't accidentally ship a different model in prod mid-pilot.
[05:26] Priya: Rollouts are controllable via the control plane — canary and A/B, with automatic fallback to pinned variant. We'll demo that.
[05:33] David: Great. One last question on observability: token-level cost breakdown per route — can we get that into our billing exports?
[05:40] Jordan: Yes — we can push token-level tags and outputs into your billing export. We'll include an example schema.
[05:47] Lina: Procurement asks for a PO template and invoice examples to run internally.
[05:52] Samir: We'll attach both.
[05:56] Priya: Any blockers on technical prerequisites?
[05:59] Noah: We'll provide an isolated subnet and test certs. Need to know required IP ranges and certificate formats.
[06:06] Priya: We'll send the exact network checklist in the next email.
[06:10] (brief pause)
[06:14] Samir: Ok time to wrap — recap and actions. Jordan to send SOC2 and security bundle; Priya to schedule pilot and runbench; Samir to draft amendment; Maya to confirm PO timeline. Any additional items?
[06:26] Maya: Please include a short one-pager on fallback policy options for PHI handling.
[06:31] Jordan: Noted, will include.
[06:34] Lina: If we get finance sign-off early, we might ask for a short pilot-to-reserved credit.
[06:39] Samir: That's on the table — we'll include language.
[06:42] Maya: Thanks everyone.
[06:44] Samir: Thanks all, we'll follow up with the doc pack tomorrow.
[06:47] (call ends)

Attachments/Links referenced: [Redwood SOC2 package link], [Private deployment network checklist], [pilot benchmark spreadsheet (placeholder link)], [sample term-sheet v2].
