text:
Header:
Date: 2026-07-14
Start: 15:00 UTC
Duration: ~52 minutes
Attendees: Samir Shah (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Jordan Lee (Redwood CSM), Maya Patel (Cedarbrook), David Rios (Cedarbrook CTO), Ellen Fox (Security), Noah Grant (SRE), Lina Torres (Procurement), Speaker 1 (intern)

Summary: Quick check-in to align on contract extension length, committed capacity, and plan for Private (VPC/on-prem) pilot in the EU cluster. Security artifacts and pilot logistics to be shared. Customer raised latency and data residency constraints for EU patients.

Topics: contract term / committed capacity, private edge on-prem pilot, SOC2/KMS, latency vs cost tradeoffs, timeline for PO and pilot kickoff.

Transcript:
[00:00] Samir: Hey everyone, thanks for joining — we'll keep this tight, fifteen to fifty two wait, sorry, fifty two minutes ish. Quick intros — Jordan, Priya and I'm Samir from Redwood.
[00:12] Maya: Hi all — Maya here, I'll run this on our side. We've got David, Ellen and Noah on the line too.
[00:20] Samir: Awesome. The goal today is to confirm the extension term and go over the private/on-prem pilot plan in the EU region, since that's the blocker for your security and residency folks.
[00:29] David: Yep, main ask from execs is predictable unit costs and having EU-resident inference for PHI-adjacent data. We don't need full air gap but VPC with customer-managed keys and audit logs.
[00:41] Ellen: Also need to validate KMS/HSM options and retention policies. We do regular audits and SOC two is non-negotiable.
[00:48] Jordan: Understood. We have the SOC2 bundle ready; I'll attach it after the call. We also have our private control-plane option — that's what most customers use for EU residency.
[00:58] Priya: Quick technical note: for the on-prem pilot we typically bring a scaled-down cluster (2-4 GPU nodes) to validate latency and KV cache behavior — and we'll instrument tracing for token-level cost.
[01:10] Noah: Do you support the same model catalog on private? We run mixed workloads: short chatQL and longer document summarization, so sequence length variance matters.
[01:18] Priya: Yes, same catalog; we recommend a quantized variant for cost-sensitive routes. The runtime supports prefix caching and continuous batching, so we can tune for your traffic profile.
[01:28] Lina: From procurement: we need pricing bands for committed vs burst usage and an example amendment for a 12-month extension. Also PO lead times.
[01:38] Samir: We'll share a term-sheet with committed capacity tiers and burst buffers. For renewals we usually do 10-15% headroom; for private/on-prem there's an initial pilot uplift that can be credited back if you convert to a reserved pool.
[01:52] Ellen: On encryption: can we use our KMS? HSM? We can't have keys managed in Redwood's shared account.
[02:00] Priya: Yes — we integrate with customer KMS in the private deployment model. HSM support is part of the enterprise package; we'll include the integration notes in the security bundle.
[02:10] David: Great. Also latency targets — our product needs 95th percentile < 120ms for short chat flows, and batch summarization is allowed to be slower. Is that realistic with the EU private pilot?
[02:22] Priya: Short answers: yes, if we place the inference nodes in the same region as your frontend and we enable KV prefix caching for repeated prompts, we can hit sub 120ms 95p for 1-2k tokens. We'll validate during the pilot.
[02:34] Maya: There's also concern about fallback behavior: if private nodes are saturated, will requests route to hosted and does that break residency?
[02:42] Jordan: Good callout. Our smart routing can be policy-based — you can force strict-local or allow cross-region fallback. For PHI, we'd recommend strict-local failover to a compatible private in another EU AZ, not to hosted.
[02:58] Noah: How do you handle KV cache warming when we roll a new model version? We saw cold-start hits previously with another vendor.
[03:06] Priya: We have warmup hooks to prefill caches from a snapshot and can seed frequently-used prefixes. We'll show the runbook during the pilot.
[03:14] Speaker 1: (muffled) sorry, can you repeat the cache part?
[03:18] Priya: Sure — we snapshot active keys and replay them on deploy so the productivity drop is minimal.
[03:24] Samir: Back to commercial — Maya, do you have a preferred extension length? 12 vs 24 months changes pricing.
[03:31] Maya: Leadership prefers 12 with an option to extend at renewal if performance and cost meet targets — given budget cycles.
[03:38] Samir: Noted. We'll draft a 12-month amendment with an optional conversion credit clause for pilot to reserved capacity.
[03:46] Lina: Payment terms: Net30 is standard. If we commit higher capacity, can we discuss Net45 or installments?
[03:53] Samir: We can explore installments for larger deals; I'll flag finance. Net30 default, but open to terming in the amendment.
[04:02] Ellen: A security question — audit logs: do you provide immutable audit trails and retention controls per customer? We need 7 years for some artifacts.
[04:11] Jordan: Yes, for private we can configure retention and exports to your logging stack. We'll include sample retention configs.
[04:20] David: Any chance to run a small performance comparison vs MegaCloudAI — they quoted lower per-token but didn't include caching advantage.
[04:28] Priya: We can run a benchmark focusing on cost-per-effective-token considering caching and batching — sometimes headline per-token is misleading. We'll include the assumptions in the spreadsheet.
[04:38] Maya: Timeline check: if we sign the amendment by 7/23, when can pilot start?
[04:44] Priya: Pilot kickoff week of 7/27 if lab and networking pre-reqs are ready. We need a signed access form and a small lab network range by 7/21.
[04:52] Noah: Lab network — we prefer to keep it on our VPN with a jump-host. Is that supported?
[04:58] Priya: Yes, we support connectivity via VPN peering or private interconnect — we have a checklist. We'll share details.
[05:06] Samir: Ok let's surface risks — anything else from Cedarbrook side?
[05:11] Ellen: Compliance sign-off is the big one. We'll need the SOC2 evidence and the KMS integration plan to satisfy internal auditors.
[05:19] Maya: Also want to confirm model pinning/rollout controls so we don't accidentally ship a different model in prod mid-pilot.
[05:26] Priya: Rollouts are controllable via the control plane — canary and A/B, with automatic fallback to pinned variant. We'll demo that.
[05:33] David: Great. One last question on observability: token-level cost breakdown per route — can we get that into our billing exports?
[05:40] Jordan: Yes — we can push token-level tags and outputs into your billing export. We'll include an example schema.
[05:47] Lina: Procurement asks for a PO template and invoice examples to run internally.
[05:52] Samir: We'll attach both.
[05:56] Priya: Any blockers on technical prerequisites?
[05:59] Noah: We'll provide an isolated subnet and test certs. Need to know required IP ranges and certificate formats.
[06:06] Priya: We'll send the exact network checklist in the next email.
[06:10] (brief pause)
[06:14] Samir: Ok time to wrap — recap and actions. Jordan to send SOC2 and security bundle; Priya to schedule pilot and runbench; Samir to draft amendment; Maya to confirm PO timeline. Any additional items?
[06:26] Maya: Please include a short one-pager on fallback policy options for PHI handling.
[06:31] Jordan: Noted, will include.
[06:34] Lina: If we get finance sign-off early, we might ask for a short pilot-to-reserved credit.
[06:39] Samir: That's on the table — we'll include language.
[06:42] Maya: Thanks everyone.
[06:44] Samir: Thanks all, we'll follow up with the doc pack tomorrow.
[06:47] (call ends)

Attachments/Links referenced: [Redwood SOC2 package link], [Private deployment network checklist], [pilot benchmark spreadsheet (placeholder link)], [sample term-sheet v2].

Notes on audio/transcription: occasional noise and overlap; some proper nouns (e.g., vendor names) may be mis-transcribed.

title: Flow stability & throughput sprint sync - Lumenix pilot
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-07-01 10:00 PDT
Duration: ~54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Lee (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Marta Ruiz (Lumenix Platform), Devon Chang (Lumenix SRE), Aisha Patel (Lumenix CTO)

Summary:
Quick sync to review last 48h pilot runs, tune continuous batching and concurrency targets, and agree next steps for a 24h steady-state validation. Conversation touches on queueing behavior, KV cache warmup, and cost tradeoffs between host tiers.

Topics:
- Recent throughput runs and anomalies
- Continuous batching knob adjustments
- Concurrency targets vs latency SLOs
- Queue shaping and backpressure observed
- Next steps and deliverables

Action items (discussed in call): see metadata action_items field.

Transcript body:
[00:00] Jordan: Okay hey everyone, thanks for jumping on, hope — yeah — good morning Aisha, Marta, Devon. Quick agenda, we'll do a short recap of the two baseline runs we ran Friday and Sunday, then Priya will share her findings on batching and concurrency, and then we'll sync on the 24 hour run and responsibilities. Anything else to add?
[00:12] Aisha: No, that sounds good. I mostly want to make sure we can hit the 95th latency target under steady traffic.
[00:17] Marta: And also sanity check on the cost math; we're seeing a big gap between expected and observed tokens per GPU.
[00:22] Devon: Plus those spike drains we saw last night — queue depth climbed and led to one pod crash on staging.
[00:27] Jordan: Right, thanks. Priya, you're up.
[00:30] Priya: Cool, thanks. So quick remind: the pilot is on a mix of c5-like frontends and a Redwood Dedicated pool (g4-ish test nodes) with model variant rdx-2-medium quantized. I'll share the run deck after this.
[00:45] Priya: Run one (Fri) was a burst test — 10 minute ramp to 1.2k rps, target concurrency per worker set to 16. We saw average token throughput around 320k tokens/min but P95 latency climbed to 420 ms and queue depth went to ~120 requests for a short window.
[01:05] Priya: Run two (Sun night) was a paced ramp to a steady 600 rps for 2 hours, concurrency bumped to 32 to test batching; continuous batching was enabled with target batch window 20ms. That run had better average latency (P50 ~95ms) but p99 still had tails near 1.6s and occasional backpressure events.
[01:28] Marta: When you say continuous batching, we use the internal term flow? In our logs it shows as "batcher_thread" — sometimes it's picked up as batch windows of 40ms not 20, what's causing that drift?
[01:40] Priya: Yeah that's a good callout. We saw application-level jitter from their frontends: if request arrival is bursty, the batcher occasionally widens its collection window to avoid tiny batches; so the effective window varies.
[01:52] Devon: We also noticed cold KV cache hits the first 15 minutes of the run — warmup was poor. Some of our pods got restarted for unrelated infra churn and that flushed the cache.
[02:03] Priya: Important: KV cache 'warmup' matters a lot for tail latency. We instrumented a run where we pre-warmed keys and the p99 dropped by ~400ms.
[02:12] Aisha: Is the KV cache on the VM local SSD or network store? That impacts warmup time.
[02:17] Marta: It's on a mounted EBS-ish volume in staging, so slower than local NVMe.
[02:22] Priya: That explains it. For production we recommended local NVMe, but if the customer wants to stay with EBS we can adjust warmup and batching.
[02:30] Jordan: Let's talk about concurrency numbers. You bumped concurrency to 32 — what's the trade? CPU/GPU saturation?
[02:37] Priya: At 16 concurrency we had underutilized GPUs some of the time — batching didn't trigger enough. At 32 we forced larger batches which improved tokens/sec but increased p50 slightly; the P95/P99 tails improved only if KV cache was warm. There is a band of concurrency where we get best cost-per-token and still meet SLAs.
[02:58] Devon: Did you see GPU memory pressure at 32? We saw a few OOM-ish logs but they were actually retries — the runtime reclaimed KV cache memory under pressure.
[03:08] Priya: Right — not full OOM, but eviction churn. The runtime will start to evict older KV entries when memory crosses threshold which increases latency for requests needing that state.
[03:18] Jordan: Sounds like three levers: batching window, concurrency per worker, and cache warmup strategy. Any other levers?
[03:25] Priya: Also model quantization level — we used rdx-2-medium quant — moving to rdx-2-small (more quantized) reduces per-token latency but sometimes changes quality; and routing policy — having overflow to smaller model during surges keeps latency bounded.
[03:39] Marta: On quality, our product team wants <1% regression on rouge/whatever eval. We'll need to run llm-evals.
[03:46] Jordan: Noted. For this sprint we tune throughput; we'll gate model swap until we have eval results.
[03:51] Devon: Back to the queue crash: when queue depth hit 300, we saw one worker hit a healthcheck fail and k8s killed it. Is there a way Redwood's runtime can shed load gracefully?
[04:02] Priya: Yes, we have backpressure and graceful refusal policies. In current pilot config we had 'soft reject' disabled; enabling soft rejects with a small early-fail helps the orchestrator avoid hard crashes.
[04:13] Jordan: Can you enable that for the 24h run?
[04:16] Priya: Yep, we'll enable soft rejects and a max-queue threshold of 220 per worker. We'll also adjust autoscale burst threshold to allow one extra standby for quick spikes.
[04:25] Marta: Will that impact cost? Extra standby GPUs could add to hourly.
[04:29] Jordan: It's a trade — but cost of a single extra GPU for smoothing may be less than cost of customer downtime or pod crash. We'll run the numbers and show cost delta.
[04:37] Aisha: Regarding cost math — we need the per-token $ at steady-state 600 rps and at ramped 1.2k rps. Please show both with and without soft reject.
[04:45] Jordan: Noted. Priya, what's the recommended concurrency for the 24h run?
[04:49] Priya: My recommended starting point is concurrency 48 with continuous batching window target 25ms, soft reject enabled at queue 220, KV cache pre-warm script to run 15 minutes before traffic. That should hit tokens/sec target and keep P95 under 300ms assuming local NVMe; with EBS expect extra ~120ms additive.
[05:07] Devon: Is 48 per worker safe memory-wise on our nodes?
[05:10] Priya: On the current GB of GPU it pushes memory but should be within limits if we increase kv max entries cap and nuke entries older than X. I'll include the exact kernel mem numbers in the run notes.
[05:22] Marta: You mentioned pre-warm script — can you describe what you need from us to pre-warm appropriately?
[05:27] Priya: We'll need a sample of top 5k keys or request fingerprints plus sample payloads so the script can hit the same prefixes. If Lumenix can provide the real traffic trace, we can build a warmup playback that mimics real arrival patterns.
[05:40] Marta: We'll deliver the trace — I'll upload to the shared drive by EOD tomorrow.
[05:44] Aisha: About the observed p99 spikes — any chance it's due to GC pauses in their frontends? We saw correlated JVM GC logs in the timestamp window.
[05:53] Devon: We should correlate. I'll share metrics from the frontend producer. But we also saw network retransmits.
[05:58] Priya: That would explain the batcher widening — if frontends pause then resume with a burst, batch windows extend. Continuous batching tries to adapt but it can't fix upstream jitter.
[06:09] Jordan: So we have three domains: Redwood runtime knobs, infra (KV location / disk), and frontend request shaping. We'll address all three for the 24h run.
[06:17] Priya: For instrumentation, I'm adding more traces: per-batch sizes, per-request latencies with KV hit/miss tag, and queue wait time. That will help triage p99.
[06:27] Jordan: Good. Any security or compliance notes before we run longer tests?
[06:31] Marta: We need to ensure logs don't include PII; we've been redacting request bodies in staging but double-check the warmup script won't leak customer data.
[06:39] Priya: Understood; warmup will use synthetic hashed keys, not real payloads, unless you explicitly provide sanitized samples. We'll default to hashed fingerprints.
[06:46] Aisha: Also, can Redwood's metrics export to our Grafana stack? We want to keep a single pane.
[06:52] Jordan: Yes, we have a metrics exporter and can add a Grafana dashboard link. Priya will share the dashboard JSON.
[06:58] Priya: I'll share dashboards plus a lightweight prometheus scrape config.
[07:02] Devon: On autoscaling, can you explain the burst policy again? We had an incident where scale-up lagged ~45s and that exacerbated queueing.
[07:10] Priya: The default scale-up conservatively waits for sustained load for 30s; for pilot we can change to 10s sensitivity with a warm pool of one idle GPU. That reduces cost-effectiveness a bit but cuts queue spikes.
[07:23] Jordan: Let's capture two scenarios in the cost spreadsheet: aggressive scale (10s, 1 warm pool) and conservative scale (30s, no warm pool).
[07:32] Marta: Agreed. Also include impact of moving KV to local NVMe vs staying on EBS in cost delta.
[07:37] Priya: Will do.
[07:40] Jordan: Any blockers to running the 24h starting Friday?
[07:43] Marta: We need to ensure infra team will not rotate nodes during the run; I'll coordinate with ops. Also want a play-by-play Slack channel.
[07:50] Devon: We'll create a channel and have SRE on-call watch it.
[07:54] Priya: For the run parameters summary: concurrency 48, continuous batch target 25ms, soft-reject on at 220 queue, pre-warm 15m, metrics scrape to Lumenix Grafana, aggression scale 10s with 1 warm standby for the aggressive test. I'll post config and runbook after the call.
[08:13] Aisha: How will we verify quality drop? We need a quick lint on model outputs for a small prompt set.
[08:18] Priya: We'll run an automated eval on the shared testset every 6 hours and report regression; if we detect >1% delta we'll auto-fail the run. That will be integrated into the runbook.
[08:27] Jordan: Okay, let's talk timeline. Priya run and report due 7/3, Jordan SOW 7/2, Lumenix artifacts 7/2, deep dive week of 7/6. Anything else?
[08:36] Marta: Can you also include a note about fallback routing to smaller model during surge as an optional toggle? We may want to enable that if tails persist.
[08:44] Priya: Noted. We'll include fallback routing scenarios and the expected latency/cost numbers.
[08:48] Devon: Small ask—can we add a single-run sticky session test to validate KV locality per user? We saw cross-tenant cache misses.
[08:56] Priya: Yes, we can add a sticky-session experiment in the 24h run matrix.
[09:00] Jordan: Great. Quick recap from me: Redwood will provide run config, runbook, dashboards, and Priya will execute the 24h steady-state and share results. Lumenix will provide traffic traces and open Slack channel and ensure nodes won't be rotated. We'll aim to reconvene post-run to triage.
[09:14] Aisha: Sounds good.
[09:16] Marta: Thanks everyone.
[09:18] Devon: Thanks.
[09:20] Jordan: I'll send a calendar invite for a 7/6 deep-dive and drop the SOW and pricing later today.
[09:25] Priya: I'll get the run notes in the repo and kick off the warmup tests.
[09:29] Jordan: Ok wrap; thanks all.

End of transcript.

[07:23] Jordan: Let's capture two scenarios in the cost spreadsheet: aggressive scale (10s, 1 warm pool) and conservative scale (30s, no warm pool).
[07:32] Marta: Agreed. Also include impact of moving KV to local NVMe vs staying on EBS in cost delta.
[07:37] Priya: Will do.
[07:40] Jordan: Any blockers to running the 24h starting Friday?
[07:43] Marta: We need to ensure infra team will not rotate nodes during the run; I'll coordinate with ops. Also want a play-by-play Slack channel.
[07:50] Devon: We'll create a channel and have SRE on-call watch it.
[07:54] Priya: For the run parameters summary: concurrency 48, continuous batch target 25ms, soft-reject on at 220 queue, pre-warm 15m, metrics scrape to Lumenix Grafana, aggression scale 10s with 1 warm standby for the aggressive test. I'll post config and runbook after the call.
[08:13] Aisha: How will we verify quality drop? We need a quick lint on model outputs for a small prompt set.
[08:18] Priya: We'll run an automated eval on the shared testset every 6 hours and report regression; if we detect >1% delta we'll auto-fail the run. That will be integrated into the runbook.
[08:27] Jordan: Okay, let's talk timeline. Priya run and report due 7/3, Jordan SOW 7/2, Lumenix artifacts 7/2, deep dive week of 7/6. Anything else?
[08:36] Marta: Can you also include a note about fallback routing to smaller model during surge as an optional toggle? We may want to enable that if tails persist.
[08:44] Priya: Noted. We'll include fallback routing scenarios and the expected latency/cost numbers.
[08:48] Devon: Small ask—can we add a single-run sticky session test to validate KV locality per user? We saw cross-tenant cache misses.
[08:56] Priya: Yes, we can add a sticky-session experiment in the 24h run matrix.
[09:00] Jordan: Great. Quick recap from me: Redwood will provide run config, runbook, dashboards, and Priya will execute the 24h steady-state and share results. Lumenix will provide traffic traces and open Slack channel and ensure nodes won't be rotated. We'll aim to reconvene post-run to triage.
[09:14] Aisha: Sounds good.
[09:16] Marta: Thanks everyone.
[09:18] Devon: Thanks.
[09:20] Jordan: I'll send a calendar invite for a 7/6 deep-dive and drop the SOW and pricing later today.
[09:25] Priya: I'll get the run notes in the repo and kick off the warmup tests.
[09:29] Jordan: Ok wrap; thanks all.

End of transcript.

title: Sealed node updates & hardware constraints - Argentum Pharmaceuticals discovery
preview:
Meeting header:
- Date/Time: 2026-05-18 16:00 PDT
- Duration: 52m
- Attendees: Jordan Lee (Redwood AE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood SE), Dr. Elena Rossi (CTO - Argentum), Marcus Vale (Head of IT), Priya Shenoy (Data Platform Eng), Tom Alvarez (Regulatory), plus an unnamed attendee (connected late)

Auto-summary (auto-gen, may be rough):
Argentum is evaluating a sealed/on-prem private deployment for sensitive R&D NLP workloads. Main concerns: offline update workflow, hardware constraints (proprietary appliance spec, limited GPU models), auditability (immutable logs), and regulatory cadence for patching. They want a pilot to validate latency and update procedures inside their sealed environment.

Topics:
- Deployment topology options (single sealed cluster vs. regional replicated nodes)
- Offline update transport and media signing + verification
- Kernel/quantization behavior on older Ampere A100-equivalents and mixed GPU pools
- Maintenance windows and zero-touch rollback policies
- Audit logs, KMS/HSM, and attestation for third-party auditors

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan: hey everyone thanks for joining we got about fifty minutes on the calendar, quick intros, then we'll jump into your constraints and runbook needs.
[00:12] Elena: thanks Jordan, this is Elena, CTO at Argentum, we run a bunch of internal LLM features for R&D note summarization and IP extraction. Security and compliance are the blockers for any hosted services.
[00:22] Marcus: Marcus here, head of infra. we have a sealed datacenter network — air-gapped with strict crossconnects. only approved media for updates, offline usb or signed images via courier.
[00:30] Priya (Redwood): got it, thanks. quick question — what GPU models do you have in that sealed cluster? that determines a lot for quantization and kernel options.
[00:38] Marcus: mostly older 80GB Ampere plates, some 40GB units, and two NVLink nodes that are custom. we can't bring in vendor-provided binaries without FIPS/HSM signing.
[00:47] Jordan: okay, so constraints are hardware model variety + signature/attestation for updates. that's common. we do sealed deployments — we call it \"Private with sealed node\" — where the control plane sits outside but the inference runtime runs entirely on your nodes.
[01:02] Tom: sorry to jump, what's the difference between that and Redwood Dedicated? is that like managed?
[01:06] Priya (Redwood): good question. Dedicated is reserved capacity in our cloud. Private sealed is on-prem — customer-managed network and we hand over an appliance or container bundle that you run inside the air-gapped environment. there are different options for update cadence and signing.
[01:22] Elena: we need a strict offline update workflow. our auditors require chain-of-custody for binary transfers and an immutable log the auditor can pull. also we need the ability to pause automatic rollouts.
[01:34] Priya (Redwood): we support signed images and an update manifest you can host locally. there's an offline transfer tool — it's essentially an archive with a manifest and a detached signature. you verify via your HSM before shipping to nodes. also we can generate an audit bundle — logs + sig verification steps.
[01:50] Marcus: can the runtime run on our slightly older A100 equivalent? we've seen other runtimes crash with mixed F32/F16 paths.
[01:57] Priya (Redwood): short answer: yes, but there are nuance. our runtime picks kernels at startup based on capability detection; older cards may fall back to slower but stable kernels. we also have quantized paths that change memory usage. we'll need to run a compatibility bench.
[02:12] Speaker 6: (inaudible) slow for some of our long seqs — the KV cache is big, how do you handle that
[02:17] Jordan: KV cache management is per-process; we support sharding and spill-to-disk for very large sequences, but spill behavior in sealed env needs ops input — i.e., you must allow local scratch.
[02:29] Elena: scratch is allowed but it's encrypted at rest. also our patch window is strict — major updates only once a quarter unless there's a critical CVE.
[02:41] Priya (Redwood): that's fine, we can map your patch cadence to our rollouts. we also support canary policies where you can apply updates to a subset of nodes inside the sealed cluster using a labelled node policy.
[02:53] Marcus: how do you handle telemetry? we cannot phone-home usage stats.
[02:58] Priya (Redwood): telemetry can be disabled. we can provide offline usage and cost reports that you collect and then upload to Redwood control plane if you want. or we can leave the control plane completely out — though that removes remote orchestration.
[03:13] Tom: auditors will want RBAC, SSO integration, and immutable audit logs. do you integrate with our Okta and Splunk?
[03:19] Jordan: yes — for SSO we support SAML/Okta and SSO providers. For audit logs, the runtime emits structured logs and we have an ingest adapter for Splunk and local ELK stacks in air-gapped mode.
[03:33] Elena: what about KMS — we use an HSM-backed KMS for keys and signatures. can Redwood runtime talk to our HSM?
[03:41] Priya (Redwood): yes, we support KMS/HSM integrations but the exact integration pattern depends on whether you want keys to be local only or escrowed. in air-gapped mode we expect local-only keys. we ship a plugin for PKCS#11-based HSMs.
[03:54] Marcus: any special licensing for kernel changes or custom builds? we may need a build that includes our FIPS libs.
[04:01] Priya (Redwood): we can provide a source-compatible build under an enterprise agreement. final builds can be signed by your HSM. there is an engineering cadence for custom kernels — typically 4-6 weeks for a validated build.
[04:13] Jordan: ok let's talk pilot shape. a minimal POC: three-node sealed cluster, 2x 80GB nodes + 1x 40GB, run a small inference benchmark and an offline update test (deliver signed bundle, apply, rollback). Customer to bring hardware inventory and maintenance windows.
[04:32] Elena: that aligns with what we need. success criteria — P50 latency under 200ms for our summarization prompts, P95 under 600ms, throughput 8 concurrent sessions per 80GB card.
[04:45] Priya (Redwood): noted. we'll run a series of tests and share a benchmark sheet. for quantization, we recommend INT8 for 80GB where quality is acceptable, but we'll show you tradeoffs.
[04:56] Marcus: what about offline dependencies — Python wheels or CUDA runtimes? we can't accept unsigned third-party libs.
[05:02] Priya (Redwood): we provide a minimal runtime bundle that includes the CUDA-compatible libs; we can also vendor static builds that you've scanned. anything third-party can be re-signed by your HSM before deployment.
[05:13] Tom: on auditability — do you create a non-repudiable chain of custody for each binary that includes who signed it, timestamps, and hashes?
[05:21] Priya (Redwood): yes, the manifest includes hash, signer identity (certificate thumbprint), and a timestamp. we can also produce a human-readable provenance report for auditors.
[05:31] Elena: what's the expected support model during the pilot? we need on-call SE coverage during our initial week of tests.
[05:36] Jordan: we typically include a 2-week onboarding support window for pilots, SEs on-call M-F with escalation for criticals. for air-gapped installs we can do an on-site visit if required — that's an option.
[05:47] Marcus: any known blockers running in our network topology? we use strict egress rules and a jump host for transfers.
[05:53] Priya (Redwood): our offline transfer tool works over an approved jump host; we can also do signed USB transfers with a manifest verification step. we just need the ability to run the verifier in your environment.
[06:04] Elena: also want to understand perishability of prompts and data retention — our data governance requires no user prompt logs kept beyond 30 days and anonymization for retained inputs.
[06:13] Priya (Redwood): retention policies are configurable; logs can be scrubbed locally before creating an audit bundle. we can implement an automated retention policy in the runtime that purges logs older than X days.
[06:24] Jordan: quick note — some of the nouns in our docs might be misread by the ASR, so the transcript might show \"K V catch\" instead of KV cache, apologies if that shows up later.
[06:34] (laughter)
[06:36] Elena: that actually happened — the automated transcript called our HSM \"hsmash\" last time.
[06:40] Priya (Redwood): noted. 
[06:42] Jordan: wrapping up: next steps per the action items above. we'll send the sealed-node checklist and sample network diagram, plus SOC2/ISO artifacts. propose a follow-up deep dive next Tuesday to review kernel compatibility results and a timeline for the POC.
[06:58] Elena: sounds good. Marcus and I will share hardware inventory and blackout windows by Friday.
[07:04] Tom: I'll put together the regulatory checklist and circulate.
[07:08] Jordan: great, thanks everyone. bye.

(End of recording)

References / shared artifacts mentioned:
- Sealed node deployment checklist (to be sent)
- Air-gapped offline transfer manifest sample (to be sent)
- Benchmark sheet (shared during pilot)
- SOC2 / ISO attestations (Priya to share)

Note on transcript quality: medium — some names and technical terms may be slightly off due to ASR (e.g., \"KV cache\" as \"K V catch\", \"HSM\" as \"hsmash\").

title: Supplier Admittance Criteria and Billing Playbook Workshop
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-22
Start: 15:00 PT
Duration: 54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Lee, Priya Natarajan, Marco Alvarez (Redwood) / Amelia Torres, Samir Patel, Lily Zhang, Derek O'Neal (Horizon)

Summary (auto): Workshop to align supplier acceptance criteria (MSA/DPA redlines), billing playbook for pilot -> production, and SLA requirements for Dedicated vs Hosted. Action items captured at end.

Topics: MSA redlines, DPA exceptions, dedicated capacity SLAs, KMS/encryption, audit log retention, billing cadence and bridge funding for pilot, procurement timeline

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan Lee: Hey everyone thanks for joining, I'll kick off, um quick agenda - align on the MSA/DPA redlines you surfaced, talk about SLA and pricing playbook for the pilot and the potential path to reserved capacity.
[00:22] Amelia Torres: Great thanks Jordan - we got your availability, and Lily and Samir are on point for product and security. Our procurement team wants to make sure there are clear admittance criteria for any vendor, including evidence of SOC2, KMS integration, and a clean data residency plan.
[00:40] Priya Natarajan: Yep, understood. On the SOC2 piece we are SOC2 Type II in our hosted offering; for Private deployments we can integrate customer KMS or HSM and provide audit log exports. We can share our security pack.
[01:05] Samir Patel: When you say "audit log exports" is that push or pull? We need a way to pull logs into our SIEM daily, and retention needs to meet 7 years for some of our regulated data use cases. That sounded surprising on your public docs.
[01:25] Priya Natarajan: Good point. Currently we offer log streaming to customer endpoints for Dedicated and Private. For hosted we have an export API and can set up a syslog forwarder. Full 7-year retention is often an enterprise add-on, we can discuss options (we've seen customers ship to their long-term storage).
[01:50] Lily Zhang: Also from product: we need deterministic latency for certain inference flows, the difference between hosted and dedicated is material here. If dedicated is required we need clear SLOs and crediting.
[02:10] Jordan Lee: For Dedicated we publish p50/p95/p99 latency targets on the SLA matrix; if we miss SLOs we provide service credits and an escalation path. Priya will share the matrix after this call.
[02:26] Derek O'Neal: On billing: our finance team wants a clear playbook for pilot billing vs production commit. Do you invoice monthly? Do you allow short-term bridge funding or credits while procurement processes?
[02:40] Marco Alvarez: We typically invoice monthly for hosted usage. For pilots we can issue usage credits to be applied against the first invoice or extend a short-term PO-invoice arrangement for a limited pilot. For Dedicated we require a reserved capacity commitment which is usually a monthly or annual invoice depending on the contract.
[03:02] Amelia Torres: The MSA/DPA—our counsel flagged two items: indemnity caps and data deletion obligations. They want a clause that handles permanent deletion on contract termination and to define retention windows. We also have to be explicit about subprocessors.
[03:22] Jordan Lee: Understandable. We can agree to defined deletion windows and a subprocessor list disclosure. On indemnity, we often have standard caps tied to fees; we can look at language that's acceptable for mid-market customers, and for enterprise we negotiate. We'll send a redline per your counsel's notes.
[03:44] Samir Patel: KMS: we need customer-managed keys for production models that touch PHI. Does Redwood support bring-your-own-key (BYOK) for hosted?
[03:55] Priya Natarajan: Hosted BYOK in the strictest sense is limited; for production with PHI we recommend Private or Dedicated with KMS/HSM integration so keys never leave your control. For hosted we can offer envelope encryption and customer-managed keys via certain cloud KMS integrations, but it's different per cloud. We'll surface the exact options.
[04:20] Lily Zhang: Are there any model variant fallbacks? We want graceful degradation if capacity is constrained, e.g., fallback to a lower-cost quantized variant but preserve PII controls.
[04:33] Priya Natarajan: Yes, smart routing/fallbacks are supported. You can pin model families per route and set fallback policy to compatible variants; we also support per-route policies for privacy-sensitive requests. We'll document compatibility with the quantized variants.
[04:55] Speaker 1 (Amelia?): Sorry quick clarifying q - about audit logs - you mentioned streaming; is that included or an extra?
[05:04] Marco Alvarez: For Dedicated/Private streaming is included. For hosted it's available via an add-on or via our observability package; depends on retention needs.
[05:15] Derek O'Neal: On pricing approvals, our controller asks if per-token billing can be capped or if we can get a committed spend discount during ramp.
[05:28] Jordan Lee: We offer committed discount tiers for Dedicated or for multi-month commits on hosted. There's also a ramping commit structure—initial pilot credits then a consumption commit once you hit agreed throughput. We'll include a worked example in the playbook.
[05:45] Amelia Torres: Procurement also needs to know the approval gates: what internal documents do you need to onboard a vendor? For example we require: W9, MSA signed, DPA signed, insurance cert, SOC2 report. Anything else?
[06:04] Jordan Lee: That list is accurate. We can provide W9, SOC2 report, cyber insurance cert, and MSA/DPA redlines. For some customers we also provide a security questionnaire response (e.g., SIG) and a data flow diagram.
[06:20] Samir Patel: SIG fillouts take time. Can you pre-fill or provide a golden copy?
[06:26] Marco Alvarez: Yes we have a standard SIG/Lite pack we can pre-fill with our baseline answers and mark items for legal review. That tends to speed review.
[06:40] Lily Zhang: On SLA credits — can you explain the crediting calculation? Our legal wants a straightforward formula.
[06:48] Priya Natarajan: Our standard is a percentage of monthly recurring fees pro-rated to the period impacted—so failure at p99 threshold for a given month translates to X% credit. The detailed tiers are in the SLA matrix. We'll send the explicit formula.
[07:05] Derek O'Neal: Is there a minimum term for reserved capacity? We need flexibility for the first year.
[07:13] Jordan Lee: Minimums vary; common options are monthly commitments with a 3–6 month minimum for smaller pools, or annual discounts for 12-month commits. We can put a mid-hero option on the playbook.
[07:30] Amelia Torres: One more on DPA—data residency: we need all EU personal data to stay in EU regions. Does Redwood offer region pinning?
[07:40] Priya Natarajan: Yes region pinning for Hosted (EU regions) is supported for customers on certain enterprise tiers. Dedicated/Private gives full control over region and VPC placement. We'll call out region guarantees in the playbook and the DPA.
[07:58] Samir Patel: Your redlined DPA - do you support joint liability carveouts for subprocessors?
[08:06] Jordan Lee: We can disclose subprocessors and offer a review process; liability carveouts are typically handled case-by-case — we'll flag the standard approach in the redline and note which items require legal escalation.
[08:22] Lily Zhang: For the pilot we propose 8 weeks, limited to 200k tokens/day, with telemetry and a performance baseline. Can you commit to a benchmark run?
[08:34] Priya Natarajan: Yes, we can run a 72-hour benchmark on your representative prompt set on Dedicated test pods and provide p50/p95/p99 numbers plus cost per 1k tokens estimate. We'll need sample prompts and a target sequence length distribution.
[08:52] Derek O'Neal: Who signs the SOW for the pilot? Is it a statement of work referencing the MSA? How do you prefer to structure that contractually?
[09:03] Jordan Lee: Usually an SOW that references the master MSA works well; pilot SOW with scope, timeline, and acceptance criteria, plus a PO from you for any non-credited value. We'll draft a template.
[09:18] Amelia Torres: Good — that helps. On the procurement timeline, our target is to finish legal review and have a signed MSA/DPA by mid-May, procurement gating the purchase in the week after. Is that realistic?
[09:30] Jordan Lee: Yes that aligns - if we send redlines by Friday and you review, we can book a legal-to-legal call the first week of May and push to signatures mid-May.
[09:44] Marco Alvarez: I'll include an onboarding checklist with the playbook that lists required docs, security artifacts, and a recommended schedule for the first 60 days post-signature.
[09:56] Samir Patel: One operational question: during the pilot, can we enable scoped data deletion endpoints for test datasets? Our policy requires deletion APIs for PII data used in testing.
[10:08] Priya Natarajan: Yes we can enable deletion APIs on Dedicated and Private. For hosted pilots we can provide a defined process. We'll document retention and deletion controls in the DPA redline.
[10:21] Lily Zhang: If we move to production and want model fine-tuning with customer data, how is that handled under the DPA?
[10:30] Jordan Lee: That will be explicitly scoped — we treat fine-tuning differently: data usage constraints, opt-in for model training, and contract clauses for IP and model ownership. We'll surface standard clauses and options.
[10:45] Amelia Torres: Okay, sounds like we have a path. Quick ask: can you provide a short one-page checklist we can show procurement that lists "must-have docs" and the expected timeline?
[10:56] Marco Alvarez: Yup, I'll add that to the playbook and call it "Procurement Acceptance Checklist".
[11:05] Derek O'Neal: Last finance q - can you accept Net-60?
[11:09] Jordan Lee: For Net payment terms we can accommodate Net-45 typically; Net-60 requires legal/finance approval and sometimes a credit check. We'll note this as an exception path.
[11:20] Amelia Torres: Thanks — that covers most of our asks.
[11:23] Jordan Lee: Great, recap: Jordan to send redline + one-pager; Priya to share SLA matrix and benchmark process; Marco to provide procurement checklist and SIG pack; Derek will confirm dates and PO flow. Legal-to-legal meeting planned week of 5/4.
[11:40] Lily Zhang: Thanks everyone. We'll review the materials and get back with any further redlines.
[11:46] Jordan Lee: Thanks all. Meeting closed.

text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-22
Start: 15:00 PT
Duration: 54 minutes
Attendees: Jordan Lee, Priya Natarajan, Marco Alvarez (Redwood) / Amelia Torres, Samir Patel, Lily Zhang, Derek O'Neal (Horizon)

Summary (auto): Workshop to align supplier acceptance criteria (MSA/DPA redlines), billing playbook for pilot -> production, and SLA requirements for Dedicated vs Hosted. Action items captured at end.

Topics: MSA redlines, DPA exceptions, dedicated capacity SLAs, KMS/encryption, audit log retention, billing cadence and bridge funding for pilot, procurement timeline

Transcript:
[00:00] Jordan Lee: Hey everyone thanks for joining, I'll kick off, um quick agenda - align on the MSA/DPA redlines you surfaced, talk about SLA and pricing playbook for the pilot and the potential path to reserved capacity.
[00:22] Amelia Torres: Great thanks Jordan - we got your availability, and Lily and Samir are on point for product and security. Our procurement team wants to make sure there are clear admittance criteria for any vendor, including evidence of SOC2, KMS integration, and a clean data residency plan.
[00:40] Priya Natarajan: Yep, understood. On the SOC2 piece we are SOC2 Type II in our hosted offering; for Private deployments we can integrate customer KMS or HSM and provide audit log exports. We can share our security pack.
[01:05] Samir Patel: When you say "audit log exports" is that push or pull? We need a way to pull logs into our SIEM daily, and retention needs to meet 7 years for some of our regulated data use cases. That sounded surprising on your public docs.
[01:25] Priya Natarajan: Good point. Currently we offer log streaming to customer endpoints for Dedicated and Private. For hosted we have an export API and can set up a syslog forwarder. Full 7-year retention is often an enterprise add-on, we can discuss options (we've seen customers ship to their long-term storage).
[01:50] Lily Zhang: Also from product: we need deterministic latency for certain inference flows, the difference between hosted and dedicated is material here. If dedicated is required we need clear SLOs and crediting.
[02:10] Jordan Lee: For Dedicated we publish p50/p95/p99 latency targets on the SLA matrix; if we miss SLOs we provide service credits and an escalation path. Priya will share the matrix after this call.
[02:26] Derek O'Neal: On billing: our finance team wants a clear playbook for pilot billing vs production commit. Do you invoice monthly? Do you allow short-term bridge funding or credits while procurement processes?
[02:40] Marco Alvarez: We typically invoice monthly for hosted usage. For pilots we can issue usage credits to be applied against the first invoice or extend a short-term PO-invoice arrangement for a limited pilot. For Dedicated we require a reserved capacity commitment which is usually a monthly or annual invoice depending on the contract.
[03:02] Amelia Torres: The MSA/DPA—our counsel flagged two items: indemnity caps and data deletion obligations. They want a clause that handles permanent deletion on contract termination and to define retention windows. We also have to be explicit about subprocessors.
[03:22] Jordan Lee: Understandable. We can agree to defined deletion windows and a subprocessor list disclosure. On indemnity, we often have standard caps tied to fees; we can look at language that's acceptable for mid-market customers, and for enterprise we negotiate. We'll send a redline per your counsel's notes.
[03:44] Samir Patel: KMS: we need customer-managed keys for production models that touch PHI. Does Redwood support bring-your-own-key (BYOK) for hosted?
[03:55] Priya Natarajan: Hosted BYOK in the strictest sense is limited; for production with PHI we recommend Private or Dedicated with KMS/HSM integration so keys never leave your control. For hosted we can offer envelope encryption and customer-managed keys via certain cloud KMS integrations, but it's different per cloud. We'll surface the exact options.
[04:20] Lily Zhang: Are there any model variant fallbacks? We want graceful degradation if capacity is constrained, e.g., fallback to a lower-cost quantized variant but preserve PII controls.
[04:33] Priya Natarajan: Yes, smart routing/fallbacks are supported. You can pin model families per route and set fallback policy to compatible variants; we also support per-route policies for privacy-sensitive requests. We'll document compatibility with the quantized variants.
[04:55] Speaker 1 (Amelia?): Sorry quick clarifying q - about audit logs - you mentioned streaming; is that included or an extra?
[05:04] Marco Alvarez: For Dedicated/Private streaming is included. For hosted it's available via an add-on or via our observability package; depends on retention needs.
[05:15] Derek O'Neal: On pricing approvals, our controller asks if per-token billing can be capped or if we can get a committed spend discount during ramp.
[05:28] Jordan Lee: We offer committed discount tiers for Dedicated or for multi-month commits on hosted. There's also a ramping commit structure—initial pilot credits then a consumption commit once you hit agreed throughput. We'll include a worked example in the playbook.
[05:45] Amelia Torres: Procurement also needs to know the approval gates: what internal documents do you need to onboard a vendor? For example we require: W9, MSA signed, DPA signed, insurance cert, SOC2 report. Anything else?
[06:04] Jordan Lee: That list is accurate. We can provide W9, SOC2 report, cyber insurance cert, and MSA/DPA redlines. For some customers we also provide a security questionnaire response (e.g., SIG) and a data flow diagram.
[06:20] Samir Patel: SIG fillouts take time. Can you pre-fill or provide a golden copy?
[06:26] Marco Alvarez: Yes we have a standard SIG/Lite pack we can pre-fill with our baseline answers and mark items for legal review. That tends to speed review.
[06:40] Lily Zhang: On SLA credits — can you explain the crediting calculation? Our legal wants a straightforward formula.
[06:48] Priya Natarajan: Our standard is a percentage of monthly recurring fees pro-rated to the period impacted—so failure at p99 threshold for a given month translates to X% credit. The detailed tiers are in the SLA matrix. We'll send the explicit formula.
[07:05] Derek O'Neal: Is there a minimum term for reserved capacity? We need flexibility for the first year.
[07:13] Jordan Lee: Minimums vary; common options are monthly commitments with a 3–6 month minimum for smaller pools, or annual discounts for 12-month commits. We can put a mid-hero option on the playbook.
[07:30] Amelia Torres: One more on DPA—data residency: we need all EU personal data to stay in EU regions. Does Redwood offer region pinning?
[07:40] Priya Natarajan: Yes region pinning for Hosted (EU regions) is supported for customers on certain enterprise tiers. Dedicated/Private gives full control over region and VPC placement. We'll call out region guarantees in the playbook and the DPA.
[07:58] Samir Patel: Your redlined DPA - do you support joint liability carveouts for subprocessors?
[08:06] Jordan Lee: We can disclose subprocessors and offer a review process; liability carveouts are typically handled case-by-case — we'll flag the standard approach in the redline and note which items require legal escalation.
[08:22] Lily Zhang: For the pilot we propose 8 weeks, limited to 200k tokens/day, with telemetry and a performance baseline. Can you commit to a benchmark run?
[08:34] Priya Natarajan: Yes, we can run a 72-hour benchmark on your representative prompt set on Dedicated test pods and provide p50/p95/p99 numbers plus cost per 1k tokens estimate. We'll need sample prompts and a target sequence length distribution.
[08:52] Derek O'Neal: Who signs the SOW for the pilot? Is it a statement of work referencing the MSA? How do you prefer to structure that contractually?
[09:03] Jordan Lee: Usually an SOW that references the master MSA works well; pilot SOW with scope, timeline, and acceptance criteria, plus a PO from you for any non-credited value. We'll draft a template.
[09:18] Amelia Torres: Good — that helps. On the procurement timeline, our target is to finish legal review and have a signed MSA/DPA by mid-May, procurement gating the purchase in the week after. Is that realistic?
[09:30] Jordan Lee: Yes that aligns - if we send redlines by Friday and you review, we can book a legal-to-legal call the first week of May and push to signatures mid-May.
[09:44] Marco Alvarez: I'll include an onboarding checklist with the playbook that lists required docs, security artifacts, and a recommended schedule for the first 60 days post-signature.
[09:56] Samir Patel: One operational question: during the pilot, can we enable scoped data deletion endpoints for test datasets? Our policy requires deletion APIs for PII data used in testing.
[10:08] Priya Natarajan: Yes we can enable deletion APIs on Dedicated and Private. For hosted pilots we can provide a defined process. We'll document retention and deletion controls in the DPA redline.
[10:21] Lily Zhang: If we move to production and want model fine-tuning with customer data, how is that handled under the DPA?
[10:30] Jordan Lee: That will be explicitly scoped — we treat fine-tuning differently: data usage constraints, opt-in for model training, and contract clauses for IP and model ownership. We'll surface standard clauses and options.
[10:45] Amelia Torres: Okay, sounds like we have a path. Quick ask: can you provide a short one-page checklist we can show procurement that lists "must-have docs" and the expected timeline?
[10:56] Marco Alvarez: Yup, I'll add that to the playbook and call it "Procurement Acceptance Checklist".
[11:05] Derek O'Neal: Last finance q - can you accept Net-60?
[11:09] Jordan Lee: For Net payment terms we can accommodate Net-45 typically; Net-60 requires legal/finance approval and sometimes a credit check. We'll note this as an exception path.
[11:20] Amelia Torres: Thanks — that covers most of our asks.
[11:23] Jordan Lee: Great, recap: Jordan to send redline + one-pager; Priya to share SLA matrix and benchmark process; Marco to provide procurement checklist and SIG pack; Derek will confirm dates and PO flow. Legal-to-legal meeting planned week of 5/4.
[11:40] Lily Zhang: Thanks everyone. We'll review the materials and get back with any further redlines.
[11:46] Jordan Lee: Thanks all. Meeting closed.
