title: QuillHealth — Private VPC connectivity & runtime workshop
preview:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-08
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 71 minutes
Attendees: Maya Cohen (Redwood AE), Diego Ramos (Redwood SE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood Solutions Eng), Sara Ibrahim (QuillHealth CTO), Eli Park (QuillHealth Platform Eng), Anita Gomez (QuillHealth Security)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
Redwood walked QuillHealth through network topology options for Redwood Private deployed into customer VPCs. Key topics covered: PrivateLink for control-plane connectivity, NAT and egress for container image pulls, Route53 resolver patterns for cross-account DNS, and runtime considerations — KV cache locality, batching strategies, and quantization tradeoffs. Security concerns (KMS, SOC2 artifacts, SSO) and next steps (send runbooks, run pilot) agreed.

Topics covered: PrivateLink vs VPCE, NAT hairpin and egress, subnets design, DNS resolver forwarding, image pull timing, KV cache warmup, batching sizing vs p95, quantization impact on throughput, on-prem air-gap considerations.

Action items (auto list):
- See action_items field for owner/due dates.

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call — we have Diego and Priya here from Redwood. Sara, Eli, Anita, Marcus from QuillHealth. How's everybody doing?
[00:11] Sara: All good, thanks. We're excited to dig into the connectivity details. We've got compliance deadlines so want this to be tight.
[00:19] Diego: Great. We'll start with a high-level topology then go into control-plane connectivity via PrivateLink and then runtime network patterns like NAT for image pulls and DNS. Feel free to interrupt.
[00:30] Eli: Quick note up front: our cluster is in a dedicated account, we want no internet inbound and minimal egress, but we do allow controlled egress for image pulls from our ECR.
[00:40] Anita: And from security: we need KMS integration details and proof of no plaintext logs leaving our VPC.
[00:47] Maya: Totally. We'll cover KMS and audit logging. Also, Private deployments can run with the control plane using interface endpoints — PrivateLink — or with a self-managed control-plane connector. Diego, want to start the network sketch?
[01:00] Diego: Sure. Picture three subnets: public NAT / jump, private for inference nodes, and restricted for management. For AWS specifically, we recommend interface endpoints — privatelink — from the customer account to Redwood's control-plane. That keeps control-plane traffic over AWS backbone. You avoid opening inbound rules.
[01:18] Eli: We heard about cross-account interface endpoints — do we need to allow anything in route tables other than default?
[01:25] Diego: Not much. You need to ensure the subnet's route table allows outbound to the ENIs created by the endpoint. Also security groups on the endpoint must allow the management CIDRs.
[01:36] Marcus: What about image pulls? Our nodes need to pull from ECR and from Redwood's private registry for runtime artifacts. We want to avoid making the inference subnets public.
[01:45] Priya: Typical approach — put a NAT gateway or NAT instance in a public subnet for egress. If you want zero public NAT, we can use VPC Egress-only with a proxy in the management subnet, or an outbound PrivateLink to a partner registry. But note: ECR requires a VPC endpoint for faster pulls and avoids routing through NAT.
[02:04] Eli: ECR VPC endpoint — is that interface-based too?
[02:07] Diego: Yes, ECR uses interface endpoints for API, and gateway for manifest, but AWS has several endpoints. Practically, adding ECR endpoints plus S3 endpoints (if you layer layers in S3) reduces NAT usage.
[02:18] Anita: But our security team wants to log egress and to enforce proxy scanning — can we funnel pulls through our proxy?
[02:24] Maya: You can. We often recommend a small management outbound proxy in a restricted subnet that does image scanning/TLS inspection, and keep inference nodes in private subnets with no public IPs. Redwood's agent images can be pulled by the management plane and distributed internally, or we can pull directly with mutual TLS to your registry.
[02:40] Sara: Ok. Now runtime: we care about latency. Our SLO: p95 < 120ms for 256-token responses. How does that interface with PrivateLink and NAT?
[02:50] Diego: Control-plane calls (instance health, model status) are low QPS and don't affect per-token latency. The data-plane (inference traffic) stays within your VPC when using Redwood Private — model inference runs on nodes in your subnets. The main latency sources are model compute and KV cache hits. Network adds a small amount: DNS, service-to-service TLS handshakes on first connection, and if you're doing cross-account storage calls.
[03:12] Priya: We see cold-kv-cache misses add 20–80ms depending on sequence length and cache distribution. Once warmed, prefix/KV cache hits can reduce token generation by up to 60–80% for repeated prompts.
[03:26] Eli: How is the KV cache stored? Local memory on the inference node? Shared?
[03:31] Diego: Two modes. Primary mode is local KV cache in RAM per model shard — fastest. We also offer a clustered cache layer for cross-node sharing, backed by a fast in-cluster redis-like store. Clustered caches help when you scale horizontally, but add network hops. For most customers in a single VPC we recommend local cache with sticky routing for best latency.
[03:52] Sara: Sticky routing meaning route requests for the same conversation to the same node?
[03:56] Diego: Exactly. Session affinity or consistent hashing — keeps K/V locality. That reduces KV misses.
[04:02] Anita: If we use local RAM cache, what happens on node replacement? Are there strategies to warm the cache?
[04:08] Priya: We do pre-warm tooling: replay small prompt sets to prime the cache after deploys, and we have an incremental background warmup that runs at low QPS. Also, we maintain a persisted snapshot of hot keysets that can be loaded on startup.
[04:22] Eli: Can we pre-seed from S3 and avoid network egress?
[04:26] Diego: Yes, snapshots can be stored in your private S3 bucket and loaded locally during boot. That avoids external egress.
[04:31] Sara: Good. Now quantization: we plan to use 4-bit quantized models to save cost. What latency tradeoffs are we looking at?
[04:39] Diego: Quantization reduces memory and improves throughput but might increase CPU/GPU kernel runtime for some ops. In our testing, int8 and 4-bit quantization give roughly 1.1–1.6x throughput improvements on Ampere and H100 family GPUs depending on kernel maturity. P95 latency can be slightly higher for very short requests due to packing/unpacking overheads, but for longer sequences you typically see wins. We recommend enabling quantization profiles in non-prod first and measuring end-to-end latency.
[05:06] Priya: Also note that quantization can change distribution of tokens and thus affect cache hit rates if the logits change subtly. We recommend running a small eval set.
[05:16] Anita: From security: anything with quantization that affects model integrity or reproducibility we should flag for audits.
[05:22] Maya: We'll provide a reproducibility note and guidance for evals.
[05:26] [05:27] Speaker 6 (Marcus): Sorry small interruption — AWS PrivateLink has limits on ENIs per AZ per account. Should we be concerned for large fleets?
[05:34] Diego: Good call. Yes, interface endpoints create ENIs, and there are account-level ENI limits. For large fleets we recommend cross-account endspoint consolidation or using a proxy/bastion model where one management cluster interacts with the control plane and nodes talk to that cluster locally. Also, we can use fewer endpoints and route through NAT in management account for control-plane traffic if necessary.
[05:53] Eli: What about DNS — we have Route53 private zones. How does Redwood name resolution work?
[05:58] Priya: We recommend Route53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints for cross-account DNS. You can set up forwarding rules so that requests to redwood-managed domains resolve to the interface endpoint IPs. This avoids public DNS resolution and keeps traffic internal.
[06:12] Anita: Are we able to audit DNS queries?
[06:15] Diego: You can enable Route53 query logging to CloudWatch or S3 and retain logs for audits. Additioanlly, our control-plane can be configured to forward logs to your SIEM — but we typically leave full log ownership to the customer.
[06:28] Sara: Can you walk through a typical control-plane handshake when a node starts up in our VPC?
[06:34] Diego: On node boot, agent initializes and attempts to register with control-plane via the PrivateLink endpoint. That is an HTTPS call over the ENI to the control-plane. It authenticates with mTLS and exchanges config — model pins, routing rules. After that it pulls model artifacts; if model artifacts are in your private S3 or ECR, pulls go via your VPC endpoints or NAT depending on setup. Agent reports health and opens a persistent TLS socket for command/control.
[07:00] Eli: Persistent TLS socket — would that be long-lived and does it keep sockets open to the control plane?
[07:05] Priya: Yes, it's long-lived and low QPS. It stays idle mostly. It's more of a management channel. Data-plane inference calls are local to the node; we don't tunnel inference traffic to the public control plane.
[07:16] Marcus: Suppose there's an AZ failure — what's the graceful fallback?
[07:20] Diego: We recommend a multi-AZ deployment with cross-AZ routing. If a node is lost we shift traffic to other nodes; sticky routing means some will miss KV cache and see a bump in latency. That's where the cache snapshots and background warmup limit p95 spikes. Also you can set canary weights so that during scale-up we route a small percentage of traffic to newly started nodes first.
[07:38] Maya: Right — Redwood's rollout policies support canary and A/B splits, and automatic fallback to hosted model variants if a private cluster becomes unhealthy (optional).
[07:47] Anita: About auditability: we need proof that no user data leaves our VPC. How can we guarantee that?
[07:54] Diego: For Redwood Private, data-plane inference stays in your VPC by default. The control-plane is only for telemetry and management; you can opt to host a private control-plane connector so even management traffic stays inside your network. We'll provide architecture diagrams and runbooks.
[08:09] Maya: Also we support customer-managed KMS for model encryption keys; logs can be configured to remain in your account.
[08:17] Anita: KMS integration — does Redwood create keys in our account or use a cross-account key?
[08:22] Priya: Ideally keys stay in your account and you can grant the service principal limited decrypt permissions. We walk through IAM trust boundaries in the runbook.
[08:31] Sara: Back to performance — can you share typical p50/p95 numbers for a medium model with batch sizes 1,4,8?
[08:38] Diego: Sure — rough numbers from our internal tests: single-shard medium model (256 context): batch=1 -> p50 45ms, p95 110ms; batch=4 -> p50 30ms, p95 90ms; batch=8 -> p50 25ms, p95 95ms (note p95 can move because head-of-line blocking and packing/unpacking overhead vary). These numbers depend heavily on GPU family and quantization. We'll send the benchmark sheet.
[09:03] Eli: Those p95s are close to our SLO. How about throughput — tokens per second?
[09:08] Diego: On A100-ish, a 70B quantized model might do ~300 tokens/s per GPU when well batched. With small batches under 1–4 tokens it's much lower. Cache hit rates dramatically change effective tokens/s for application-level throughput.
[09:25] Sara: So caching is key. If we have session affinity and local cache, what's the expected cache hit rate for our use-case — lots of templated prompts and patient notes redactions?
[09:35] Priya: For templated prompts, we see 60–85% token hit rates after warmup. For more free-form conversational logs it's lower. If you have stable prompts for redaction/NER, you'll get high reuse.
[09:46] Anita: Any data residency concerns with cache snapshots?
[09:49] Diego: Snapshots live in your S3 bucket under a path you control. We never send snapshots to external accounts unless you explicitly request managed snapshots.
[09:58] [10:00] Eli: Network ops question: do we need extra ENI quotas for agent-to-control plane?
[10:04] Diego: Yes monitor ENI quota. Each interface endpoint consumes ENIs per AZ. If you run many small subnets or cross-account endpoints, you should request ENI quota increases proactively.
[10:14] Marcus: Could we do a transit VPC approach with fewer endpoints?
[10:17] Diego: Absolutely. A transit model concentrates the endpoints in one account and proxies management traffic. It's a common pattern for scaled fleets. But that adds a hop and potential single point that must be hardened.
[10:27] Sara: Any gotchas with hairpin NAT — we recall some setups had issues where an endpoint in same subnet tried to reach a public NLB and hairpinned back and failed.
[10:35] Priya: Right — hairpin/NAT hairpin can break health checks. For interface endpoints you avoid hairpin. If you use an NLB in front of something in the same VPC you must ensure proper source/destination checks or use ALB with targets in separate subnets. We can provide a troubleshooting checklist.
[10:51] Anita: Regarding compliance: will Redwood provide SOC2 docs and an attestation for Private installs?
[10:56] Maya: We will share our SOC2 report and the private deployment compliance notes. For on-prem we provide a separate compliance pack explaining the responsibilities between customer and Redwood.
[11:06] Sara: On-prem tangent — if we ever run an air-gapped deployment, how do model updates work?
[11:11] Diego: For air-gapped, we provide a deployable artifact bundle and signed release images. You host the images on your internal registry. Updates are pulled as scheduled via offline transfer. We support an operator-run process for upgrades.
[11:23] Eli: For the pilot, we want to measure cold-starts, KV miss penalty, and p95 under node replacement. Can Redwood instrument those metrics?
[11:30] Priya: Yes — the Console exposes token-level latency, KV hit rates, and trace spans. You can hook our tracing into X-Ray or your APM. We'll give a recommended dashboard.
[11:40] Marcus: About tracing — does it leak request content?
[11:43] Diego: Tracing metadata is configurable. You can redact or suppress request text; only token-level timings are sent by default unless you opt in.
[11:51] Anita: One more question on TLS certs for PrivateLink — who rotates certs?
[11:55] Priya: For mutual TLS, the customer manages the server certs if you run private control-plane. For Redwood-managed control-plane, we manage certs and provide rotation notices. Keys can be stored in your KMS if you want customer-controlled keys.
[12:09] [12:10] Maya: Ok I'm going to pivot to runbook items — Diego will share perf sheet and recommended subnet sizing, Priya will share DNS resolver examples and sample CloudFormation for endpoints.
[12:22] Diego: For subnet sizing, consider NIC/socket limits and ephemeral port counts. We recommend larger CIDR blocks for inference subnets because GPUs open many connections for metrics and orchestration. For large fleets use /20 or /19 per AZ depending on scale.
[12:40] Eli: That seems big but fine; we can carve that.
[12:44] Sara: We want an explicit plan: pilot test steps and success criteria.
[12:49] Maya: Proposed pilot: (1) provision 2-AZ cluster with Redwood agent and PrivateLink connection; (2) run synthetic workload and capture p50/p95 + kv-hit rate; (3) run a modeled production workload for 48 hours; (4) validate security controls. Success criteria: p95 within 20% of your SLO, KV hit rate > 50% for templated workload, no unexpected egress.
[13:12] Eli: We'll need the Terraform modules and sample CF templates.
[13:15] Priya: We'll attach Terraform + CloudFormation snippets in the runbook.
[13:18] Marcus: On billing: do image pulls or snapshots incur data transfer costs to Redwood?
[13:23] Maya: No — for Private deployments, data plane egress is within your account. We bill for control-plane service fees and reserved capacity if you choose Dedicated. We'll send pricing notes.
[13:33] Anita: One more on SSO — we need SAML integration for admin access to Console.
[13:37] Diego: We support SAML/OIDC and can integrate with your IdP. For Private deployments, Console can be integrated with your SSO via SAML and SCIM provisioning for users.
[13:49] Sara: Great. Any concerns about cross-account IAM?
[13:52] Priya: We'll provide minimal IAM role examples. Principle of least privilege is recommended: a role for provisioning, a role for runtime with restricted S3 and ECR permissions.
[14:02] Eli: Looking again at runtime — you mentioned batching strategies. Can we control batching to favor latency or throughput?
[14:08] Diego: Yes batching policies are tunable per-route. For sync API calls you can set max wait and max batch sizes. For interactive latency-sensitive endpoints we typically set max wait 5–10ms and small max batch size. For high-throughput offline jobs we use bigger waits and larger batch sizes to maximize token throughput.
[14:25] Priya: An advanced pattern is adaptive batching: increase batch size during low QPS and shrink during bursty loads so p95 stays bounded. Redwood supports adaptive batching heuristics.
[14:36] Sara: Is there a chance batching causes request reordering or head-of-line issues for conversations?
[14:42] Diego: We preserve per-session ordering. Head-of-line can occur for mixed-length sequences in the same batch; we mitigate via sequence bucketing and padding heuristics.
[14:52] Marcus: Will we get per-token cost breakdowns?
[14:55] Maya: Yes Console has token-level cost and per-model breakdowns. For Private deployments, cost mapping works if you enable usage reporting to your account.
[15:04] Anita: On encryption at rest for model artifacts — is that handled via our KMS?
[15:09] Priya: Yes artifacts in S3 can be encrypted with your KMS key. We also sign artifacts.
[15:15] [15:16] Speaker: small side convo... (inaudible)
[15:25] Diego: A quick note on quantization again — depending on your HW, some kernels are slower for 4-bit until we use optimized fused kernels. We maintain a catalog of recommended quantization profiles per GPU family.
[15:37] Eli: Can Redwood run a small benchmark on our infra to validate profiles?
[15:40] Maya: Yes that's part of the pilot — Diego will run a perf sweep with your sample model and dataset.
[15:46] Diego: Also recommend running with our trace instrumentation so we can attribute latency to compute vs network vs cache.
[15:53] Sara: Ok let's recap next steps.
[15:55] Maya: Recap: send architecture checklist, Terraform/CloudFormation snippets, perf spreadsheet, SOC2 + compliance pack, and schedule pilot. Any other asks?
[16:05] Anita: Add sample IAM policies and KMS trust policy.
[16:08] Maya: Noted. We'll include those.
[16:11] Eli: We'll assemble a small pilot team and share the ECR/S3 details.
[16:15] Sara: Thanks everyone. That was super helpful.
[16:19] Maya: Great — we'll follow up over email and update the CRM.

(Several minutes of wrap-up, scheduling a follow-up call)

[70:45] End of meeting.

Notes on transcription quirks: occasional mis-hearings ("K-B cache" for KV cache), speaker labels sometimes generic, punctuation uneven.

References & artifacts mentioned (links):
- Pilot perf spreadsheet (link shared in chat)
- Private VPC runbook and Terraform snippets (to be attached)
- SOC2 & compliance pack (link to Redwood secure drive)
- Route53 resolver example (CF template snippet)
text:
Meeting header:
Date: 2026-04-08
Time: 16:00 UTC
Duration: 71 minutes
Attendees: Maya Cohen (Redwood AE), Diego Ramos (Redwood SE), Priya Natarajan (Redwood Solutions Eng), Sara Ibrahim (QuillHealth CTO), Eli Park (QuillHealth Platform Eng), Anita Gomez (QuillHealth Security)

Auto-summary (auto-generated, may be rough):
Redwood walked QuillHealth through network topology options for Redwood Private deployed into customer VPCs. Key topics covered: PrivateLink for control-plane connectivity, NAT and egress for container image pulls, Route53 resolver patterns for cross-account DNS, and runtime considerations — KV cache locality, batching strategies, and quantization tradeoffs. Security concerns (KMS, SOC2 artifacts, SSO) and next steps (send runbooks, run pilot) agreed.

Topics covered: PrivateLink vs VPCE, NAT hairpin and egress, subnets design, DNS resolver forwarding, image pull timing, KV cache warmup, batching sizing vs p95, quantization impact on throughput, on-prem air-gap considerations.

Action items (auto list):
- See action_items field for owner/due dates.

Transcript:
[00:00] Maya: Hey everyone, thanks for joining. Quick roll call — we have Diego and Priya here from Redwood. Sara, Eli, Anita, Marcus from QuillHealth. How's everybody doing?
[00:11] Sara: All good, thanks. We're excited to dig into the connectivity details. We've got compliance deadlines so want this to be tight.
[00:19] Diego: Great. We'll start with a high-level topology then go into control-plane connectivity via PrivateLink and then runtime network patterns like NAT for image pulls and DNS. Feel free to interrupt.
[00:30] Eli: Quick note up front: our cluster is in a dedicated account, we want no internet inbound and minimal egress, but we do allow controlled egress for image pulls from our ECR.
[00:40] Anita: And from security: we need KMS integration details and proof of no plaintext logs leaving our VPC.
[00:47] Maya: Totally. We'll cover KMS and audit logging. Also, Private deployments can run with the control plane using interface endpoints — PrivateLink — or with a self-managed control-plane connector. Diego, want to start the network sketch?
[01:00] Diego: Sure. Picture three subnets: public NAT / jump, private for inference nodes, and restricted for management. For AWS specifically, we recommend interface endpoints — privatelink — from the customer account to Redwood's control-plane. That keeps control-plane traffic over AWS backbone. You avoid opening inbound rules.
[01:18] Eli: We heard about cross-account interface endpoints — do we need to allow anything in route tables other than default?
[01:25] Diego: Not much. You need to ensure the subnet's route table allows outbound to the ENIs created by the endpoint. Also security groups on the endpoint must allow the management CIDRs.
[01:36] Marcus: What about image pulls? Our nodes need to pull from ECR and from Redwood's private registry for runtime artifacts. We want to avoid making the inference subnets public.
[01:45] Priya: Typical approach — put a NAT gateway or NAT instance in a public subnet for egress. If you want zero public NAT, we can use VPC Egress-only with a proxy in the management subnet, or an outbound PrivateLink to a partner registry. But note: ECR requires a VPC endpoint for faster pulls and avoids routing through NAT.
[02:04] Eli: ECR VPC endpoint — is that interface-based too?
[02:07] Diego: Yes, ECR uses interface endpoints for API, and gateway for manifest, but AWS has several endpoints. Practically, adding ECR endpoints plus S3 endpoints (if you layer layers in S3) reduces NAT usage.
[02:18] Anita: But our security team wants to log egress and to enforce proxy scanning — can we funnel pulls through our proxy?
[02:24] Maya: You can. We often recommend a small management outbound proxy in a restricted subnet that does image scanning/TLS inspection, and keep inference nodes in private subnets with no public IPs. Redwood's agent images can be pulled by the management plane and distributed internally, or we can pull directly with mutual TLS to your registry.
[02:40] Sara: Ok. Now runtime: we care about latency. Our SLO: p95 < 120ms for 256-token responses. How does that interface with PrivateLink and NAT?
[02:50] Diego: Control-plane calls (instance health, model status) are low QPS and don't affect per-token latency. The data-plane (inference traffic) stays within your VPC when using Redwood Private — model inference runs on nodes in your subnets. The main latency sources are model compute and KV cache hits. Network adds a small amount: DNS, service-to-service TLS handshakes on first connection, and if you're doing cross-account storage calls.
[03:12] Priya: We see cold-kv-cache misses add 20–80ms depending on sequence length and cache distribution. Once warmed, prefix/KV cache hits can reduce token generation by up to 60–80% for repeated prompts.
[03:26] Eli: How is the KV cache stored? Local memory on the inference node? Shared?
[03:31] Diego: Two modes. Primary mode is local KV cache in RAM per model shard — fastest. We also offer a clustered cache layer for cross-node sharing, backed by a fast in-cluster redis-like store. Clustered caches help when you scale horizontally, but add network hops. For most customers in a single VPC we recommend local cache with sticky routing for best latency.
[03:52] Sara: Sticky routing meaning route requests for the same conversation to the same node?
[03:56] Diego: Exactly. Session affinity or consistent hashing — keeps K/V locality. That reduces KV misses.
[04:02] Anita: If we use local RAM cache, what happens on node replacement? Are there strategies to warm the cache?
[04:08] Priya: We do pre-warm tooling: replay small prompt sets to prime the cache after deploys, and we have an incremental background warmup that runs at low QPS. Also, we maintain a persisted snapshot of hot keysets that can be loaded on startup.
[04:22] Eli: Can we pre-seed from S3 and avoid network egress?
[04:26] Diego: Yes, snapshots can be stored in your private S3 bucket and loaded locally during boot. That avoids external egress.
[04:31] Sara: Good. Now quantization: we plan to use 4-bit quantized models to save cost. What latency tradeoffs are we looking at?
[04:39] Diego: Quantization reduces memory and improves throughput but might increase CPU/GPU kernel runtime for some ops. In our testing, int8 and 4-bit quantization give roughly 1.1–1.6x throughput improvements on Ampere and H100 family GPUs depending on kernel maturity. P95 latency can be slightly higher for very short requests due to packing/unpacking overheads, but for longer sequences you typically see wins. We recommend enabling quantization profiles in non-prod first and measuring end-to-end latency.
[05:06] Priya: Also note that quantization can change distribution of tokens and thus affect cache hit rates if the logits change subtly. We recommend running a small eval set.
[05:16] Anita: From security: anything with quantization that affects model integrity or reproducibility we should flag for audits.
[05:22] Maya: We'll provide a reproducibility note and guidance for evals.
[05:26] [05:27] Speaker 6 (Marcus): Sorry small interruption — AWS PrivateLink has limits on ENIs per AZ per account. Should we be concerned for large fleets?
[05:34] Diego: Good call. Yes, interface endpoints create ENIs, and there are account-level ENI limits. For large fleets we recommend cross-account endspoint consolidation or using a proxy/bastion model where one management cluster interacts with the control plane and nodes talk to that cluster locally. Also, we can use fewer endpoints and route through NAT in management account for control-plane traffic if necessary.
[05:53] Eli: What about DNS — we have Route53 private zones. How does Redwood name resolution work?
[05:58] Priya: We recommend Route53 Resolver inbound/outbound endpoints for cross-account DNS. You can set up forwarding rules so that requests to redwood-managed domains resolve to the interface endpoint IPs. This avoids public DNS resolution and keeps traffic internal.
[06:12] Anita: Are we able to audit DNS queries?
[06:15] Diego: You can enable Route53 query logging to CloudWatch or S3 and retain logs for audits. Additioanlly, our control-plane can be configured to forward logs to your SIEM — but we typically leave full log ownership to the customer.
[06:28] Sara: Can you walk through a typical control-plane handshake when a node starts up in our VPC?
[06:34] Diego: On node boot, agent initializes and attempts to register with control-plane via the PrivateLink endpoint. That is an HTTPS call over the ENI to the control-plane. It authenticates with mTLS and exchanges config — model pins, routing rules. After that it pulls model artifacts; if model artifacts are in your private S3 or ECR, pulls go via your VPC endpoints or NAT depending on setup. Agent reports health and opens a persistent TLS socket for command/control.
[07:00] Eli: Persistent TLS socket — would that be long-lived and does it keep sockets open to the control plane?
[07:05] Priya: Yes, it's long-lived and low QPS. It stays idle mostly. It's more of a management channel. Data-plane inference calls are local to the node; we don't tunnel inference traffic to the public control plane.
[07:16] Marcus: Suppose there's an AZ failure — what's the graceful fallback?
[07:20] Diego: We recommend a multi-AZ deployment with cross-AZ routing. If a node is lost we shift traffic to other nodes; sticky routing means some will miss KV cache and see a bump in latency. That's where the cache snapshots and background warmup limit p95 spikes. Also you can set canary weights so that during scale-up we route a small percentage of traffic to newly started nodes first.
[07:38] Maya: Right — Redwood's rollout policies support canary and A/B splits, and automatic fallback to hosted model variants if a private cluster becomes unhealthy (optional).
[07:47] Anita: About auditability: we need proof that no user data leaves our VPC. How can we guarantee that?
[07:54] Diego: For Redwood Private, data-plane inference stays in your VPC by default. The control-plane is only for telemetry and management; you can opt to host a private control-plane connector so even management traffic stays inside your network. We'll provide architecture diagrams and runbooks.
[08:09] Maya: Also we support customer-managed KMS for model encryption keys; logs can be configured to remain in your account.
[08:17] Anita: KMS integration — does Redwood create keys in our account or use a cross-account key?
[08:22] Priya: Ideally keys stay in your account and you can grant the service principal limited decrypt permissions. We walk through IAM trust boundaries in the runbook.
[08:31] Sara: Back to performance — can you share typical p50/p95 numbers for a medium model with batch sizes 1,4,8?
[08:38] Diego: Sure — rough numbers from our internal tests: single-shard medium model (256 context): batch=1 -> p50 45ms, p95 110ms; batch=4 -> p50 30ms, p95 90ms; batch=8 -> p50 25ms, p95 95ms (note p95 can move because head-of-line blocking and packing/unpacking overhead vary). These numbers depend heavily on GPU family and quantization. We'll send the benchmark sheet.
[09:03] Eli: Those p95s are close to our SLO. How about throughput — tokens per second?
[09:08] Diego: On A100-ish, a 70B quantized model might do ~300 tokens/s per GPU when well batched. With small batches under 1–4 tokens it's much lower. Cache hit rates dramatically change effective tokens/s for application-level throughput.
[09:25] Sara: So caching is key. If we have session affinity and local cache, what's the expected cache hit rate for our use-case — lots of templated prompts and patient notes redactions?
[09:35] Priya: For templated prompts, we see 60–85% token hit rates after warmup. For more free-form conversational logs it's lower. If you have stable prompts for redaction/NER, you'll get high reuse.
[09:46] Anita: Any data residency concerns with cache snapshots?
[09:49] Diego: Snapshots live in your S3 bucket under a path you control. We never send snapshots to external accounts unless you explicitly request managed snapshots.
[09:58] [10:00] Eli: Network ops question: do we need extra ENI quotas for agent-to-control plane?
[10:04] Diego: Yes monitor ENI quota. Each interface endpoint consumes ENIs per AZ. If you run many small subnets or cross-account endpoints, you should request ENI quota increases proactively.
[10:14] Marcus: Could we do a transit VPC approach with fewer endpoints?
[10:17] Diego: Absolutely. A transit model concentrates the endpoints in one account and proxies management traffic. It's a common pattern for scaled fleets. But that adds a hop and potential single point that must be hardened.
[10:27] Sara: Any gotchas with hairpin NAT — we recall some setups had issues where an endpoint in same subnet tried to reach a public NLB and hairpinned back and failed.
[10:35] Priya: Right — hairpin/NAT hairpin can break health checks. For interface endpoints you avoid hairpin. If you use an NLB in front of something in the same VPC you must ensure proper source/destination checks or use ALB with targets in separate subnets. We can provide a troubleshooting checklist.
[10:51] Anita: Regarding compliance: will Redwood provide SOC2 docs and an attestation for Private installs?
[10:56] Maya: We will share our SOC2 report and the private deployment compliance notes. For on-prem we provide a separate compliance pack explaining the responsibilities between customer and Redwood.
[11:06] Sara: On-prem tangent — if we ever run an air-gapped deployment, how do model updates work?
[11:11] Diego: For air-gapped, we provide a deployable artifact bundle and signed release images. You host the images on your internal registry. Updates are pulled as scheduled via offline transfer. We support an operator-run process for upgrades.
[11:23] Eli: For the pilot, we want to measure cold-starts, KV miss penalty, and p95 under node replacement. Can Redwood instrument those metrics?
[11:30] Priya: Yes — the Console exposes token-level latency, KV hit rates, and trace spans. You can hook our tracing into X-Ray or your APM. We'll give a recommended dashboard.
[11:40] Marcus: About tracing — does it leak request content?
[11:43] Diego: Tracing metadata is configurable. You can redact or suppress request text; only token-level timings are sent by default unless you opt in.
[11:51] Anita: One more question on TLS certs for PrivateLink — who rotates certs?
[11:55] Priya: For mutual TLS, the customer manages the server certs if you run private control-plane. For Redwood-managed control-plane, we manage certs and provide rotation notices. Keys can be stored in your KMS if you want customer-controlled keys.
[12:09] [12:10] Maya: Ok I'm going to pivot to runbook items — Diego will share perf sheet and recommended subnet sizing, Priya will share DNS resolver examples and sample CloudFormation for endpoints.
[12:22] Diego: For subnet sizing, consider NIC/socket limits and ephemeral port counts. We recommend larger CIDR blocks for inference subnets because GPUs open many connections for metrics and orchestration. For large fleets use /20 or /19 per AZ depending on scale.
[12:40] Eli: That seems big but fine; we can carve that.
[12:44] Sara: We want an explicit plan: pilot test steps and success criteria.
[12:49] Maya: Proposed pilot: (1) provision 2-AZ cluster with Redwood agent and PrivateLink connection; (2) run synthetic workload and capture p50/p95 + kv-hit rate; (3) run a modeled production workload for 48 hours; (4) validate security controls. Success criteria: p95 within 20% of your SLO, KV hit rate > 50% for templated workload, no unexpected egress.
[13:12] Eli: We'll need the Terraform modules and sample CF templates.
[13:15] Priya: We'll attach Terraform + CloudFormation snippets in the runbook.
[13:18] Marcus: On billing: do image pulls or snapshots incur data transfer costs to Redwood?
[13:23] Maya: No — for Private deployments, data plane egress is within your account. We bill for control-plane service fees and reserved capacity if you choose Dedicated. We'll send pricing notes.
[13:33] Anita: One more on SSO — we need SAML integration for admin access to Console.
[13:37] Diego: We support SAML/OIDC and can integrate with your IdP. For Private deployments, Console can be integrated with your SSO via SAML and SCIM provisioning for users.
[13:49] Sara: Great. Any concerns about cross-account IAM?
[13:52] Priya: We'll provide minimal IAM role examples. Principle of least privilege is recommended: a role for provisioning, a role for runtime with restricted S3 and ECR permissions.
[14:02] Eli: Looking again at runtime — you mentioned batching strategies. Can we control batching to favor latency or throughput?
[14:08] Diego: Yes batching policies are tunable per-route. For sync API calls you can set max wait and max batch sizes. For interactive latency-sensitive endpoints we typically set max wait 5–10ms and small max batch size. For high-throughput offline jobs we use bigger waits and larger batch sizes to maximize token throughput.
[14:25] Priya: An advanced pattern is adaptive batching: increase batch size during low QPS and shrink during bursty loads so p95 stays bounded. Redwood supports adaptive batching heuristics.
[14:36] Sara: Is there a chance batching causes request reordering or head-of-line issues for conversations?
[14:42] Diego: We preserve per-session ordering. Head-of-line can occur for mixed-length sequences in the same batch; we mitigate via sequence bucketing and padding heuristics.
[14:52] Marcus: Will we get per-token cost breakdowns?
[14:55] Maya: Yes Console has token-level cost and per-model breakdowns. For Private deployments, cost mapping works if you enable usage reporting to your account.
[15:04] Anita: On encryption at rest for model artifacts — is that handled via our KMS?
[15:09] Priya: Yes artifacts in S3 can be encrypted with your KMS key. We also sign artifacts.
[15:15] [15:16] Speaker: small side convo... (inaudible)
[15:25] Diego: A quick note on quantization again — depending on your HW, some kernels are slower for 4-bit until we use optimized fused kernels. We maintain a catalog of recommended quantization profiles per GPU family.
[15:37] Eli: Can Redwood run a small benchmark on our infra to validate profiles?
[15:40] Maya: Yes that's part of the pilot — Diego will run a perf sweep with your sample model and dataset.
[15:46] Diego: Also recommend running with our trace instrumentation so we can attribute latency to compute vs network vs cache.
[15:53] Sara: Ok let's recap next steps.
[15:55] Maya: Recap: send architecture checklist, Terraform/CloudFormation snippets, perf spreadsheet, SOC2 + compliance pack, and schedule pilot. Any other asks?
[16:05] Anita: Add sample IAM policies and KMS trust policy.
[16:08] Maya: Noted. We'll include those.
[16:11] Eli: We'll assemble a small pilot team and share the ECR/S3 details.
[16:15] Sara: Thanks everyone. That was super helpful.
[16:19] Maya: Great — we'll follow up over email and update the CRM.

(Several minutes of wrap-up, scheduling a follow-up call)

[70:45] End of meeting.

Notes on transcription quirks: occasional mis-hearings ("K-B cache" for KV cache), speaker labels sometimes generic, punctuation uneven.

References & artifacts mentioned (links):
- Pilot perf spreadsheet (link shared in chat)
- Private VPC runbook and Terraform snippets (to be attached)
- SOC2 & compliance pack (link to Redwood secure drive)
- Route53 resolver example (CF template snippet)

title: Multi-NIC VPC Edge + Quantization: streaming fallbacks & p99 surface analysis - Catalyst Networks
preview: Deep technical session investigating intermittent p99 spikes observed when Catalyst routes streaming requests through the multi-NIC VPC edge. We covered how quantization changes execution paths, how KV cache hot-shards and network IRQ/coalescing cause tail amplification, and tradeoffs between streaming vs non-streaming fallbacks. Agreed to run targeted microbenchmarks single-vs-dual NIC and share benchmark spreadsheet; customer to provide traffic profile.

title: SummitBridge Onboarding Kickoff + KMS / SSO Review

Agreed to run a 2-week pilot on hosted Redwood with canary routing to Dedicated during peak loads. Next: send security pack, draft SOW, schedule runbook workshop and pilot benchmark window.
